responseSet.title: Do Cultural Differences Affect Training or Have All Analysts, Across Cultures, Been Trained Equally Since 1920?
responseSet.authorMast: Rachel G. Seidel and  Susana Muszkat
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Do Cultural Differences Affect Training or Have All Analysts, Across Cultures, Been Trained Equally Since 1920?11

Rachel Seidel and Susana Muszkat

Susana Muszkat

One Camden Place, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA—rseidelmd@comcast.net

Av Sao Gualter, 453 Alto de Pinheiros, São Paulo, Brazil—smuszkat@terra.com.br

Susana Muszkat  

The aim of this panel was to present preliminary results drawn from 252 email surveys and 94 interviews with candidates in psychoanalytic training in the three regions of the IPA, part of a continuing research project, ‘International Perspective on Psychoanalytic Training and Local Institutional Culture.’2 This project is among the first studies done by candidates concerning international psychoanalytic training experiences.

The Principal Investigator of the research is Andrea R. Q. de Pereira, and the Co-investigators who participated in this presentation are María Rita Ragau, and Laura Borensztein (Pereira et al., 2004). The object of the study was to contribute to psychoanalytic education by providing an international database from which to explore and compare candidates’ opinions about educational policies and experiences in seminars, supervision, and training analysis. An area of particular interest for the investigators was the impact of social reality—expressed in the forms of local culture and training institution culture—on the mindset of candidates.

Susana Muszkat, President of IPSO, introduced the panel. Placing the research in an historical context, María Rita Ragau noted that Max Eitingon's tripartite model of psychoanalytic education became compulsory for candidates in 1925. As King (1994) records, by 1927, Eitingon, then President of the IPA, reported that, in the large societies of Berlin, London and Vienna, training was actually being done along similar lines. However, there were certain differences of emphasis in psychoanalytic theory, and these differences evolved into the famous Anna Freud/Melanie Klein Controversial Discussions that stimulated intense affect and heated debate in the British Psycho-Analytical Society and in the IPA at that time.

Recently, communications from both the President-Elect and the Secretary of the IPA indicate that controversy and passion about issues of analytic education continue to exist.

responseSet.title: Authority as Paradox: The Transformations of Don Quijote
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Authority as Paradox

The Transformations of Don Quijote

Beatriz Priel

Beatriz Priel

Beer-Sheva IL-84105, Israel—bpriel@bgu.ac.il
(Final version accepted 23 February 2006)

Beatriz Priel  

Don Quijote, Don Quixote, analytic authority, aesthetic experience, analytic third, transitional phenomena

The author's contention is that the analysand's temporary attribution of authority to the analyst is inherent in the analytic situation; this is seen as a transitional and paradoxical form of authority pertaining neither to internal nor external reality, but dwelling in the analytic third. The author proposes a conceptualization of psychoanalytic authority as a form of aesthetic authority according to Gadamer's definitions. While the scientific and hermeneutic codes for the understanding of authority in psychoanalysis assume that the main issue at stake is the delimitation of the objectivity or the subjectivity of the analyst's knowledge, this aesthetic perspective centres on the analysand's attribution of a claim of truth to analytic interpretations, and on the experience of recognition. The experience of recognition of a possible truth is particular and context-bound, as well as self-transformational. A reading of three episodes from Cervantes's The history of Don Quixote de la Mancha illuminates the transitional and paradoxical character of aesthetic authority within a transformational dialogue. These episodes are read as dramatizations of different positions vis-à-vis the paradoxical authority that characterizes transformational dialogues.

[[RunningHead]]

A compulsion for antiquity: Freud and the ancient world by Richard H. Armstrong Ithaca, NY: Cornell UP. 2005305 + xii p. (Cornell Studies in the History of Psychiatry series, Makari GJ, Gilman SL, editors.)

Richard Gottlieb

Richard Gottlieb

263 W End Ave, Apt 1E, New York, NY 10023, USA—rmgottlieb@aol.com

Richard Gottlieb  

If you have wondered about the ancient sources of the phrase ‘royal road’ (via regia)1 since your first year of psychoanalytic training—but been afraid to ask—then Richard H. Armstrong's impressive treatment of the many uses Freud makes of antiquity will slake a long-endured thirst. (For impatient readers, the answer is given below.2) Those psychoanalysts interested in the Western classical tradition will be entranced by this book, a detailed and closely argued examination of Freud's relationship with the ancient world and of the ways he deployed his knowledge of antiquity in developing psychoanalysis.

1‘The interpretation of dreams is the royal road to knowledge of the unconscious activities of the mind.’ (Added by Freud to editions of The interpretation of dreams after 1909.)2Via regia is the Latin for the king's road that was the commercial route between Damascus and Aqaba in biblical times. The story (Numbers, chap. 20, v. 14-21) describes the road taken, unsuccessfully, through hostile territory by the Israelites; they were ‘knocked off the road,’ according to Armstrong. In the later works of Philo the royal road became the way that leads to the knowledge of God, the way of the true children of Israel. Later yet, the Greek church fathers adapted it to mean the way not just of truth but of orthodoxy, the way of faith, the ‘one royal road’ that avoided heresy. Ultimately, the Church itself became the royal road (pp. 249-50).1740

Überleben und Versuche der WiederbelebungPsychoanalytische Studien mit Überlebenden der Shoah und mit ihren Familien in Israel und in der Diaspora [Survival and efforts at revival—Psychodynamic studies of Holocaust survivors and their families in Israel and the Diaspora] by Hillel Klein Stuttgart: Frommann-Holzboog (Biermann C, Nedelmann C, editors. Jahrbuch der Psychoanalyse, Supplement 20). 2003319 p.

Wolfgang Hegener

Wolfgang Hegener

Nassauische Strasse 30, D-10717 Berlin, Germany—W.Hegener@TU-Berlin.de

Wolfgang Hegener  

This book is unusual in many respects. Author Hillel Klein, the well-known Israeli psychoanalyst and psychiatrist, wrote it in the last few years of his life as the sum of his experiences over several decades in treating Holocaust survivors and their families. The book is also a moving personal record. As a Polish Jew who was the only member of his family to survive the exterminatory anti-Semitic hatred of the National Socialists, Klein describes his own experiences and the process of working them out in an impressive and deeply moving way.

The book opens with the account of a key incident from 1942: a young SS man was guarding a group of tired prisoners and asked the young Hillel, who was only just 19 years old, ‘Tell me the truth. Who will win the war?’ When this man evasively said, ‘There are no victors in war—only victims’, the soldier was furious and replied, ‘If you don't tell the truth, Jewish dog, I'll kill you on the spot!’ Hillel finally answered, ‘Germany will lose the war. You can't kill everyone’. After a brief pensive silence, the SS man left him. Hillel was then insulted and beaten by the other members of the group for his independence of mind (p. 11). This tremendously courageous reaction is in many ways characteristic of Klein's attempt at what he calls ‘revival’. In a situation constructed entirely for the purpose of his complete physical and psychic destruction, he asserted his right to answer as an individual; he thereby distanced himself from the group and removed the ‘false self’ that had been projected on to him by the Nazi aggressors.

An experience immediately following the Liberation in May 1945 illustrates a further remarkable attempt to banish terror and death from his self-image and to return to his self-representation that belonged to the pre-Shoah period. At this time, Klein was in the Theresienstadt concentration camp and was so emaciated that he weighed only 28 kg. He shouted over to his fellow prisoners who were attacking some German soldiers, ‘Don't do the same to them as they did to us yesterday! Why are you doing that?’ Shortly afterwards, during a walk in the surrounding area he arrived at a stately home and proceeded not to the kitchen but to the library, where he found a slim volume of Heinrich Heine's Book of Songs. On reading the lines ‘In the beauteous month of May …’, he started to cry and thus began an intensive mourning process (p. 285).

[[RunningHead]]

Und Freud hat doch Recht. Die Entstehung der Kultur durch Transformation der Gewalt.Bausteine einer allgemeinen Kulturtheorie [So Freud was right. The emergence of culture through the transformation of violence. Outline of a general theory of culture] by Eberhard T. Haas Gießen: Psychosozial. 2002. 407 p.

Martin Teising

Martin Teising

Ludwig-Braun-Str. 13, D-36251 Bad Hersfeld, Germany—Teising@t-online.de

Martin Teising  

This book of cultural criticism contains essays by the author from the 1990s to 2005, most of which have been revised for this publication. The most important statement is contained in the book's subtitle: the emergence of culture through the transformation of violence. Haas hereby gives us a multifaceted and powerful reminder of the bad news that Freud entered in the genealogical register of humanity. Taking up the tradition of social psychology in psychoanalysis, Haas points to internal laws on which the cultural dimension of the human being is founded and which determine the human condition from ‘which we are in danger of becoming further and further removed with regard to projects relating to biotechnological reform and promises of perfection’ (p. 9).

Haas has arranged his texts, arising from interdisciplinary dialogues over many years, in five sections, which after the introduction are entitled ‘The Oedipus complex’, ‘Mourning’, ‘Religion'and‘Art’. He links Bionian formulations with Freud's writings of cultural criticism and draws some support from non-psychoanalytic authors. Girard's cultural theory and the ‘social drama’ of Turner, the ethnologist, as well as the works of the classical scholar, Walter Burkert, provide some external validation of Freud's work and the contemporary relevance and significance of Freud's writings on cultural theory become clear to the reader.

The Oedipus complex is also understood as the core complex of Freud's cultural theory. Oedipus, whose existence is cursed with disaster by the oracle, is abandoned and thrown out by his parents, who wanted to avoid their fate.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.291

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The unsung psychoanalyst: The quiet influence of Ruth Easser by Mary Kay O'Neil Toronto: University of Toronto Press. 2004. 250 p.

Deborah Cabaniss

Deborah Cabaniss

903 Park Avenue, New York, NY 10021, USA—dsc3@columbia.edu

Deborah Cabaniss  

It is by now a cliché to write that psychoanalysis is the lonely profession of men and women who toil alone in their offices, their work privy only to their patients. Yet, with the burdens of confidentiality and the paucity of empirical data about practice, one can generally only guess what goes on within the psychoanalytic consulting room. ‘What exactly does he say to his patients?’ we wonder. ‘Does she really do what she says she does in her papers?’ ‘What would it be like to be supervised by him?’ In this fascinating book, Mary Kay O'Neil uses the words of one analyst's colleagues, supervisees, students, and (importantly!) analysands to remove the fourth wall of one psychoanalytic office to afford a rare glimpse inside.

Following its title, a conceit of this book is that it tells the story of not only one psychoanalyst but of the multitude of psychoanalysts who train and practice in relative obscurity. To be fair, though, O'Neil's subject was no ordinary psychoanalyst. Ruth Easser was a Canadian who trained and matured at the Columbia Psychoanalytic and Psychosomatic Clinic for Training and Research (hereafter Columbia) just after it was founded in 1944. Easser was there practically from the beginning, matriculating as a candidate in 1946, graduating in 1949 and appointed as a training analyst in 1954 at the age of 32. She was an influential teacher and sought-after supervisor at Columbia until she moved back to her native Toronto in 1970.

604

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Association For Child Psychoanalysis Panel on Children11

Elizabeth Tuters and Ana De Schvartzman

Ana De Schvartzman

72 Woodlawn Ave W, Toronto, ON - M4V 1G7, Canada—etuters@sympatico.ca

Cabello 3565, 10 “B”, C1425APK Buenos Aires, Argentina—arozen@ciudad.com.ar

Ana De Schvartzman  

Two analysts who work with children from different psychoanalytic traditions each presented a clinical case vignette, followed by a discussant with a different theoretical/cultural perspective. Our aim was to show the way we work with children who use play/words to both communicate and control their trauma and ensuing developmental struggles with a focus on the interface of transference/ countertransference. Participant discussion was encouraged.

Two 5/6 year-old boys were presented with a brief background and details from two sessions.

The first case, seen four times weekly, was presented by Stephanie Smith in a playful way, giving the audience an appreciation of the way the psychoanalyst worked with the child through the child's construction of play themes, to give the child an opportunity to show and tell the analyst how his mind works. Stephanie Smith allowed the child to present his material in whatever way was available to him, through play themes and toys, including a tape recorder to tell his story.

Esperanza Perez de Pla, the discussant, focused on what important data had been omitted, namely the subjective states of the psychoanalyst, and her counter-transference reactions. She thought the details about the child's first year of life and an understanding of the complexity of the parents’ feeling states with respect to their child were missing. The discussant suggested the ambivalence was intense for both parents and for the psychoanalyst, and wondered if the analyst was involved in a sadistic enactment of the complexity of the child's relationship identification with his mother. She thought the psychoanalyst and the child were involved in a repetition compulsion.

The general discussion that followed pointed out the importance of the nonverbal and playfulness which was the transforming aspect of this work. The child entered the session in anger and chaos, and left calm and with a mind able to think about returning to his analyst the next day. He had a link, therefore he could think. This case was felt to be emotional, as no one had ever listened to this boy the way his psychoanalyst had; he now had a place to bring all his chaotic feelings.

responseSet.title: Association for Child Psychoanalysis Panel on Adolescents
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Association for Child Psychoanalysis Panel on Adolescents11

Elizabeth Tuters

Elizabeth Tuters

72 Woodlawn Ave W, Toronto, ON - M4V 1G7, Canada—etuters@sympatico.ca

Elizabeth Tuters  

Two analysts of adolescents from different psychoanalytic traditions each presented a clinical case vignette, followed by a discussion from a different theoretical/cultural perspective. Our aim was to show the way we work with adolescents who use words/actions to both confuse and enlighten, with a focus on the challenges to the transference/countertransference reactions. Participant discussion was encouraged.

We had the pleasure of hearing two analysts present material from work with two girls aged over 15 years. Although they are the same age, the girls present differently to the analytic work.

Each case was responded to by a discussant who presented some other ways of thinking about the material and understanding the analytic work.

The first case, 15 year-old Anna, was presented by Rachel Seidel. Anna was seen for two months in twice-weekly psychoanalytic psychotherapy before beginning four-times-weekly psychoanalysis. Anna was referred by her parents for problems with anxiety, depression, and an eating disorder, symptoms which overlaid her deeper problems in forming age-appropriate relationships and detaching from her primary objects. In the first year, Anna struggled with resistance towards her growing dependence on her psychoanalyst. After this resistance was worked through, Anna worked in the transference on problems in her early relationship to her parents and, in the final year, on the importance of her relationship with her analyst, and the pain and sadness of losing her. After three years, Anna was able to move away from home and from her analyst to go off to college with a sense that she would always have her psychoanalyst inside her to call upon and to think about. They had had a meaningful impact upon each other.

The discussant, Izelinde Garcia de Barros, suggested there was a convergence present in the latent material implicit in both Anna's and her parents’ reports. Both had difficulty elaborating the symbiotic aspects and infantile bonds with their primary objects. Both had affective bonds with each other, albeit fragile. This struggle was enacted in the analytic relationship as the analyst found herself responding to her patient's stubbornness with her own stubbornness. The analyst was able to identify this process in an authentic way with Anna. The discussant postulated she might see this mixing up of stubbornness more in terms of an intrusive projective identification with the function of projecting anxiety into the object.

[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Minutes of the Business Meeting of the International Psychoanalytical Association held in the Rio de Janeiro Rooms at the Sofitel Hotel, Rio de Janeiro, 29 July 2005, 17:30-20:00

1. Opening Remarks by the President of the IPA

Prof. Widlöcher welcomed the Members to the last Business Meeting of his administration. He said that he was delighted to see the past Latin American President, Dr Horacio Etchegoyen, present at the meeting. The meeting applauded Dr Etchegoyen.

As it had not been possible to do so earlier in the week, Prof. Widlöcher announced the winners of the Psychoanalytic Training Today Award. Although the winner of the first prize was not present, the second-prize winner came up to accept the award.

Prof. Widlöcher reminded the meeting that reports contained in the Business Meeting Booklet would be taken as read and not therefore read out during the meeting.

He introduced Dr Ralph W. Layland, a member of the IPA's Constitution, Bylaws and Procedures Committee, who would act as Parliamentarian throughout the meeting.

2. Minutes of the Business Meeting of the IPA, 10 March 2004

The President invited comment on these minutes. There were no comments and the minutes were approved with two abstentions.

3. Names of Members of the IPA Who Have Died Since the Last Congress

Prof. Widlöcher asked the meeting to stand and observe one minute's silence in honour of deceased colleagues.

864

responseSet.title: Book Notices
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

Brosig B, Gieler U (editors) (2004). Die Haut als psychische Hülle [The skin as a psychic envelope]. Gießen: Psychosozial Verlag.

From the perspective of various psychoanalytic schools, the essays brought together in this volume address the importance of the early experience of developing relations between the psyche and the body and disturbances in these relations. The influence of skin infections on the development of early childhood relationships and internal objects, as well as the function of skin symptoms and injuries to the skin for defensive purposes, are also discussed. The theoretical reference point is Anzieu's concept of the function of the skin as a psychic envelope, as developed by the ‘skin ego’.

Brummer M, Enckell H (editors) (2005). Lasten ja nuorten psykoterapia [Psychotherapy of children and adolescents]. Helsinki: WSOY. 263 p.

This textbook consists of basic papers on the psychotherapy of children and adolescents by experienced Finnish psychoanalysts. They describe problems in childhood and adolescence, and their treatment in a way that can benefit both professional people and parents.

Caldwell L (editor) (2005). Sex and sexuality.Winnicottian perspectives. London: Karnac. 179 p.

The sixth volume in this Winnicott Studies Monograph Series, and the third one to be edited by Lesley Caldwell, this multi-authored book includes chapters on Winnicottian views about childhood sexuality (Giannakoulas), violence and creativity (McDougall) and ‘sex as a defence against sexuality’ (Bertolini and Neri), as well as other essays by Green, Scarfone, Taylor Robinson, Schaedel and Phillips.

Dammasch F, Metzger H-G (editors) (2006). Die Bedeutung des Vaters.Psychoanalytische Perspektiven [The importance of the father. Psychoanalytic perspectives]. Frankfurt/M.: Brandes & Apsel.

From the starting point of a stronger emphasis on the importance of the father not only in the oedipal situation but also for early triangulation in contemporary psycho-analytic developmental psychology, the editors present an anthology that sets out a variety of psychoanalytic perspectives on the importance of the father. These range from the early father-child relationship to the importance of the internal father image for love relationships and to conflicts that can be reactivated in fathers in relation to the forthcoming birth of a child or their daughters’ development in adolescence.

responseSet.title: Attachment Theory and Psychoanalysis: Some Remarks from an Epistemological and from a Freudian Viewpoint1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attachment Theory and Psychoanalysis

Some Remarks from an Epistemological and from a Freudian Viewpoint1

Siegfried Zepf

Siegfried Zepf

Narzissenstr 5, D-66119 Saarbrücken, Germany—s.zepf@rz.uni-saarland.de
(Final version accepted 9 March 2006)

Siegfried Zepf  

attachment theory, psychoanalysis, epistemology

The author examines Bowlby's attachment theory and more recent versions of it from an epistemological viewpoint and subjects it to questioning on whether they are in line with central concepts of Freudian psychoanalysis. He argues that Bowlby's basic tenets regarding attachment theory, which later attachment theorists never seriously questioned, do not conform to scientific standards, and that psychoanalytic issues such as the dynamic unconscious, internal conflicts, interaction of drive wishes and the role of defence in establishing substitutive formations are either ignored or not treated in sufficient depth. In the light of this, Fonagy's assertion that psychoanalytic criticism of attachment theory arose from mutual misunderstandings and ought nowadays to be seen as outdated is reversed: psychoanalytic criticism can only be regarded as outdated if either basic tenets of Freudian psychoanalysis, or attachment theory or both are misunderstood.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Validation of Psychoanalytic Theories: Toward a Conceptualization of References

Marita Torsti-Hagman

Marita Torsti-Hagman

Ruukuntekijäntie 6 A 155 Vantaa 01600, Finland—marita.torsti@pp.inet.fi
10 November 2005

Marita Torsti-Hagman  

Dear Sirs,

Zachrisson and Zachrisson (2005) prompted the following comments.

Psychoanalytic conceptualization includes both the drive and the unconscious on the one hand and a hermeneutic orientation on the other. These two different conceptual worlds cannot simultaneously form causality or linear continuity.

Free association is the method of psychoanalysis, which analysts must foster in themselves and mould for use in their work. Personal analyses are essential for enabling free association and its continued functioning can only be ensured through constant self-analysis. This development of analytic thinking shapes both work by the couch and theoretical thinking. The analyst's mind is the instrument of analysis. It determines the content and timing of the whole of analytic activity, the choice of words and the tones of voice. I did not see any mention of this in Zachrisson and Zachrisson. Analysts have their own basic concepts, whose abandonment seems to be encouraged by these modern so-called scientific attempts to flee from the analyst's demanding self-discipline. There is no reason to assume that experts from other fields would be able to understand our thinking.

Psychoanalysis is an unusual skill and science in that it exists at the interface with the unconscious. Its fundamental concepts are: the unconscious, free association, the drives, the analyst's own free-associating mind, self-analysis and a very important quality: contact with the unconscious landscape of infantile sexu-ality in between the preconscious and unconscious spheres. This atlas of sexuality seems to have utterly vanished from analytic forms of know-how. In addition, central elements in analytic skills are dreams and their interpretative use in the analytic process. And the significance of transference and resistance must not by any means be forgotten. Here there is a whole group of co-ordinates not found in other sciences. True correspondence with other sciences does not exist. The simultaneity of all of the above-mentioned factors brings to this science a fractal quality, if it were to be described in mathematical form.

Beyond the Divan: Psychoanalysis in Review11

Marci Passos , Marina Tavares , Viviane Frankenthal and Marci Passos

Marci Passos

Rua Almirante Guilhobel 111/101, Lagoa, 22471-150 Rio de Janeiro RJ, Brazil—marcidoria@alternex.com.br

Rua Rubinéia, 326 - Itanhangá, 22753-090, Rio de Janeiro RJ, Brazil—marinatavares@terra.com.br

Rua Bolivar 54/1001, Copacabana, 22061-020 Rio de Janeiro RJ, Brazil—vivifrank@uol.com.br

Marci Passos  

The Brazilian Society of Psychoanalysis of Rio de Janeiro (SBPRJ) offered this opportunity to encourage exchange between Brazilian and international psychoanalytical journals and to foster dialogue between psychoanalysis and other related disciplines.

Brief Summary of Papers

Dr. Lorena Preta presented a paper entitled ‘Fertile contaminations—A model for psychoanalytical work’. She described the history of psychoanalysis as a form of knowledge that connects and interrelates various fields and experiences. She pointed out that those interactions have been used in psychoanalysis in a special way, that is, the actual objects of her research were at the same time tools of her theoretical and practical work, for example, the Oedipus myth, as both contents of the mind and a method of knowing it. Preta recalled Freud and Jung in the United States spreading the ‘pest’ of a psychism in permanent interaction: sleep, awake, conscious, unconscious, sexuality. Based on that constant exchange, she proposed the notion of ‘contaminations’ as a possibility of transformation either in therapy, or in psychoanalytical theorization, or in the relationship with the outside. But it is contamination with method: ‘We need to pay attention to our own models with a rigour not of content, which might risk becoming an oppressive orthodoxy, but of method, to achieve a dialogue of psychoanalysis with the other disciplines’. The psychoanalyst will have to face up to the repudiation of the ‘uncanny’! Preta had a conception of the review of psychoanalytical culture as a container inside which dynamics of dialogue and confrontation develop, where the ‘contaminations’ take place, and said, ‘The framework of the discourse proposed outside should be very clear, not as regards its contents or intent, but representation of the mental place that the review is going to occupy. … We cannot control or foresee the way in which these dynamics take place. Indeed we should, if necessary, presuppose a sort of

1Panel held at the 44th Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, 28 July 2005. Panellists: Lorena Preta (Rome), Alicia Killner (Buenos Aires) and Manoel Berlinck (São Paulo).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Response to Luyten, Blatt and Corveleyn

Robert Wallerstein

Robert Wallerstein

290 Beach Rd, Belvedere, CA 94920, USA—judywall@mindspring.com
25 January 2006

Robert Wallerstein  

Dear Sirs,

I am indeed gratified at the degree of response I have received to Wallerstein (2005a, 2005b) and Green (2005) over the enduring status of the theoretical pluralism that characterizes contemporary psychoanalysis worldwide. In their letter, Luyten et al. deal with the issue of the exclusionary (‘either/or’) or complementary (‘both/and’) nature of the distinction between: 1) nomothetic and idiographic stances in psychoanalytic research; and 2) what they call the related ‘neo-positivistic explanatory’ and the ‘interpretive intuitive’ approach to psychoanalytic understanding.

The essence of my position on these issues over my entire psychoanalytic lifetime, and, as I trust, was made quite clear in my dialogue with Green, is in total agreement with the viewpoint articulated by Luyten et al. Setting each of these issues up as representing antithetic and incompatible conceptual stances has been only to the detriment of our psychoanalytic scientific evolution. Our psychoanalytic progress as a science can only come in the manner so clearly stated by Luyten et al. and I thank them for highlighting this perspective in their letter.

580

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Response to Dr Tullio Carere-Comes

Elizabeth De Bianchedi

Elizabeth De Bianchedi

Uriarte 2112, Buenos Aires (1425), Argentina—bianchedi@ssdnet.com.ar
25 February 2005

Elizabeth De Bianchedi  

Dear Sirs,

In my recently published paper, you quote me mentioning an ‘evenly suspended attention’ but in it I actually speak of ‘an evenly suspended mind’, which I believe is a much stronger and more difficult attitude to achieve and maintain. In a previous paper of mine (de Bianchedi, 1991), I develop this idea more fully. By the way, I obviously confirm your quotations from Bion (1992), especially the emphasis placed by him and you (and me as well), on the opacities which obstruct intuition, which is so basic in our mental work when we use the psychoanalytic function of the personality.

As to phenomenology, I am not too familiar with the different meanings of the term, nor of Husserl's concepts, and therefore cannot speak about the similarity you mention between the two approaches. Thank your for showing me the importance of intuition in phenomenology, and the possibility of connecting both positions (obviously including psychoanalysis).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Le savoir-déporté: Camps, histoire, psychanalyse [Deported knowledge: The camps, history and psychoanalysis] by Anne-Lise Stern Paris: Seuil. 2004. 335 p.

Eva Weil

Eva Weil

37 rue Tournefort, 75005 Paris, France—evaweil@hotmail.com

Eva Weil  

Psychoanalysing and Testifying—A Double Bind?

The publication of this book might be hailed for its author's unusual position: although Anne-Lise Stern, a deportee and a psychoanalyst, has given many teachings in person over a long period, this book only appeared in 2004. However, Stern's text both goes beyond and stops short of this testimonial function by addressing it as a question in its own right. For some people, telling their experiences seems to have become an obligatory link in the chain of post-Shoah civilization and this can only be affirmed. As the author writes, ‘finding it impossible to talk about because of not being understood was something that I experienced only much later and, regrettably, mainly in the psychoanalytic community’ (p. 113).

One of the explanations that Stern suggests for this impossibility is that what we generally find intolerable, whether in the historical, philosophical or psychoanalytic field, are matters that go too close to the bone.

Anne-Lise Stern was deported from France, where her parents had fled for refuge, to Auschwitz in April 1944, having been denounced as a Jew. Until June 1945, when she returned to France, she was transferred between the camps at Bergen-Belsen, Buchenwald and Theresienstadt.

The dialogue with the author that forms the preface to this book provides some important biographical information about her relationship with the German language, or what she calls the ‘ex-mother-tongue’, as well as the analytic treatment that she undertook with Jacques Lacan, following several attempts with other analysts.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.610

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editors: On: Death in the Afternoon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Death in the Afternoon

Emad Bishara

Emad Bishara

10, rue du Conseil-Général, CH-1205 Genève, Switzerland—bishara@hin.ch
26 March 2006

Emad Bishara  

Dear Sirs,

I read Suzanne Chassey (2006) with great interest. The article showed limits we might all face when dealing with omnipotency and destructiveness. What struck me most in the patient's act was that it looked like an envious attack aiming to destroy the capacities of the analyst. In this article, the generosity of the author was patent in her desire to give Anne some gift by means of the card, and generosity was even more evident in the fact that this article was written to keep in mind Anne's beauty and the analyst's love for her. At the same time, the author has certainly given us—lecturers—a gift, as she wrote in such an intimate way about what we all feel as a traumatic professional event (Tillman, 2006). It was probably this generosity that the patient envied and attacked. By her act, she tried to destroy the analyst's capacity to give and heal; her envious attack also destroyed her own creativity; and her envious feelings towards her son led her to destroy his possibility of having a good mother. The author has also pointed out the patient's attempt to control. It is true that in all destructive processes as in war or terrorist attacks we always find either envy or desire of control or both!

responseSet.title: Hamlet. Ensayos psicoanalíticos [Hamlet: Psychoanalytic essays] Edited by Carlos Sopena Madrid: Síntesis. 2004. 192 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hamlet. Ensayos psicoanalíticos [Hamlet: Psychoanalytic essays] Edited by Carlos Sopena Madrid: Síntesis. 2004. 192 p.

Ramón Echevarría

Ramón Echevarría

Balmes 358, 5è2a, E-08006 Barcelona, Spain—13991rep@comb.es

Ramón Echevarría  

Some years ago, André Green coined the term hamletology to refer to the discipline which encapsulates the vast literature on the theme of Hamlet. Although there are many branches within the field of hamletology, one of the most important is that of the psychoanalytic literature, a branch that began with Freud himself. This book is the recent fruit of this branch, edited by a Uruguayan psychoanalyst resident in Madrid, and contains nine essays by psychoanalysts of diverse background and different theoretical orientations, although there is a clear predominance of the Lacanian perspective.

Judging by the content of the essays it would seem that the title of the book refers more to Hamlet himself than to the tragedy of the same name. Indeed, the figure of Hamlet is the focus for most of the book's chapters. This is not surprising if something Harold Bloom (2002) said is borne in mind, namely that the figure of Hamlet does not fit within the tragedy, it invades the work, transcends it. Here, then, considerations of the tragedy as a whole take a secondary role, while references to Shakespeare are few and anecdotal.

Several of the chapters in the book appear to follow the tradition, begun by Freud and firmly established through Jones (1948), of treating the figure of Hamlet as a real person. As Sopena says,

Despite his not being a real figure, when we refer to Hamlet we do so as if he were a person. He is a creature of fiction but a living one, one who is subjected to psychic forces which remain unknown both to him and to the author or reader or spectator. (p. 9)

As is known, Jones proposed analysing the unconscious of Hamlet, an imaginary figure, as if he were a living being. This, at least, was what he sought to do, and it is an approach with a solid basis, for it was a living being who created the hero, who gave him emotions and thoughts, and who dictated his behaviour. According to this approach, Hamlet is an almost-person, with his conscious life, his unconscious, his drives, his superego, his past prior to the start of the dramatic action, etc. Shakespeare created not only a role or a character, but a complete man. In fact, Hamlet is more alive than most of the figures who appear on the stage of our lives. In his essay, Jones sought to discover what type of man Hamlet was, such that he felt and acted towards certain situations in the way described to us by Shakespeare. It should be added that Jones completed his work by seeking to reveal the relationships between Hamlet and the personality of Shakespeare himself. Thus, for Jones, Hamlet enables us to get to know Shakespeare as much as Shakespeare allows us to get to know Hamlet. To an extent, Shakespeare is also the work of Hamlet.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

André J, Dejours C, Denis P, Perrot M, Schaeffer J, Schneider M, Vanier M (2005). Les sexes indifférents [Sexual non-difference]. Paris, PUF. (Petite bibliothèque de psychanalyse.) 153 p.

This multi-author work proposes a review of the current upheavals in modes of sexual life, familial structure and advances in science and medical technology, including the possibility of reproductive techniques based on cloning. Do these debates have relevance to psychoanalysis? Jacques André and the authors invited to his seminar give an affirmative answer but they also take pause for reflection. Whether in terms of non-difference or difference, the concept of femininity and masculinity or of sexual difference is something that evolves over the centuries. Is the proposition of sexual non-difference compatible with the psychoanalytic theory of sexuality? Does the question of bisexuality and psychosexual identity remain inextricable from libidinal pressure? And from the conflict resulting from sexual difference? With sexual non-differentiation, the role of primary and secondary homosexuality emerges as a form of civilising sublimation. What about otherness and the love for another?

Auerbach JS, Levy KN, Schaffer CE, editors (2006). Relatedness, self-definition and mental representation: Essays in honor of Sidney J. Blatt. London and New York: Routledge. 316 p.

This festschrift for pre-eminent researcher and psychoanalyst Sidney Blatt, professor of psychiatry and psychology at Yale, focuses on the many areas to which Blatt has made important contributions: personality development (communication modes, representations and spatialization), psychopathology, assessment of object relations and the Rorschach, different kinds of treatment research (process, outcome, singlecase), and applied psychoanalysis and character (greed, narcissism, attachment and faith). A partial list of contributors speaks to Blatt's significance and to the quality of this volume: Beebe, Jaffe and Lachmann, Freedman, Fleck, Holzman, Holt, Fonagy and Target, Luborsky, and others who themselves have been leaders in empirical psychoanalytic research.

[[RunningHead]]

Reviewers for 2006

The Editors, Elias Mallet da Rocha Barros, Glen O. Gabbard, Antonino Ferro and Paul Williams, would like to express their gratitude to the team of readers and reviewers throughout the world responsible for the successful implementation of the IJP peer review process. These colleagues devote a great deal of time and effort in the service of providing detailed and thoughtful evaluations of manuscripts, film essays and book reviews that will ultimately help the authors improve their contributions. Without their dedication, the IJP would not have achieved the scholarly status it enjoys, and we would like to thank them for everything they have done to assist us during the past 12 months. We also would like to thank members of the IJP Board of Guardians and members of the editorial boards of the IJP ‘Annuals’ projects who are working towards producing collections of IJP papers in various languages.

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editors: Reply to Dr. Golland
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Reply to Dr. Golland

Kenneth Eisold

Kenneth Eisold

353 Central Park W, New York, NY 10025, USA—KenEisold@aol.com
7 December 2005

Kenneth Eisold  

Dear Sirs,

I appreciate Jeff Golland's endorsement of my ‘“macro” level’ analysis of the difference between psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. It is a pleasure to be seen as offering a useful commentary. But I do feel that it is necessary to add that, as a result of my analysis, the terms ‘psychoanalysis’ and ‘psychotherapy’ no longer seem available to us as they were. For me, they represent historical and political entities, not clinical ones.

To be sure, I have no quarrel with the approach Golland advocates. There are many clinical differences that can be usefully distinguished, and I have no inherent quarrels with many of them. But the point for me is that these clinical differences are interesting and useful in their own ways. They do not warrant the use of the term ‘psychoanalysis’ as opposed to ‘psychotherapy.’ Perhaps one might well make the argument that one or the other is superior in certain circumstances or for particular ends. But I do think that we have to put the old polarities behind us. We can no longer take for granted the inherent superiority of any therapeutic technique and privilege it as ‘psychoanalysis.’ We are, each in his or her own way, ‘psychotherapists.’

responseSet.title: How to do Research? Reply to Otto Kernberg
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

How to do Research? Reply to Otto Kernberg11

Roger Perron

Roger Perron

6 rue Damesme, F-75013 Paris, France—RPerron1@aol.com

Roger Perron  

You Said ‘Research’?

How can one not agree with Otto Kernberg on the essential issue, namely, that it is necessary for us as psychoanalysts to develop research activities?

It seems to me equally necessary, however, to be clear about the objectives and the methods. What are we to understand by ‘research’ and ‘science’ (since it is readily added that this research must be ‘scientific’)? In other words, on what epistemological grounds can we define our aims and our methods without renouncing psychoanalysis itself? Otto Kernberg contrasts the more ‘philosophical and subjectivist’ attitude of Latin countries with the preference for empirical studies which are said to be characteristic of Anglo-Saxon and Nordic countries. He may be right, though I do not think that in saying this he means to oppose an ‘old Europe’, nostalgic for its past and given to meditation without practical usefulness, with more pragmatic, in short, more efficient countries. One cannot say that, or else one would have to accept that the passionate discussions that animated Einstein, Bohr, Heisenberg and a few others in the 1920s were only ‘philosophical’. They were scientific, because they were the very basis of the whole of quantum physics.

Otto Kernberg rightly emphasizes the need to distinguish between two types of research: on the one hand, research which aims to evaluate the efficacy of psychoanalytic treatments and of psychotherapeutic techniques inspired by psychoanalysis; and, on the other, research which concerns the psychoanalytic process, that is to say, the patient's psychic processes and their transformations in the conscious/unconscious metabolism, the patient-analyst interactions, etc., in the basic couch-armchair setting. In other domains, the latter would be designated as ‘fundamental research’ activities, while the former would be regarded more as ‘applied research’, or even ‘research-development’. The distinction is necessary. It needs to be remembered, though, that, within the very context of the exact sciences, researchers never fail to underscore the necessity of fundamental research which may initially appear to be totally ‘useless’, but without which applied and developmental research would be totally sterilized. The same is necessarily true in our domain.

responseSet.title: Commentary
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Robert Michels

Robert Michels

418 E 71st St, Suite 41, New York, NY 10021, USA—rmichels@med.cornell.edu

Robert Michels  

I was confused by Paul Israel's paper until I realized that the term ‘training analyst’ means something quite different to him than it does to me. This illustrates what is perhaps the most important lesson from our dialogue, that conversation across languages and across cultures can be difficult, misunderstandings frequent, and confusion likely. Israel uses the term to refer to analysts who are associated with institutes and who participate in selection, supervision, classroom teaching, seminars and evaluation, but specifically not to those who participate by conducting the personal analyses of psychoanalytic candidates. I would call this category ‘faculty analyst,’ while to me (and I believe to most of the psychoanalytic community) ‘training analyst’ refers to an analyst who analyzes candidates in training. Israel describes a training arrangement in which a ‘personal analysis’ is said to have no relationship to the institute, and therefore in his terms the analyst who conducts a ‘training analysis’ is not thereby a ‘training analyst.’ In most of the psychoanalytic world, the question of whether there should be a training analyst status translates to what should be the criteria for selecting those analysts who are to analyze candidates. I will return to this meaning of the question below.

Israel does not discuss this but instead focuses on the selection and education of analysts who carry out every other faculty function in an institute. He calls these faculty ‘training analysts,’ but without suggesting that they are appropriate analysts for candidates. Here I thoroughly agree with him. A faculty member may be an excellent evaluator for selection or graduation, an excellent classroom teacher or seminar leader, or even an excellent supervisor without necessarily being an appropriate analyst of candidates. These are distinct functions, and faculty should be considered and evaluated for participating in each of them separately. Many institutes have long assumed that selecting supervisors and selecting training analysts are the same task, and candidates have suffered from poor supervision conducted by good analysts as a result. Competence as a clinical analyst, for me the essential criterion for training analyst status, does not automatically translate to competence as a supervisor or evaluator, and may even be unnecessary for competence as a classroom teacher or seminar leader. It makes sense to evaluate faculty for the tasks they will be asked to perform, and not to assume that competence for one role in psychoanalytic education necessarily implies competence for other roles.

responseSet.authorMast: Stephen M. Sonnenberg and  William Myerson
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Prevention and Treatment of Organizationally Induced Trauma11

Stephen Sonnenberg and William Myerson

William Myerson

1600 W 38th St, Suite 403, Austin, TX 78731, USA—ssonnenberg@austin.rr.com

5300 San Jacinto, Ste 130, Houston, TX 77004-6841, USA—wmyerson@swbell.net

William Myerson  

William Myerson noted the potential for psychoanalytic training to induce trauma, and that long-term, intensive group consultation can help ameliorate those effects. At the Houston-Galveston Psychoanalytic Institute (HGPI) there exists an 8 year history of consultation, and when there is a lapse what has been learned can be forgotten. Myerson suggested that troubling organizational structures in institutes are bound to the training analyst system, which creates competition that cannot be addressed. He also noted that many analysts report being deeply hurt during their training.

Myerson noted his institute is growing. It was founded by white male psychiatrists in 1975, and in the last 20 years women have been admitted to the faculty and risen to positions of prominence, and now non-psychiatrists are becoming training analysts. Many aspects of this history have been hurtful and suppressed the expression of painful feelings.

As the institute grew, there arose a need for more people to do the work. A younger president tried to democratize the organization, but became incapacitated. The next president was a woman, and her tenure was difficult. She tried to work with the older faculty, while keeping alive the atmosphere of change. In the end, she could please almost no one, leading to the initial outside consultation.

Toward the end of her tenure, one senior faculty member became the subject of an ethics investigation. Also, the old education committee, composed entirely of training analysts, was replaced by a democratized board of directors, which included a candidate representative and non-training analysts. That new board faced this very disturbing ethics investigation at the same time as a new set of rules came into being which governed its function.

The new president, early in his term, wanted to reinstitute the outside consultation, but it took almost until the end of his term for it to take place. This occurred in part because of pressure from candidates. When the consultation occurred, it was led by a consultant (one of the panelists) who had previously worked with the institute. He encouraged faculty and candidates to express their feelings.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Play Misty for me (1971): The Perversion of Love

A. Carlos Silva Filho

A. Carlos Silva Filho

Rua Dr. Mario Ferraz 220, São Paulo, Brazil—luca.pacheco@terra.com.br

A. Carlos Silva Filho  

Dear Sirs,

Eileen McGinley and Andrea Sabbadini (2006) give a beautiful and precise analysis of a rich film, with a clear demonstration of basic psychological impulses and defenses in human personality. I would like to add some ideas coming from another vertex (in the Bionian sense of the term).

The main character, Evelyn, is shown to be a woman with a poor sense of identity. She has a weak and fragile ego related to a harsh and severe superego, a dynamic found in every psychotic state. Probably her mother also had a weak ego, without what might be called a capacity for holding (Winnicott), reverie (Bion) or empathy (Kohut). It is likely that her father was a severe and strong patriarchal figure, both admired and feared by the girl. He would be for her either an idealized or a persecutory object due to her shift towards the schizoparanoid splitting mechanism (Klein) in organizing experience (Ogden), from which came her regressive behavior when the ‘idealized object’ was projected on to the male radio presenter, who, in her preferred program, plays her favorite music, thus giving rise to her perverse and obsessive adoration.

As always happens in these dynamics, when the object resists attempts at control by the subject, adoration is transformed into hatred, with murderous feelings in this case towards the object's girlfriend. In this pathological situation, the outcome is potentially dramatic, and the plot unfolds with Evelyn discharging her strong destructive feelings against everybody who has relations with the man on whom she has become so dependent as to be willing to have a symbiotic fusion (Mahler) with him. The termination is her own death by falling from a cliff in her fight with Garver, the formerly beloved, now hated, radio presenter.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Response to Dr Torsti-Hagman

Anders Zachrisson and Henrik Zachrisson

Henrik Zachrisson

Prof. Dahls gt 18, N-0353 Oslo, Norway—johnaz@online.no

Norwegian Institute of Public Health, PO Box 4404, Nydalen, N-0403 Oslo, Norway—henrik.daae.zachrisson@fhi.no
28 November 2005

Henrik Zachrisson  

Dear Sirs,

We appreciate Dr Torsti-Hagman's comment on our article. It gives us the opportunity to clarify some points arising from our view on psychoanalytic theory and clinical method, which were not focused on in our paper. We agree with Dr Torsti-Hagman's description of what is central in psychoanalysis. We would perhaps have added some concepts, which are missing in her list, e.g. intrapsychic conflict and self-object relations, but that is a minor point. Psychoanalysis has several models and they focus on different aspects of the inner world. We also share her view of psychoanalytic method (which was not addressed by us) and certainly agree with her point about the analyst's ‘demanding self-discipline’. We take her initial point of the two different conceptual worlds that meet in psychoanalysis, but we disagree with her categorical conclusion about causality.

Our concern is not psychoanalytic theory and method per se, but the relationship between psychoanalysis as a knowledge system and the surrounding scientific and intellectual world. On that interface lies a potential for mutual enrichment and influence. Actually, Dr Torsti-Hagman gives a rather precise articulation of what worries us when she writes that ‘there is no reason to assume that experts from other fields would be able to understand psychoanalysis’ and that ‘true correspondence with other sciences does not exist’. It is an esoteric stance like this that we find problematic and try to address with our model.

We are wondering about her lack of interest in the questions raised by the Green-Stern controversy (Sandler et al., 2000).

266

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psyche, self and soul: Rethinking psychoanalysis, the self and spirituality By Gerald J. Gargiulo London: Whurr. 2004. 149 p.

Polly Young-Eisendrath

Polly Young-Eisendrath

195 Calais Road, Worcester, VT 05682, USA—pollye@adelphia.net

Polly Young-Eisendrath  

In 1916, Jung wrote a memorable essay on working within a spiritual dimension in our psychoanalytic endeavor. ‘The transcendent function’ lay unpublished in his files until 1953 and was revised in 1958. Jung writes in the English translation,

The shuttling to and fro of arguments and affects represents the transcendent function of opposites. The confrontation of the two positions generates a tension charged with energy and creates a living, third thing … a movement out of the suspension between opposites, a living birth that leads to a new level of being, a new situation.

(1969, p. 90)

Similar to Winnicott's potential space and Ogden's dialogical space, Jung's transcendent function is the mental space in which we can see the interplay of conscious and unconscious forces. How one comes to terms, in practice, with the unconscious, claims Jung, is ‘the fundamental question … of all religions and all philosophies. For the unconscious is not this thing or that; it is the Unknown as it immediately affects us’ (p. 68).

Jung concluded that this Unknown has an intelligence far greater than our consciousness. In presenting his widely acclaimed Terry Lectures on Psychology and Religion at Yale University in 1937, Jung, speaking in English, said ‘My psychological experience has shown time and again that certain contents issue from a psyche more complete than consciousness. They often contain a superior analysis or insight or knowledge which consciousness has not been able to produce’ (1938, p. 49). Jung's interest in spiritual matters is not, by any means, the sole root of the engagement of psychoanalysis with spirituality and religion. I have begun my review with this particular historical background because the interests of Gerald Gargiulo, as I have encountered them in this thoughtful little book of essays, seem remarkably similar to Jung's. I am a Jungian psychoanalyst. Gargiulo is a member of the International Psychoanalytic Association and the American Psychoanalytic Association.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Paul Israel

Paul Israel

Paul Israel  

My response is brief, and slightly disheartened! Michels's commentary clearly shows that there is not only a verbal misunderstanding here but also a different conception of what constitutes psychoanalytic training. My concept of the training analyst is specifically designed to avoid the distinction between the ‘training analyst’ and the ‘faculty analyst’ to which Michels returns, in line with a tradition that is more American than Anglo-Saxon. Let us therefore move on from a term, since Michels does not seem to be able to abandon it, to the question of the ‘training analyst’ (in French, analyste didacticien), and who, according to my respondent, is specifically responsible for the analysis of candidates in training.

I thought I had demonstrated in my article that none of the research so far conducted on this subject in recent decades has truly managed to define in what the ‘specific tasks and opportunities’ consist for analyses undertaken—in terms of the manifest wish—with a view to training, and in what respects these tasks fundamentally differ from those of every analyst, in every analysis! There is not space here to take up a discussion that can also be found in numerous writings devoted to the subject.

To make one final point, however: every analyst would probably agree with the statement that the wish to become an analyst should involve developing the personal analytic experience. Now, the act of embarking on the admission procedure in a training institute usually coincides with a termination of the analysis in a ‘soft’ consensus between analyst and analysand. This applies whether the analyst is officially involved in ‘training’ or is an ‘ordinary’ analyst. It is known that every sound analyst will seek to remedy this inadequacy through one or several phases.

Michels does, I think, betray some mischievousness when he purports to believe that the research to which I am referring is that advocated by an entire school of thought, objective research, which despite having limited uses is radically different from the clinical research indicated in my article. My observations are certainly in line with the trend he describes that favours an institution's adoption of methods to ensure that the selection and follow-up of candidates present the maximum of opportunities to establish their identity as analysts in a process that develops and deepens their insight into the analytic process itself, in themselves as in their patients. I therefore agree with my respondent that it is difficult to internationalize the social and cultural characteristics in which psychoanalytic institutions are immersed. Without this having any force of evidence, I would reiterate that, with its selection system and its training ‘philosophy’, the Paris Psychoanalytical Society remains one of those that has succeeded in minimizing the loss of candidates.

responseSet.title: Research Anxiety: A Response to Roger Perron's Comments
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Research Anxiety: A Response to Roger Perron's Comments

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg  

Roger Perron begins his critique by expressing his agreement with me regarding the need to develop research on, and within, psychoanalysis. His division of research into ‘applied’ and ‘fundamental’ does not quite correspond to my concepts of empirical and conceptual research, but, in so far as he intends fundamental research to mean the study of the psychoanalytic process, and applied research to mean the measurement of efficacy or outcome, I believe there is a need to integrate them with the goal of relating process to outcome in order to ascertain what is specific to psychoanalytic treatment as distinct from other forms of psychotherapy. In any case, he concludes his discussion reiterating his agreement that research is vital for the evolution of psychoanalysis.

But, here his questions begin: he expresses his concern about the aims and methods of psychoanalytic research in so far as, in his view, they raise the risk of ‘renouncing psychoanalysis itself’. Regarding applied research, he agrees with the need to carry out studies designed to evaluate the effectiveness of treatment, but questions how to conduct them. His concern centers on potential errors and false conclusions that may derive from methodologies inspired by ‘evidence-based medicine’, quoting a particularly poorly designed and questionable study carried out by INSERM in France. I agree that bad research is every bit as bad as bad clinical practice!

Regarding applied research, Perron questions the use of randomized controlled studies, given the impossibility of studying ‘homogenous’ patient populations. He believes that it is an illusion that we can measure different degrees of any particular symptom, and that it is not possible to divide patients up randomly into different types of treatment cells. What is neglected, he states, is consideration of indications of specific types of treatment for different patients. Finally, he questions the notion that the sole criterion of improvement might be the reduction of symptoms. Perron concludes this part of his argument by, again, stating that he does not intend to condemn studies aimed at establishing the degree of efficacy of a therapeutic approach that takes into account its indications, and, in this regard, expresses his full agreement with me. But, once again, he warns about the need to avoid errors that render them useless and might do a disservice to psychoanalysis.

Letters to the Editors

Martin Kemp

Martin Kemp

16 Woolstone Rd, London, SE23 2SG, UK—mkemp@kemp73.freeserve.co.uk
16 January 2006

Martin Kemp  

A final clarification from Mr Kemp is published below.

It has become clear that my letter caused deep offence to many: hurt which was not intended. I am grateful to IJP for the opportunity to apologize and to briefly explain myself.

Over recent years we have seen psychoanalysts involved in cross-disciplinary work in a number of fields—the more strictly scientific areas like neuro-psychoanalysis, and in the social sciences, biography, history and the arts. There is literature on the relationship between psychoanalysis and religion, and psychoanalysis and Marxism. Publishers’ lists and conference timetables now regularly feature attempts to make available to a wider audience analytic insights that might illuminate the most pressing contemporary political phenomena, and indeed in the summer of 2004, IJP itself sponsored an internet discussion on ‘the relevance of psychoanalysis to an understanding of terrorism’. Psychoanalytic thinkers like Dr Vamik Volkan and Dr John Alderdice have been activists in the search for peaceful outcomes of entrenched conflicts, and their articles have graced the pages of many analytic journals.

When we say, therefore, that the IJP

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Corrigenda

Glen Gabbard and Paul Williams

Paul Williams

15 February 2006

Paul Williams   Joint Editor-in-Chief

The article in the last issue of this journal by Irwin Z. Hoffman, ‘The myths of free association and the potentials of the analytic relationship’ [87(1):43-61], contains two errors in the first paragraph of the printed version. Instead of beginning with ‘I am going to present’ it begins awkwardly with ‘I present’, and, in line 6, of a less serious nature, instead of ‘I will not be attempting here’ it reads ‘I do not attempt here’. The online version of the paper and all reprints are correct. The hard copy issue reflects changes made without the author's consent after he had approved the final page proofs. We apologize to Dr. Hoffman for violating the agreement to publish the paper as it stood with his approval and for making changes that he did not authorize.

‘(Final version accepted 7 June 2005)’ should be inserted on a line after the e-mail address on p. 43).

responseSet.title: El caso Dora, más allá del diván: Diario imaginario de Ida Bauer [The Dora case, beyond the couch: The imaginary diary of Ida Bauer] by Eloísa Castellano-Maury Madrid: Biblioteca Nueva. 2003 142 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

El caso Dora, más allá del diván: Diario imaginario de Ida Bauer [The Dora case, beyond the couch: The imaginary diary of Ida Bauer] by Eloísa Castellano-Maury Madrid: Biblioteca Nueva. 2003142 p.

Ricardo Puchades

Ricardo Puchades

Cirilo Amorós 12, 4° 9°, E-46004 Valencia, Spain—ricardopuchades@hotmail.com

Ricardo Puchades  

The diary of an 18 year-old, perhaps like that of any adolescent, is a kind of soliloquy or narcissistic retreat in which an attempt is made to converse with oneself in a space

1Translated by Alan J. Nance.1725

inhabited by one's internal objects. It is, then, a form of introversion that resembles a neurosis, but one which also bears a resemblance to the temporal unfolding of any creative process that seeks to reveal itself before a potential third party: the reader. Eloísa Castellano-Maury, psychoanalyst and writer, writer and psychoanalyst, offers the reader a tale which adds an aesthetic layer to a general psychological truth about the hysterical personality, as well as to another possible and particular truth regarding the Dora case. Her work thus forms part of the artistic and literary tradition that, since the time of Freud himself, has so often been recognized for its efficacy in capturing and expressing ‘deep’ truths.

This book comprises 25 chapters, whose brevity is consistent with the kind of ‘notes’ one normally associates with a diary, along with an epilogue and an addendum. Throughout the supposed diary, it is Ida Bauer's voice we hear as she shares her intimate thoughts from between analytic sessions with Sigmund Freud, and thus we are introduced to ‘father’, ‘mother’, ‘Otto’ (her brother), Kobo and Kristel (Herr and Frau K), and Mademoiselle and Gerda (governesses) as they are woven into a plot marked by desire, frustration, resentment, seduction, falsehood and ‘double standards’, all of which fits perfectly with Freud's opinion of ‘Civilized’ sexual morality and modern nervous illness (1908); other figures also appear, such as Gustav Klimt (the painter), Sosonga (the daughter of the forest guard), Uncle Lazlo, her cousin Gisela, Fritz (who offers himself as an ideal suitor for Ida), and Freud, as well as places that belong both to memory and the present day: Vienna, Berggasse 19 and the surrounding area. The epilogue and the addendum serve as a counterpoint to what has gone before: the former as a dramatic coda based on one of the universal profiles of hysteria, the latter as a brief reflection somewhere between history and interpretation that confines itself to the analysis and the period immediately after it.

responseSet.title: Identity, Destiny and Terrorism: The Effect of Social Terror on Identity Formation1
responseSet.authorMast: Shahrzad F. Siassi and  Salman Akhtar
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Identity, Destiny and Terrorism

The Effect of Social Terror on Identity Formation1

Shahrzad Siassi and Salman Akhtar

Salman Akhtar

4419 Van Nuys Blvd Ste 201, 91403-5707 Sherman Oaks, CA, USA—s.siassi@verizon.net

71 Llanfair Cir, Ardmore, PA 19003-3342, USA—salman.akhtar@jefferson.edu

Salman Akhtar  

In his opening remarks, Dr. Akhtar explained that where and under what circumstances people grow up makes a profound difference in shaping their personality. He underscored how psychoanalysis, by virtue of its expanded exploration of elements beyond the impact of the immediate family, has become a much richer and more textured discipline. Alluding to the difference between the kinds of anxiety associated with times of crisis as opposed to times of prosperity and peace, he stressed the role of generational transmission of anxiety or any prevalent mood or affects in large groups and its profound hidden or explicit effect on children. Therefore, what's in the air is not always what is said but what is not said. These mixtures of what is said and not said then generate the volatility that can create group destiny and identity which is the subject of the panel.

The first speaker, Vamik Volkan, began his psychopolitical presentation by pointing out the importance of studying large group identity, which, alongside an individual's core identity, links him to millions of others within an ethnic, national or large group. To dramatize the workings of a large group identity, he compared the aftermath of two scenarios. First, he had the audience imagine Jack the Ripper striking in Rio and being caught, tried and incarcerated. He remarked that, after a while, he would be forgotten and that there would be no more talk about him. He then reminded the audience of the impact of Slobodan Milošević on the whole of Serbian society and how in contrast his scar will remain forever. Something, then, within society can be used for massive violence which does not belong to the individual. In this case, it was the reactivation of former grievances of the Serbs. Milošević inflamed the Serbs' ‘chosen trauma’ (Volkan, 1999). In order to reactivate that trauma, the 600 year-old bodies of three leaders were dug out and put in coffins and carried from one village to another for a whole year. This would guarantee that the schoolchildren would know about the battle of Kosovo in the 14th century. Therefore, when members of a large group are unable to mourn their loss and humiliation, they pass on the images of their injured selves and object images of those who hurt them to their offspring.

responseSet.title: Commentary on the Agnese Case
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on the Agnese Case

Paulo Guimarães Filho

Paulo Guimarães Filho

Rua dos Tamanás, 358-Alto de Pinheiros, 05444-010 São Paulo SP, Brazil—pduartegf@uol.com.br

Paulo Guimarães Filho  

Agnese presented herself to the analyst talking about her feeling that ‘everything lacked authenticity’ and how she ‘felt emotionally detached and she derived a little experience of truth only from painting, an activity she had taken up recently’. On the other hand, Roberto Basile also told us how, in the first interview, several pieces of the patient's clothes were red: the anorak, the scarf, the crash helmet, in addition to the bag that was from a fashion store, with the word ‘MAD’ in big letters. In this context, she spoke about how: ‘it's a beautiful day, but everything is so frozen outside’, which suggests to the analyst the emotional atmosphere in which she felt she was living. To our Italian colleague, the red colors represent the eruption of a powerful volcano and these initial elements led him to expect that the ‘first problem to come forward’ would be the impossibility of containing volcanic emotions. Such emotions would have to be frozen and the price to be paid was to follow life always at a distance, as if behind a screen of iced glass. The analyst also relates, very briefly, this mental functioning of the patient with the failures in the containment of her objects. She herself also said that she was always surrounded by ‘intelligent’ people, but that they didn't give her much back.

Following the presentation above, made by Basile, I thought that he was describing a patient with a mental world with very inconsistent objects, which contributes to her own fragility, particularly with respect to dealing with her emotional experiences. The most important consequence of this situation is the patient's need to protect herself by keeping emotional distance from people.

These are the aspects the analyst emphasized most and that became the central point of his approach. However, Basile also presented us with other very significant data of Agnese's mental life: the countless references to strong links to concrete objects, especially for their sensory characteristics or for the excitement they provoke. This occurs in such a manner that the analyst's attention is also caught by these aspects and, to a large extent, they are what he describes in his initial presentation. From the first interview, the red pieces of her clothes, including the red ‘MAD’ bag, stand out. Moreover, the analyst informs us that the patient was trendy, again showing the importance given to the clothing. We should also remember that the only activity in which she felt she experienced truth was painting. In the description of the patient changing her scooter for a bicycle, she also mentions the importance the excitement of the speed had for her. One may think that this quality is also present in the color red. Before examining the session concerning the movie about the pedophile, we cannot ignore that she had been involved in this exciting experience.

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Evelyne Schwaber

Evelyne Schwaber

4 Welland Road, Brookline, Massachusetts, USA—eschwaber@post.harvard.edu

Evelyne Schwaber  

It is an honor to be invited to respond to Jean-Michel Quinodoz's most interesting and richly illuminated clinical example portraying a deeply searching, candid, and self-reflective analytic work.

Shared in subtle, sensitive detail, this material offers an enhanced opportunity to engage the question of our mode of analytic listening. Lying at the essence of our clinical work, transcending our theoretical predilection as well as our own particular psychoanalytic culture, how we listen warrants, I believe, continued reflection. I shall draw upon Dr. Quinodoz's finely presented case in further pursuit of this question.

It has seemed to me that steeped within our listening, in the effort to bring forward what is unconscious, we incline toward inference of hidden meaning or intent, buttressed by our theoretical suppositions, and then attempt by our interpretive stance to guide the patient, however gently, toward recognition of what we believe we have already come to see. Discussions on technique have pivoted largely on how best to help the patient move in such a direction. While we may be open to reconsideration based on the patient's response, there remains nonetheless a fundamental epistemological quandary in the view that we can see meaning yet unconscious to the patient, for, indeed, we can only conjecture about the possibilities. What is unconscious can only be known in past tense, that is, once it has become conscious. Believing that the meaning lies unbeknownst to them yet perhaps to be seen by us, we lose sight of the fact that our vantage point, even our view of ourselves, is simply that—ours; the patient may have another, the inherent legitimacy of which is still to be found. We tend, thereby, to not ask, to not take our patients at their word, to presume defense when their perceptions do not match our own, or to skip over inquiry into passing communications—verbal and nonverbal, such as state shifts or pauses—that may, albeit seeming to derail us, lead to paths still to be learned. It is a paradox: in our search for what is hidden, we may bypass what is not, yet which, illuminated and explored, may lead us more profoundly and less inferentially toward what had lain hidden, heretofore unconscious.

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editors: Reply to Dr. Alexander
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Reply to Dr. Alexander

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

522 Dalehurst Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90024, USA—jgrotstein@earthlink.net
22 November 2005

James Grotstein  

Dear Sirs,

Dr. Alexander provides some interesting comments on Grotstein (2005), in which I re-examine the concept of projective identification and propose an additional concept, ‘projective transidentification,’ to address both the inclusion of the operation of projective identification proper and to address the mystery of how the external object becomes affected in the transaction with the subject. I agree both with Dr. Alexander and his citation of Bion that changes in theory may be less justifiable than creations of and changes in models of mind. Along that line, it was my impression that my contribution recertified Klein's omnipotent, intrapsychic model and, at the same time, offered an additional model of how the object became realistically affected.

One of the red threads running through my contribution is the premise that one had to distinguish between the phenomenological and the metapsychological perspectives when considering the action of projective identification.

268
