responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary

Ruth Stein

Ruth Stein

355 East 72nd Street, New York, NY 10021, USA—ruthstein111@aol.com

Ruth Stein  

This is a brief evocative narrative of an analytic encounter between a seasoned analyst and a deeply distressed and despairing young woman. I'm grateful to Dr. Rolland for the openness with which he offers his sensitive and subtle observations. The transparency and the keen precision with which he narrates the sequence of events in the sessions enables me, as I read his gripping account, to partially enter the threads of events and relations in his work with his patient and to articulate ways in which I understand it.

F, the patient, had an unparalleled impact on the analyst: ‘No other initial encounter with a patient has made such an impression on me as this one did.’ The scene shifts from mute dereliction to deathly agony and back again. F looks like a drop-out, a homeless person; her clothes are sexless, her face expressionless and corpse-like thin. Her long silence thickens a despair and desolation, the aridity of which can be dampened only by the tears that stream down her face and unfreeze her fixed features. The analyst takes all this in and understands wordlessly; he doesn't doubt what she's conveying and what he sees: sheer suffering. He doesn't (at least not immediately, and then only in a footnote) classify, categorize, or diagnose her. The two don't share anything contentual or verbal during this session, except a silent mutual physical presence. Dr. Rolland has a fine eye for the minute details of F's expressions and moods, particularly her shifts between distance and withholding, and opening up and communicating, at the same time as he is careful not to impose on her. He therefore sits with her silences, does not succumb to the temptation to be her savior, a temptation all too natural when in the presence of a deeply disturbed person desperately needing help, as is this woman.

In the face of such extreme suffering and urgency, I wonder why Dr. Rolland chose to set up weekly appointments with F, and why he let a week elapse between her first and second sessions, particularly as there had been hardly any verbal interchange between them. I wonder whether this low frequency was due to F's hospital arrangements. Did the hospital and the psychiatrist on whose behest F came to analysis serve as an auxiliary container, and/or an additional transference figure? Or did the dawning transferential merger (or twinship) between Dr. Rolland and F, born of their wordless communications in the first session, come to carry sufficient weight to hold the patient? In effect, in the following session, the patient ‘began talking almost at once.’ F spoke of her life-threatening anorexia, her recurrent suicide attempts, and her merger with her twin sister. We learn that F is variously but profoundly enmeshed with each member of her family.

responseSet.title: Body Modification
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Body Modification11

Asbed Aryan

Asbed Aryan

Julián Alvarez 1049, 1414 Buenos Aires, Argentina—asbedaryan@apdeba.org;asaryan@satlink.com.ar

Asbed Aryan  

Silvia Reisfeld presented a study into the meaning of tattoos, piercing, scarification and other acts of aggression in adolescents against their own skin. She examined both individual and cultural dimensions and the interrelationship between these two factors.

Hilda C. Catz's paper focused on body modifications and scars specifically related to the special features of parental inscriptions in our culture which resulted from inscriptions across different generations.

Lastly, Javier García presented a paper which emphasized the body as the object of these modifications, adornments and rituals. He drew attention to the fact that we were dealing with both an historic dimension influenced by ideologies from different periods of history, and also with an erogenous dimension seen in different forms of writing, which bring together different aspects of erogeneity and culture. He went on to detail the increasing popularity of tattooing and piercing, and, what is more alarming, body modification by means of branding with heat and scarification, or scars formed by cutting into the skin, a phenomenon which is gaining ground, in particular with adolescents and other urban groups.

Reisfeld's claim that tattooing has an elaborate intentionality was discussed. Some of the participants agreed that tattoo drawing is like a text to be deciphered and represents aspects of the ego and of the object, especially as an element that shows the mourning for a lost object. (Various clinical vignettes referred to the death of the father.) Other participants placed less importance on this idea and referred more to the actual act of body modification: the alteration of the skin and the suffering and pain caused by this, which were seen as elements common to other acts of aggression against the skin, such as scarification, branding, and the hanging of weights from a specific body part, causing stretching. It was suggested that, in the light of the above, the psychoanalytic meaning may have a darker, more individualistic side to it.

responseSet.title: The Stranger
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Stranger11

Carmen Mion

Carmen Mion

Rua Dr. Alceu de Campos Rodrigues, 275/101, 04544-000 São Paulo/SP, Brasil—carmenmion@uol.com.br
(Final version accepted 15 October 2004)

Carmen Mion  

dreams, narcissism, α-function, α-in-progress, reverie, holding

This work concerns a nine-year analytical experience with a patient who presented as a main feature an apparent inability to experience and express feelings. Right from the beginning the author was confronted with the question of the viability of analytical work, considering transference or emotional involvement in the absence of perceptible establishment of an affective link on the part of the patient. The patient never missed a session, was usually very punctual and presented very rich material, but the accounts of his life, everything he was saying, hearing and observing was manifestly deprived of any emotional meaning for the patient and consequently deprived of sense for the analyst. If, at the beginning, the question was how to communicate with the patient, after some time it became a problem of how to enable the patient to communicate with himself. Confirming an observation by Bion that the patient is the best colleague the analyst can have, the way forward was indicated by the patient himself. This article is a theoretical exercise based on this clinical experience, using concepts developed by Bion, Ferro and Winnicott.

Letters to the Editors

Erratum

Giuseppe Civitarese

Giuseppe Civitarese

Piazza A. Botta 1, I-27100 Pavia, Italy—gcivit@venus.it
26 May 2005

Giuseppe Civitarese  

On p. 719 of Civitarese (2006), some text was repeated. In the first paragraph, the text beginning ‘but more’ on line 12, and ending ‘“real event”’ on line 16 should be deleted. The latter part of this paragraph (from line 10) should now read:

However, it is likely that the transformative possibilities of dreams about the analysis do not reside so much (or not only) in the recollection of what Freud calls a memory of a ‘real event’ (either in terms of the patient's real past or in terms of phantasies from his internal world), which is obviously important but as a secondary, background issue, or as a byproduct of the psychoanalytic process, but also more in the theatrical game which engages both participants, and allows them to reach unusual levels of involvement in the service of developing the mind.

responseSet.title: Aesthetic experience: Beauty, creativity, and the search for the ideal by George Hagman Amsterdam: Rodopi (Contemporary Psychoanalytic Studies, vol. 5). 2005 168 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aesthetic experience: Beauty, creativity, and the search for the ideal by George Hagman Amsterdam: Rodopi (Contemporary Psychoanalytic Studies, vol. 5). 2005168 p.

Shierry Nicholsen

Shierry Nicholsen

1103 E. Republican St. #1, Seattle, WA 98102, USA—snicholsen@earthlink.net

Shierry Nicholsen  

The psychoanalytic literature on aesthetic matters is vast and wide-ranging—from inquiry into the psychodynamics of the artist to analysis of the creative process, to reflection on individual works of art. Rarely, however, has psychoanalytic reflection been directed to aesthetic experience as such and to the way the aesthetic dimension pervades human life. George Hagman's book does this.

Hagman's book is short, comprised of what were originally different essays on related themes in this area. The essays form a coherent whole, however, in large part because Hagman uses a theoretical lens that is fairly narrowly focused. He draws, of course, on Winnicott's idea that transitional space is the foundation of cultural experience, but he elaborates from a relational and especially from a self-psychological perspective, while providing a review of contributions from other psychoanalytic traditions. Each psychoanalytic theory has something to contribute to our understanding of aesthetic experience. Elaborating the contribution that self-psychology can make in this area is a second important contribution to Hagman's book. A third is to have brought philosophical aesthetics into the arena of psychoanalytic theorizing. Classical, 18th century, and modern philosophical aesthetics have created an extensive literature on key concepts in aesthetics, such as beauty, and also reflected on certain aesthetic experiences that have been largely ignored in the psychoanalytic literature—the ugly and the sublime being cases in point.

Hagman defines aesthetic experience as an ‘emergent phenomenon that arises in the transitional psychological zone in which our creative engagement with the world is lived’ (p. 1). From this perspective, aesthetic experience is not restricted to art and artists—it is a dimension of subjective experience, and (non-pathological) idealization is central to it. In brief, for Hagman the mutual idealization of mother and baby is the archaic foundation of aesthetic experience. The formal qualities of this relationship become the conveyors of the experience of beauty. The artist's relationship with the artwork then becomes a relationship with an idealized self-object. In this review I begin by summarizing Hagman's point of view on the role of idealization in aesthetic experience; then I comment on some of his specific chapters, and end with some discussion of areas in which Hagman might extend his contribution.

Verräter oder Verführte: Eine psychoanalytische Untersuchung Inoffizieller Mitarbeiter der Stasi [Betrayers of trust or themselves betrayed: A psychoanalytic investigationof the unofficial collaborators with the Stasi] by Ingrid Kerz-Rühling and Thomas Plänkers Berlin: Links. 2004. 245 p.

Friedrich-Wilhelm Eickhoff

Friedrich-Wilhelm Eickhoff

Engelfriedshalde 20, D-72076 Tübingen, Germany—fweickhoff@t-online.de

Friedrich-Wilhelm Eickhoff  

Supported by the Foundation for the Study of the Dictatorship of the Socialist Unity Party of the German Democratic Republic [GDR, Stiftung zu Aufarbeitung der SED-Diktatur], the authors, from the Sigmund Freud Institute, Frankfurt-am-Main, report the findings of a psychoanalytic investigation of former unofficial collaborators2 with the East German State Security Ministry. The book is one of a series of researches into East German society.

Application of psychoanalytical techniques to a political project is clearly within the tradition of Alexander Mitscherlich (Mitscherlich and Mitscherlich, 1975). The authors’ aim was to understand the conscious and unconscious motives of GDR citizens in collaborating with the State Security Service and to determine the possible psychic effects of their conspiratorial activity 14 years after the dismantling of the socialist party dictatorship, as well as the motives behind their consent to participate in this investigation. With the help of the government official formerly in charge of the records for the GDR State Security Service, whose colleague, Helmut Müller-Enbergs, examined the files and contributed a chapter to the book about them, 20 people were identified: 9 women and 11 men. Though not a representative sample of former unofficial collaborators, they consented to examination of their life histories using semistructured interviews. The interviews lasted 3-5 hours and were recorded on audiotape. A team of experts consisting of five psychoanalysts assessed the transcripts using an evaluation form. These were linked to the files of the Stasi (a German acronym for the East German Secret Police)

1Translated by Keith Westerfield.2Inoffizieller Mitarbeiter was the official term used by the East German government.306

Response to Emad Bishara

Susanne Chassay

Susanne Chassay

663 Harvard Ave, Menlo Park, CA 94025, USA—schassay@earthlink.net
3 April 2006

Susanne Chassay  

Dear Sirs,

I appreciate Emad Bishara's kind response to my article. My attempt to extricate myself from the kind of splitting that such acts of destruction as my patient's suicide give rise to has required me to hold polarized states, such as the arc of envy/generosity, in dynamic tension with each other.

863

responseSet.title: Melanie Klein by Julia Kristeva (Ross Guberman, translator) New York: Columbia University Press. 2001. 296 p. (European Perspectives: A Series in Social Thought and Cultural Criticism.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Melanie Klein by Julia Kristeva (Ross Guberman, translator) New York: Columbia University Press. 2001. 296 p. (European Perspectives: A Series in Social Thought and Cultural Criticism.)

Margaret Fitzpatrick Hanly

Margaret Fitzpatrick Hanly

96 Elm Avenue, Toronto, M4W 1P2, Canada—cema.hanly@utoronto.ca

Margaret Fitzpatrick Hanly  

This book, subtitled Madness: Matricide as pain and creativity, is the second volume of a trilogy on female genius, the first being on Hannah Arendt and the last on Colette. Kristeva, psychoanalyst and literary theorist, is a professor of linguistics at the University of Paris VII.

As a cultural historian, Kristeva understands the place of psychoanalytic thought in other disciplines which gives this work a greater breadth of reference and allusion than belongs to many works on psychoanalytic theory and technique. What marks this volume as the product of Kristeva's mind is: 1) the focus on ‘female genius’ as involving both ‘the desire for knowledge’ and an ‘affinity for the archaic’; 2) the setting up of a dialogue between the ideas of Freud, Klein and Lacan; and 3) the contextualizing of Klein's ideas within some facets of contemporary culture.

The book opens with a quote from Alix Strachey: ‘She's a dotty woman. But there's no doubt whatever that her mind is stored with things of thrilling interest. And she's a nice character’ (p. 5). Kristeva ends the section: ‘Melanie Klein has since become a major figure of indisputable worth—just like the psychoanalysis that she practiced so ingeniously’ (p. 6). The tone and content of the work are dramatic, at times provocative, quite different from the low-key, systematic and scholarly examination of Klein in the contemporaneous Likierman (2001).

A few headings will give a glimpse of the issues Kristeva tackles: ‘Jewish families, European stories’; ‘In the beginning was the death drive’; ‘The grief that affords us a soul’; ‘A most early and tyrannical superego’; ‘The cult of the mother or ode to matricide?’; ‘Do women have an affinity for the archaic?’; ‘The post-Kleinian approach to the archaic and the primary’; ‘Cultural acts of sublimation: Art and literature’; ‘The politics of Kleinianism’.

Kristeva's work can be read as an attempt to account for Klein's genius in her uncovering a set of infantile fantasies and impulses toward the mother's body and the powerful primitive emotions of anxiety and envy unleashed by the recovery process. Kristeva sees Klein's genius as emerging both despite and through a certain ‘madness’, through the raw primary-process imagery in her language. Kristeva calls Klein a ‘founder without a text’, for she sees a flawed English text (much revised by Riviere) as partially accounting for the remarkable influence of Klein's ideas. She views Klein's empiricism and her theoretical awkwardness as giving her work an open quality that invites interpretation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Play Misty for Me (1971): The Perversion of Love

Clint Eastwood , Eileen McGinley and Andrea Sabbadini

Andrea Sabbadini

65 Cheverton Road, London, N19 3BA, UK—e.mcginley@tiscali.co.uk

38 Berkeley Road, London, N8 8RU, UK—a.sabbadini@googlemail.com

Andrea Sabbadini  

Play Misty for me, Clint Eastwood's directorial debut, is a film about ‘mellow groove’ disc jockey Dave Garver (played by Eastwood) letting himself be seduced by a young woman, Evelyn (Jessica Walter), after she has asked him to play a song for her on his radio programme. Soon after, Garver finds himself stalked by Evelyn and increasingly persecuted by her obsessional demands, just as he struggles to re-establish a serious relationship with his girlfriend Tobie (Donna Mills). He tries to contain Evelyn's seductiveness and her blackmailing tactics, but, as she becomes murderously violent, this proves an impossible task: after a series of assaults on those close to him, she attacks him with a knife. Garver punches her in self-defence and she falls to her death into the ocean.

Play Misty for me is set in Carmel, the seaside town in California to which Eastwood would be elected Democratic Mayor 15 years later. This location also gave him the opportunity to indulge in his passion for music, shooting a scene at the nearby Monterey Jazz Festival. The film was written and co-scripted by Jo Heims and is based on the narrative of one of her female friends. This collaboration between Heims and Eastwood enriches both the female and male perspectives of the protagonists, and gives further depth to the accuracy of their psychological depiction. Although famous for his action movies such as Sergio Leone's ‘Spaghetti Westerns’ and the violent thriller Dirty Harry and its sequels, Clint Eastwood is in fact a liberal-minded and thoughtful individual (Bingham, 1994; Douglas, 1974; Smith, 1993) who detests violence and has shown its horrific consequences in many of his later films. Baker argues that it was indeed with Play Misty for me that Eastwood's ‘deconstruction’ of his own screen persona, as ‘the prototypical macho male Hollywood hero: cold, refractory, cruel, sadistic, murderous, ferocious and seemingly invulnerable’ (1999, p. 821), actually started. The traditional myth of masculinity, Baker concludes, ‘is thus challenged and undone by the icon of screen patriarchy himself’ (p. 823).

[[RunningHead]]

Pre-natal Traumas11

Lilia De Semeniuk and Elizabeth De Bianchedi

Elizabeth De Bianchedi

Gorriti 4674, 1414 Buenos Aires, Argentina—liliabordone@fibertel.com.ar

Uriarte 2112, 1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina—bianchedi@ssdnet.com.ar

Elizabeth De Bianchedi  

After the reading of Silvia Neborak's paper, the moderator asked colleagues to discuss their ideas about emotions, actions, fantasies and dreams as manifestations of pre-natal life, which are expressed in post-natal life and also in the psychoanalytic link. In psychoanalysis, countertransference is a basic way of its perception.

Dr. Monserrat, Barcelona, said that she had no doubts about the influence of what is prenatal in post-natal life, but that it is not always possible to detect and recognize this influence. She recounted that, during the Olympic Games in Barcelona, a pregnant woman detected strong fetal movements as a consequence of the very loud noises from fireworks during the festivities. In post-natal life, the child, when hearing loud noises, got frightened, remained paralysed and fell asleep. The author recalled two situations: first, of a pregnant woman, who was at a party where the noises were very loud and moved to a quieter spot to preserve the baby; second, of a patient whose mother spoke with a very loud voice. When something moved the patient emotionally, she had sharp noises in her ears and became dizzy as a reaction to the emotional contact.

Hilda Botero, Bogotá, commented that she has been able to prevent premature births when working with the dreams of pregnant women, which she referred to as intra-uterine interventions. Giorgio Corrente, Rome, introduced himself as someone who works with groups, and asks whether the dreamy state of a mother could change the mental basic assumption. According to Bion's theories, if there is no change in the basic assumption, there is no evolution. Could the basic assumption organization be an individual (and not a group) manifestation? Neborak told Dr. Corrente that he had underlined the importance of reverie and the primary capacity for a dreamlike state. Sara Flores, Lima, believed the author's countertransferential experience to be an expression of a communication from unconscious to unconscious, as termed by Freud. The author answered that, when the countertransference occurrence is followed by a memory, as in this case, an analyst feels he is on ‘safe ground’.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Review of The training Seminars of Ralph R. Greenson, MD

Lee Jaffe

Lee Jaffe

3262 Holiday Court, Suite 210, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA—lsjaffe@gmail.com
22 April 2006

Lee Jaffe  

Dear Sirs,

My book, which contains annotated transcripts of the Greenson seminars on assessment and the initial interviews, was reviewed by Jerome Winer (2006). I much appreciate his thoughtful comments, however, I do want to clarify three points.

First, in his review, Dr. Winer suggests that there might be some intrigue related to the fact that Greenson's library was donated to the Institute in San Diego rather than his own in Los Angeles. Here I must disappoint the reader, as the reason is not related to any sordid conflicts. In fact, Greenson always held a great fondness for the San Diego Institute since he was one of its founders. I regret not making this clear in the acknowledgments section of the book, as in this case I failed to consider how reasonable it is to suspect that splits and conflicts are the rule (not the exception) in our psychoanalytic circles.

Second, I appreciate Dr. Winer's highlighting the value of the book for candidates, but want to add that the book had two other target audiences described in the introduction. The book is also written as an opportunity for teachers to ‘observe’ the inspired teaching of psychoanalysis. For those of us who have concerns about how to train our teachers to be more effective, I hope that giving an example such as Greenson might be useful; it certainly has been for me.

Last, the book was written to provide some basic concepts for clinicians who work in the managed care environment, including those who do not have the benefit of analytic training. It is to this audience that I directed many of my annotations about insurance demands for very quick assessments and short-term treatments. The idea was to demonstrate the value of psychoanalytic insights beyond the practice of psychoanalysis. I am convinced that the future of psychoanalysis is brighter if we can use our insights to assist those working non-analytically, including those who administer medications. For this reason, the book is also directed at an audience of non-analytic clinicians and students, who should find Greenson's analytic insights about initial patient contacts and assessment useful. In the long run, such experiences could improve the reputation of psychoanalysis in the general mental health community, and even interest more clinicians in getting analytic training. Toward this end, my book was intended to be one small contribution.

[[RunningHead]]

Trained to kill: Soldiers at war by Theodore Nadelson Baltimore, MD: Johns Hopkins University Press. 2005. 208 p.

Adrienne Harris

Adrienne Harris

80 University Pl, 5th floor, New York, NY 10012, USA—aharris@psychoanalysis.net

Adrienne Harris  

This moving book, challenging both in its topics and its insights, represents a significant part of the professional psychoanalytic career of its author. Theodore Nadelson spent 40 years (from the aftermath of the war in Vietnam through work with soldiers from the Gulf War) treating American veterans in inand outpatient contexts at a Veterans Administration hospital in Boston. In this book, he summarizes a lifetime of observation and contemplation, holding out to the reader a powerful and tragic complexity: war making has an intense, mesmerizing effect on men, on their passions, loyalties, identities and bodies. In literary and clinical examples, Nadelson demonstrates how the excitement and the thrilling confirmation of identity and masculinity that military life, training, and fighting bring come to be inevitably counterpoised with the terrible unfixable sequelae of soldiering.

Nadelson's book draws on a number of different literary traditions. It is the memoir of a man (part Bildungsroman, part professional diary, part clinical notes)

1154

[[RunningHead]]

La situation analysante [The analysing situation] by Jean-Luc Donnet Paris: Presses Universitaires de France (Le fil rouge series). 2005. 216 p.

Geneviève Welsh-Jouve

Geneviève Welsh-Jouve

9 rue Fagon, F-75013 Paris, France—genevieve.welsh@club-internet.fr

Geneviève Welsh-Jouve  

In this book, the author gives us a gradual increase in the polyphonic complexity found in the psychoanalytic process, just as Johann Sebastian Bach did in the Art of fugue. No doubt, the interplay of polyphonic lines is here more important than was the instrumental individuality described in Donnet (1995). As in Bach's Well-tempered clavier, the functional adaptation of the instrument is meant to be an integral feature of the work. Now, Donnet studies the complexity of the process in a very rich and complex series of 11 chapters. Paradoxes, disjunctions, oppositions, contrasts and dialectic links are so numerous and subtle that I hardly dared to review and gladly welcome seven wonderful and tasteful vignettes that enlighten the metapsychological stakes.

In planning this review, I noted that the first chapter begins by quoting Polonius in Hamlet: ‘Though this be madness, yet there is method in't’ (II, ii, l. 206). Feeling slightly overwhelmed, I recalled Hamlet speaking of his own madness: ‘I am but mad north-north-west: when the wind is southerly, I know a hawk from a handsaw’ (l. 378). I decided to use as pointers four pairs of ‘subjects’—like four cardinal points or four parts in a fugue—as a ‘method’ for my review:

playing/protecting method;

illustration/interanalytic exchange;

acting/speaking; and

treatment/analysis.

Playing/Protecting Method

A basic question opens the book: how is it possible to let an associative sequence appear and disclose afterwards its unconscious logic? We take it for granted that we have to use the fundamental rule based on the psychoanalytic method.

1160

responseSet.title: On: Reading Loewald: Oedipus Reconceived
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Reading Loewald: Oedipus Reconceived

Marvin Osman

Marvin Osman

9735 Wilshire Boulevard, Suite 446, Beverly Hills, CA 90212, USA—Marpaosman@AOL.com
August 31, 2006

Marvin Osman  

Dear Sirs,

Since I have a particular interest in the characteristics of the Oedipus complex and its time and place in child development, I was much intrigued by Ogden's superb paper (2006), which delineated and discussed Loewald's reconception of the complex, including novel features differing from what was bequeathed to us by Freud. Reading it has stimulated me to submit views of my own in an endeavor to constructively supplement Loewald's formulation that Ogden refers to as ‘a watershed in the development of psychoanalytic thought’ (p. 651).

While not directly acknowledged by Loewald (nor, for that matter, Ogden) I believe a close reading of ‘The waning of the Oedipus complex’ (1979), in his intricately associating the Oedipus complex with the phases of separation-individuation of the first years of life and its ultimate resolution with the emancipation from psychic fusion with a parent(s) to relative autonomy, discloses by implication its origin and representation in early life, preceding the time designated for the Oedipus complex proper. Loewald points directly to the early origins of his conception when he states ‘…the oedipal attachments, struggles and conflicts must also be understood as new versions of the basic union-individuation dilemma’ (p. 775). And also when he says:

In the process of becoming and being an adult, significant emotional ties with parents are severed. They are not simply renounced by force of circumstances, castration threats, etc.—although these play an important instrumental role—but they are also actively rejected, fought against, and destroyed to varying degrees. …Be that as it may, in the course of what we consider healthy development, this active urge for emancipation comes to the fore (already in early phases of the separation-individuation process). (p. 756-7)

Therefore, according to Loewald, insofar as the Oedipus complex manifests itself ‘already in early phases of the separation-individuation process,’ this indicates that it actually covers a span of years longer than was generally thought, that is from early infancy all the way to latency, a trajectory coinciding with the vicissitudes of separation-individuation and, under the usual circumstances, to and during a period of relative autonomy, coinciding with the phallic phase Oedipus complex proper (according to Freud). I have depicted the characteristics of an early-life Oedipus complex (Osman, 2000; 2004) manifesting itself up to and sometimes even beyond the phallic phase, which bears similarity to Loewald's formulation.

[[RunningHead]]

Craft and spirit: A guide to the exploratory psychotherapies by Joseph D. Lichtenberg Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2005195 p.

Jane Tillman

Jane Tillman

25 Main Street, Stockbridge, MA 01262-0962, USA—Jane.Tillman@austenriggs.net

Jane Tillman  

This book addresses a plural audience of psychoanalysts, psychotherapists and the lay public, focusing mainly on technique. It is Lichtenberg's most recent in a prolific theoretical and clinical career as a distinguished psychoanalyst, psychotherapist, and teacher. Writing concisely and evocatively about intricate theoretical and technical matters, he covers a large psychoanalytic terrain with economy and clarity. Lichtenberg demystifies psychoanalytic language for the novice, while at the same time reinvigorating a familiar lexicon for the experienced clinician. In such an effort, complex subjects are rendered understandable and meaningful through the author's obvious skill as a writer. One drawback of his project is that at times rich theoretical material may be too condensed or simplified for the psychoanalysts in his audience. At times I was left feeling that the book was clinically sophisticated, but I also had a lingering experience that Lichtenberg's necessary simplifications skirt the real controversies and differences a deep analytic discussion might produce. On the other side, I also imagine a lay audience is not terribly engaged by the combative intellectual sparring that often occurs in our psychoanalytic culture. Apart from this dilemma, Lichtenberg's book is successful as a summary of the method and aims of exploratory psychotherapy. I found myself thinking of the concept of ‘clinical pearls’ or bits of wisdom received from a wise and seasoned analyst, and was reminded of the invaluable and generous Survival as a psychotherapist (Coltart, 1993), or the pearls of wisdom associated with Winnicott's writing.

1744

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self, Other and Dialogical Space in Autistic Disorders

Vera Fonseca and Vera Bussab

Vera Bussab

Rua Paulo IV, 292, 05657-130 São Paulo, SP, Brazil—verareg@ajato.com.br

Rua Paulo IV, 292, 05657-130 São Paulo, SP, Brazil—vsbussab@usp.br
(Final version accepted 8 February 2005)

Vera Bussab  

autism, dialogism, pervasive developmental disorders

The authors propose to re-evaluate Tustin's thesis using both the extensive clinical material of a child with an autistic disorder [PDD], and current research on infant development. Beebe's concept of ‘interaction structure’ that could be inferred from the fifth month of life using face-to-face research data is used for postulating a dialogic space that would be generated inside such structure, allowing for definition of self and other in a relationship. If such space is collapsed, the other would be a threat of annihilation for the self, thus it should be avoided or merged with, which could explain the systematic avoidance of the not-me in autistic states. The authors stress that these ideas are just one alternate model, among several, that seems to be able to explain the patient's deviant development.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

Mind the Gap: On Common Ground and True Pluralism

Patrick Luyten , Sidney Blatt and Jozef Corveleyn

Jozef Corveleyn

Tiensestraat 102, B-3000 Leuven, Belgium—patrick.luyten@psy.kuleuven.be;

300 George Street, New Haven, CT 05511, USA;

Tiensestraat 102, B-3000 Leuven, Belgium
18 January 2006

Jozef Corveleyn  

Dear Sirs,

We read with much interest the recent discussion between Wallerstein (2005a, 2005b) and Green (2005), and the subsequent responses (Epstein, 2005; Wallerstein, 2005c). In our opinion, this discussion is indeed urgently needed, as these issues are likely to determine the future of psychoanalysis both as a science and as a profession.

More precisely, this discussion touches upon two distinctions that are central in this debate: 1) between a nomothetic stance—aimed at generating probabilistic laws concerning human behaviour—and an idiographic stance—focused on understanding and explaining an individual case; and 2) between a more interpretive or intuitive or, for that matter, more clinical approach, and a more neo-positivistic, explanatory approach. Luyten et al. (in press) discuss these distinctions within psychoanalysis and argue that the juxtaposition of these approaches is not only unfruitful, but also, to a large extent, false. Any scientific endeavour involves interpretation, as any interpretation should involve testing ideas in a systematic way. In addition, psychoanalytic research should be about developing empirically based rules for translating nomothetic knowledge to individual cases as well as about the reverse process of taking observations in an individual case to establish the generality of these observations and the conditions for possible exceptions to nomothetic principles. In fact, we have argued that research should always involve such nomothetic-idiographic-nomothetic cycles, combining group designs with (multiple) case studies, to test and refine nomothetic knowledge at the level of individual cases, which can then be tested again in group designs and so on. To refer to such nomothetically derived clinical theories about the etiogenesis of psychopathology and the clinical process, we have coined the term ‘master narratives’ and have examined how these help in understanding the particular idiosyncratic narrative of a given patient as well as assisting in the identification of special conditions for their expression. The psychoanalytic literature contains some excellent examples of how nomothetic and idiographic research can inform each other (e.g. Ablon and Jones, 2005; Shedler and Westen, 2004; Hauser et al., in press). Unfortunately, however, the current psychoanalytic world largely seems to be divided into exaggerations of two cultures—one culture more interpretive and narrative in orientation, the other more neo-positivistic and probabilistic—that are not on talking terms with each other.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Critical Reflections on Intersubjectivity in Psychoanalysis11

Andrea Marzi , Gregorio Hautmann and Sandra Maestro

Sandra Maestro

Via Pian d'Ovile, 96, I-53100 Siena, Italy—amarzi@comune.siena.it

Via Cittadella, 39, I-50144 Firenze, Italy—poggio_sereno@tin.it

Via S. Martino, 83, I-56125 Pisa, Italy—smaestro@inpe.unipi.it
(Final version accepted 24 May 2005)

Sandra Maestro  

intersubjectivity, postmodernism, analytic process

The authors review the philosophical trend known as postmodernism and the way it has influenced a part of psychoanalytic thought, concluding with some comments on the qualities and shortcomings of the new developments. The authors consider the origins and the cultural and aesthetic-philosophical meaning of postmodernism, identifying some key concepts such as deconstructionism, the disappearance of the ‘individual subject’ and individual identity, and the rejection of ‘in-depth’ models of psychoanalysis. Then they examine various, wide-ranging developments in psychoanalytic thought and treatment. They review the intersubjective field in psychoanalysis, especially in the USA, and then explore whether the underlying lack of truth to be discovered, stressed by these ‘new view’ statements, or the fact that the ‘truth’ only exists in linguistic-narrative constructions is consistent with basic analytic concepts such as the unconscious, phantasy, transference and countertransference, which recall the tri-dimensional nature of inner psychic reality. The psychoanalytic process is a condition activated through a bond that is able to hold and contain the relationship of the analytic couple and the patient's unconscious world and not through hermeneutic or narrative constructions.

[[RunningHead]]

Sense of Reality, Reality Testing and Reality Processing in Borderline Patients11

Luis Figueiredo

Luis Figueiredo

Rua Alcides Pertiga 65, Cep 05413-000 São Paulo: SP, Brazil—lclaudio@netpoint.com.br
(Final version accepted 16 March 2005)

Luis Figueiredo  

borderline patient, reality test, sense of reality, depressive position, Oedipus complex

Although borderline patients are suitable for it, they often undergo repeated testing of phantasies against objective reality without success. A reason for these partial failures is that subjects have not been able to build a solid sense of reality and have found many difficulties for processing reality demands and possibilities. The author puts forward some thoughts on this question, such as the importance of working through the depressive position and the Oedipus complex in the process of building up a solid sense of reality. A sense of reality also favours continuous reality processing. When reality processing becomes as continuous as life itself, the repetition of individual reality tests becomes less frequent and necessary, and more satisfying. The author also presents some recommendations for the psychoanalytic treatment of such patients, stressing the importance and the difficulties of the introduction of the borderline patient into the triangular situation because primitive defences—such as disavowal and denial—are mobilized.

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editors: On: Whose Bion? Who is Bion?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Whose Bion? Who is Bion?

Tullio Carere-Comes

Tullio Carere-Comes

Viale Vittorio Emanuele 90, I-24121 Bergamo, Italy—tucarere@gmail.com
17 February 2006

Tullio Carere-Comes  

Dear Sirs,

I am very much in agreement with de Bianchedi in the belief that ‘the injunction to eschew memory, desire, and understanding gives us a model of an “evenly suspended attention”’, one which ‘is not Bion's perspective of Freud's proposition of an evenly suspended attention, but something much more complex and difficult to achieve’ (2005, p. 1532). Indeed, freedom from memory, desire and understanding must be established ‘as a permanent, durable and continuous discipline’(Bion, 1992, p. 315). But why should one feel compelled to engage in such arduous discipline? My response is: because there cannot be a really free-floating attention without freedom from memory, desire and understanding. On one side, free association and free-floating attention are not free at all, being just determined by unconscious drives and motives rather than by conscious intentions. On the other side, the recommendation to the analyst to work with an open mind, free from any preconception (Freud, 1912), means that the analyst should discipline his or her attention for it to remain evenly or freely suspended, that is not conditioned by the associative flux, nor by the analyst's theory. Only to the extent that such discipline (only implicit in Freud but made explicit by Bion) has been established is one free from the opacities that obstruct intuition. The more the analyst can approximate this freedom, ‘the more confidently can he discount the origin of his observations as due to the “personal equation”’ (Bion, 1992, p. 315).

As far as I know, Bion never used the term phenomenology to refer to his own approach, nor does de Bianchedi make any reference to the strict similarity between Bion's and the phenomenological approach. Yet they are so similar that one can hardly tell one from the other. The basic assumption is the same, i.e. the assertion of or the belief in the possibility of a direct, non-mediated knowledge of things through the faculty of intuition. In both perspectives, intuition is there, as an innate human or biological capacity, but usually obstructed or damaged by all sorts of preconceptions and expectations. To the extent that intuition is cleared from all opacities, by means of a special discipline that phenomenology calls epoché and Bion calls freedom from memory and desire, one gets to the basics of lived experience—or, in Husserl's terms, one returns to the things themselves.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The quiet revolution in American psychoanalysis:Selected papers of Arnold M. Cooper Edited by Elizabeth L. Auchincloss New York: Routledge (New Library of Psychoanalysis series). 2005277 p.

Malkah Notman

Malkah Notman

54 Clark Rd. Brookline, MA 02445, USA—malkah_notman@hms.harvard.edu

Malkah Notman  

Arnold Cooper has been a major contributor and supporter of the substantial changes in psychoanalysis by others in the past 25 years. He has also been instrumental in teaching and in encouraging new ideas. He has in some ways identified with the ‘outsider’, both in his personal history, and with the Columbia Psychoanalytic Institute as a splinter group originally created with an ideal of reforming psychoanalytic ideas. Although hardly an outsider in the current psychoanalytic scene, this perspective potentially offers him a basis for originality. He has advocated maintaining connections to the wider scientific and academic world, as well as the importance of research. He has been influential in integrating psychoanalytic concepts with psychiatry, in research, teaching and writing and has been active in international psychoanalysis.

This book brings together a number of papers in which Cooper proposes and develops his ideas. If there is a single thread, it is the author's openness to new ways of understanding psychoanalysis and taking a creative look at different theoretical concepts, terms and categories. Many of the ideas Cooper expressed have been subsequently studied and elaborated by others, so they have had a wide influence. He examines established ideas with a respectful but critical approach, assessing their current appropriateness, and puts all the debates he addresses and concepts he discusses into historical and theoretical context. The papers in this book were primarily published in the 1980s and early 1990s. Several notable ideas and formulations stand out. Cooper is among those currently questioning the validity and usefulness of the concept of ‘transference neurosis’. He presents the novel idea that castration anxiety is a façade for deeper and earlier conflicts about passivity, and he develops and elaborates the concept of the narcissistic-masochistic character, arguing for the ‘power of the weak’ as a masochistic seduction of the powerful.

Cooper emphasizes throughout the importance of pre-oedipal issues as compared to the classical idea of the origin of neurosis in the oedipal phase, and he recognizes that one overarching theory may not be possible or necessary for different clinical realities. He advocates considering research and observations from related fields that have relevance for psychoanalysis.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Use of the Past and the Present in the Clinical Setting

Pasts and Presents1

Janine Puget

Janine Puget

Paraguay 2475, 7º, 1121 Buenos Aires, Argentina—janinep@fibertel.com.ar
(Final version accepted 16 May 2005)

Janine Puget  

present to one's past, past to one's present, subject-subject relationship, interference

The author provides a brief summary of Latin American literature concerning temporality. She shows that a common theme throughout all these papers is that the analytical relationship is considered to be bipersonal and symmetrical, thus demonstrating a concern for establishing the basis by which social subjectivity can be reconsidered. This literature displaces the idea of linear time from its central position and introduces other measures of time. The analytical relationship takes place not only in the past but also in a newly created present. This is the ongoing present, of what is happening now, instantaneous and without a prior history attached to it. This leads the author to suggest that there is a present to one's history and a history to one's present. She then analyses the consequences of this proposition by examining some clinical material where she attempts to pinpoint those instances in which the analyst may have reacted defensively, tending to position himself in the analysand's past instead of being able to take action in the present. Clinical material from the IJP is used.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Contemporary Psychoanalysis in Ethnic Chinese Societies

New Developments1

Andrés Rascovsky and Teresa Yuan

Teresa Yuan

Arenales 1733, P.B. “B”, C1061AAU Buenos Aires, Argentina—rasmiandy@ciudad.com.ar

Marcelo T. de Alvear 2127, 6° “A”, C1122AAG Buenos Aires, Argentina—tyuan@psi.uba.ar

Teresa Yuan  

Many professionals, intellectuals, and politicians expectantly await the awakening of the ‘sleeping giant’, as Napoleon once referred to China. Members of the psychoanalytic movement appreciate how difficult will be the task of introducing and developing our science in that country, and this panel was organized to give initial perspectives. Before inviting the panelists to present, the moderator, with practical experience since 1995 of working in the People's Republic of China (PRC), Hong Kong and Taiwan, remarked that 2005 represents the 10th anniversary of the IPA starting operations in the PRC, which have knock-on effects throughout the region. Psychoanalysis is a cross-cultural discipline whose international discourse and practice face new challenges in ethnic Chinese societies. Although much research is required into these new encounters and developments, psychoanalysis will never lose its unique and metaphorical identity. This is one of the discipline's creative convergences and motive of inspiration for the Chinese thinking.

Alf Gerlach presented the paper ‘Psychoanalysis, an inspiration for Chinese culture?’ Until the beginning of the 1990s, psychotherapeutic methods used in the PRC were oriented to behavioral therapy or supportive techniques. The techniques were based on traditional Chinese models, which exclude disclosures about one's own feelings and thoughts to a stranger as well as openness to sexual desire. These cultural patterns are more oriented to conflicts of shame than to feelings of guilt and impede the adoption of insight-oriented psychotherapy.

First, Gerlach discussed psychotherapy and psychoanalysis in pre-revolutionary China. Western medicine was first introduced in the 19th century by Christian missionaries, the first psychiatric clinic opening in Canton in 1898. In 1935-9, Dr. Bingham Dai, a psychiatrist born in China and psychoanalytically trained in the USA, worked at the Peking Union Medical College, but was forced to emigrate to the USA after the Japanese occupation. Adolf J. Storfer, a member of the Viennese Psychoanalytical Society, who had escaped to Shanghai from Nazi persecution, started to publish the Gelbe Post, a German-language journal, but he too emigrated in 1939 due to the occupation.

responseSet.title: Should There be a Training Psychoanalyst Status?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Should There be a Training Psychoanalyst Status?11

Paul Israel

Paul Israel

83-7 ave d'Italie, F-75013 Paris, France—paul.israel@wanadoo.fr

Paul Israel  

Introduction

This question has no relevance in absolute terms; it only pertains within the frame-work of institutionalized psychoanalysis, founded on what some call Freud's second fundamental principle, stating that anyone who wants to practise psychoanalysis must undergo (or have undergone) psychoanalysis him- or herself.

The question then is: should the International Psychoanalytical Association (IPA) institute the status of training psychoanalyst? (See Dewald, 1973.)

Given that the analytic community has been divided ever since its inception on the issue of what constitutes psychoanalytic training, the question could seem naive, or even sardonic. While analysis of a future analyst as a sine qua non has never been brought into question, its status within institutional training has remained a source of opposition and even conflict. Through the decades, and in the various psychoanalytic societies, the range of positions on this question extends from ‘the recommendation to have undertaken analysis’ (without mention of where, when or with whom) all the way to the requirement that this analysis be or has been under-taken with a specifically designated psychoanalyst, accountable to the institute for this period of training. We all know how much individual experience influences the convictions held in these matters.

As it is not my intention to review the history of these discussions and the arguments used by opposing sides, my discussion of the question will be based on my own experience in the society to which I belong. The Paris Psychoanalytical Society (Société Psychanalytique de Paris, SPP) is among the associations which are strongly in favour of a necessary separation between so-called ‘personal’ analysis and the actual training process, entrusted to training institutes. Given that the IPA has recently accepted the idea that there could be other training models than the one proposed by Eitingon, we must reorient reflection on what a training analyst should be, in member psychoanalytic societies, focusing on the different stages of training and on the quantitative and qualitative support structures they require.

responseSet.title: The Role of Consciousness in Working with Trauma: Necessary Condition or Irrelevant Distraction?1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Consciousness in Working with Trauma

Necessary Condition or Irrelevant Distraction?1

Cordelia Schmidt-Hellerau and Fred Busch

Fred Busch

246 Eliot Street, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467, USA—drcshpd@comcast.net

246 Eliot Street, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467, USA—drfredbusch@comcast.net

Fred Busch  

Fred Busch opened the meeting by addressing the general question of this panel: psychic functioning seems to be increasingly understood by some analysts to be something categorically different in trauma and in neurosis. Since its access to meaningful remembrance is usually barred, the trauma tends to be re-enacted in the analytic relationship. Hence, different methods—in particular focusing on the analyst's empathy, holding and containing—are proposed for treating traumatized patients. Busch challenged these assumptions especially with regard to the necessity of alternate techniques, with a number of observations:

with reference to Freud papers on technique, there is a general tendency to enact in the transference—in neurotic as well as in traumatized patients;

most memories before age 7 are stored as motor memories, thus early trauma would tend to be enacted similarly as any other childhood experience;

a deep emotional involvement of the analyst, empathy and containing are the basics for any analytic process to flourish and thus not specific to the treatment of trauma patients;

differences in how trauma is represented need to be considered, based on whether we are dealing with ‘shock’ or ‘cumulative’ trauma and the age at which the trauma took place.

Busch suggested that the focus on ‘the analyst's way of being with the patient’, the relational perspective, obscures a number of questions that underlie the panel, i.e. the accessibility of trauma to consciousness.

Stefano Bolognini presented an analytic case, Lisa, a young woman who had survived a traumatic car accident in which her boyfriend was killed. Lisa, who wanted analysis for professional reasons, could remember every detail that led to the accident, but very little about her time in the car before she lost consciousness and fell into a coma for several days. Bolognini reported that the accident was for a long time in the background or even forgotten between analyst and patient.

1Panel held at the 44th Congress of the International Psychoanalytical Association, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, 30 July 2005. Panelists: Stefano Bolognini (Bologna), Alan Sugarman (La Jolla), Leopold Nosek (São Paulo).

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion

Trudy Mcguinness

Trudy Mcguinness

32 Cavendish Rd, London, NW6 7XP, UK—trudymcguinness@tiscali.co.uk

Trudy Mcguinness  

I feel privileged to have been asked to comment on Jean-Claude Rolland's fascinating presentation of his work with a severely ill patient. In this paper, Dr Rolland gives us an insight into both his theoretical position and his style of interpretation. This provides us with a welcome opportunity to attempt to understand, not only Dr Rolland's conceptual framework, but also how our French colleagues work; an analytic style which sometimes seems so different from ours.

It is quite apparent that Dr Rolland's style of interpretation, both in content and timing, is quite different from my own and, I would imagine, most of my British colleagues. The silence in the sessions is quite striking and, when Dr Rolland does make an interpretation, it is constructed by linking elements of the material, but with little explanation to the patient. Nevertheless, I think we would all be familiar with the material and the way in which it evolves in the session. I find it so interesting that such a different way of interpreting can elicit such familiar material. Dr Rolland's emphasis on maintaining the setting and not interrupting the flow of the free association is rooted in his belief about what he is doing as an analyst. In his view, if he can foster the bringing into consciousness of repressed thoughts and wishes, this will fundamentally force internal reorganisation of the psychic apparatus, very much following the thoughts of Freud pre-1923. Clearly, Dr Rolland's theoretical understanding of the patient underpins his interpretations, as is true for all of us, but because he says so little it sometimes seems, on reading the progression of the sessions, that there are some aspects of the material that might seem fundamental to me, that he chooses not to interpret. It is difficult to know if he does not see something in a particular way, or if he feels that an interpretation would be counter to what he is trying to achieve. For example, in the first, silent consultation, it is clear that he is quite aware of the patient's ambivalence and conflict about coming, yet he says nothing to the patient about those conflicts and anxiety. Would he believe that a comment about her conflict between life and death, or her hope and despair would be an acting out on the part of the analyst, prompted by the analyst's own anxiety?

responseSet.title: Sexual Abuse: The Abusive Family Unit
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sexual Abuse: The Abusive Family Unit11

Serapio Marcano and Adriana Prengler

Adriana Prengler

Calle San Rafael, Quinta ‘Shuruata’, Urb. Santa Fe Norte, Caracas 1080, Venezuela—serapiomarcano@cantv.net

Calle Los Andes, Quinta ‘Lali’, Urb. Prados del Este, Caracas 1080, Venezuela—lalipren@telcel.net.ve

Adriana Prengler  

There were three presentations, beginning with ‘Sexual abuse: The abusive family unit’, which was given by Maria Kuitca, with Elda Irungaray. The authors presented the results of an investigation, the aim of which was to ascertain if it was possible to evaluate the degree of success in the psychoanalytical treatment of infant/juvenile sexual abuse, and used a clinical case study to exemplify their presentation. The theoretical investigation used a number of theoretical parameters which included physical and socio-environmental factors and the characteristics of the psychosexual development of the child used in psychoanalysis, which include the concept of the early Oedipus. It was emphasized that the critical developmental stage of the child is of fundamental importance when sexual abuse takes place. Other theoretical parameters mentioned were group violence, the interplay of roles, and the pathology of the transgressor, whose borderline substratum may be concealed behind a melancholic and psychopathic defence system. The differentiation between these two factors is fundamental in as far as this may change the prognosis and the possibility of working towards the reconstitution of family bonds even in the absence of the family members living together. Also mentioned as a theoretical parameter was the degree of risk for the abused child after the revealing of the secret which had been maintained within the family unit or from the outside world. In both the former and the latter, the determinant factors are implicit in the unconscious family structure. Finally, a difference between sexual abuse, rape and incest was also mentioned. It was stressed that, in order to monitor the progress of the therapeutic task, it should be carried out within an interdisciplinary network which would enable the integration of individual therapeutic intervention with that of the family, the couple and other social organisms. It was highlighted that violence in the context of the family arises just as much from socio-cultural problems as from emotional disturbances. These are both multicausal and interdependent and can lead to sexual abuse within the family.

Maurice Apprey  

The ‘design’ concept in this book's title has every potential for creating controversy in the field of psychoanalysis. In such fields as architecture, engineering and management, ‘design’ is a perfectly legitimate subfield with longstanding traditions. In our field, there is much greater reflexivity between patient and analyst, subject and object, agent and an object of change. There is negotiation, there is intersubjectivity everywhere. Even when a story is potentially not about an analyst, that analyst may still be in the story. Architects, engineers and managers are, nevertheless, not completely free of problems of ambiguity even if they have been grappling with them with relative success in resolving tensions between the designer and designed. In management science, Buchanan (1992) has written about ‘wicked problems in design thinking'—a most evocative metaphor and a reference to indeterminacy

Controverses sur l'autisme et témoignages [Autism: Debates and testimonies] by Denys Ribas Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. 2005. 206 p.

Sesto-Marcello Passone

Sesto-Marcello Passone

108 bis, bd A.-Blanqui, F-75013 Paris, France—sesto.passone@psp.ucl.ac.be

Sesto-Marcello Passone  

There is a vast popular scientific literature on the subject of autism—and on child-hood autism in particular. This disorder exerts a powerful fascination both through its profoundly enigmatic nature and through its consequences for those affected by it. Long overlooked, this major disorder identified by Leo Kanner has now been the source of a field of diverse practices, theories and research for around 50 years. These generally conflict or even clash within a context of passionately held views with ideological tendencies that only raise further questions. The author of other works on the treatment of autism, Denys Ribas focuses his discussion here on the aspects that may foster an open and constructive debate about autism: the nature of the disorder, current scientific theories on the subject and their implications for methods of treating those affected.

Drawing on a fund of experience obtained over several years as director of an outpatient clinic in Paris, his training as a child psychiatrist, membership of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society and editor of the Revue Française de Psychanalyse, Ribas sets out to ‘take apart’ that which in current theories of autism—psychoanalytic and cognitive theories in particular—too often brings an ideological slant to this highly impassioned debate. He asks, however, if it may still be possible to find some points of convergence.

[[RunningHead]]

The telescoping of generations.Listening to the narcissistic links between generations by Haydée Faimberg Hove: Routledge (New Library of Pyschoanalysis). 2005. 156 p.

Stefano Bolognini

Stefano Bolognini

Via dell'Abbadia n.6, I-40122 Bologna, Italy—fef8279@iperbole.bologna.it

Stefano Bolognini  

Can reading a scientific text directly influence the day-to-day work of a psycho-analyst? I believe so, and I think that Faimberg's is one of the few books which can really open up new perspectives and provide us with unusual tools for reading; as much in the reconstruction of patients’ personal journeys as in the understanding of what happens in the analytic experience.

The author has had scientific papers published in specialist journals, has participated actively at numerous conferences and is known for her commitment to the IPA, which has kept her up to date on the developments in the psychoanalytic movement around the world. She trained in Argentina, then moved to France, and has always paid keen attention to developments in British, Italian and North American psychoanalysis. The author offers us a highly original perspective, thanks to her well-integrated multiculturalism.

This latest contribution is enriched by its ‘wide-angle lens’ perspective, shown by the acknowledgements, in which Faimberg expresses her gratitude and debt to teachers and colleagues from a broad spectrum of times and places. From her original professional training in Latin America, these include Willy Baranger, Jose Bleger, David Liberman, Jorge Mom, Enrique Pichon-Riviere and Guillermo Maci; and from her subsequent scientific development in Europe, André Green, Piera Aulagnier, Jean Laplanche, Serge Leclaire, Wilfred Bion and Joyce McDougall.

The book includes an introduction by the author, and is organized into 11 chapters, which are only partly the result of the reworking of papers published previously during the course of her lengthy and rigorous research on the subject, which has been deepened consistently and continuously over many years.

Faimberg's theoretico-clinical viewpoint originates from those who leave their mark in the first chapter, which gives the book its title and which is dedicated to the study of ‘alienated identifications’. The chapter provides a fascinating reflection on the stories of patients which, in many cases, are ‘stories of somebody else’. We are presented with an original view of the transmission of narcissistic links between generations. The analytic couple gradually discover, through the analysis of the transference and an understanding of the past, the secret ‘tax’ which the subject pays—without knowing it—to people and situations which, by this time, exist only in his internal world. From there, these people keep him bound to outdated mandates of restoration and reparation, which actually belong to previous generations, and which the family has unconsciously transmitted to him. Mario, a seriously ill patient, shows these dynamics in an exemplary way (and in fact has become a famous case in contemporary psychoanalysis).

Dominique Bourdin  

A member of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, Claude Balier has run a prisonbased regional medical service providing psychiatric and psychological treatment for detainees who are often serving long sentences. The majority of his patients have committed criminal offences, murders, sexual assaults or other violent acts. For many years, this author, with substantial clinical experience, has been exploring both what forms of psychoanalytic and multidisciplinary care are possible in a setting of this kind and the psychic functioning of patients with a propensity for extremely violent behaviour. Balier has set up an association that specializes in clinical practice and discussion concerning the psychotherapeutic treatment of sex offenders (in groups and individual follow-up care).

His previous books (Balier, 1988, 1996) mainly proposed a clinical approach to patients of this kind, as well as some discussion of the methodological conditions of their follow-up care. At the same time, he presented his quest for working concepts to use in considering these pathologies: psychoanalytic practice, technique and theory today are closely interconnected.

[[RunningHead]]

Le livre noir de la psychanalyse: Vivre, penser et aller mieux sans Freud [The black book of psychoanalysis: Living, thinking and feeling better without Freud] Edited by Catherine Meyer Paris: Les Arènes. 2005. 827 p.

Simon Kipman

Simon Kipman

68 rue de Vaugirard, F-75006 Paris, France—s.d.kipman@wanadoo.fr

Simon Kipman  

‘Black is Black’

A vast tome targeted at the general public, ‘The black book’ was the subject of a major media campaign even before it went on sale. It is this campaign that has given it a readership among psychoanalysts, leading some of us to debate it in the media; it is still unclear whether this stems from a particularly successful promotion or an element of public opinion. In fact, it becomes clear from reading this book that there is no room for debate and that the polemical tone deliberately adopted is rather intended to generate interest and emotion and to ‘dazzle the mind’, to echo a director of a major private television network in France. The book's title and subtitle (‘living … better without Freud’) are more reminiscent of the sensationalist press than of a serious scientific work.

This book consists of two parts of differing length: one sets out to be a formal indictment of Freud and therefore everyone who has followed him. The other is an apologia for the therapeutic methods propounded by the authors, which can be grouped under the general heading of ‘behavioural therapies’ (I avoid the term ‘psychotherapies’ because the association to which they belong in France refers to ‘cognitive-behavioural therapies’).

Now Sigmund Freud is long gone, while psychoanalysis is undoubtedly thriving better now than in its founder's day. Fortunately, thought does not depend on one single author of reference, or reverence. Through its different methods of application, psychoanalysis specifically sets out to enlarge the capacity or freedom to think among those who undertake it. It is therefore unclear what purpose is served by this indictment of Freud, any more than by rewriting history through a challenge to the portrayal of one particular character. The historical method deployed, which consists in commenting on and condemning a specific author's attitudes and behaviour, is more like an investigative report in the popular press than a historical re-examination or review. In short, we might have expected more of a radical, even impassioned, ‘critique’ of psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Intergenerational Transmission of Trauma: What We Have Learned From Our Work With Mother and Infants Affected by the Trauma of 9/111
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intergenerational Transmission of Trauma

What We Have Learned From Our Work With Mother and Infants Affected by the Trauma of 9/111

Miriam Pierce and Anni Bergman

Anni Bergman

200 W 57th St, New York, NY 10019, USA—mimipierce@aol.com

224 W 20th St, New York, NY 10011, USA—aebergman@aol.com

Anni Bergman  

The World Trade Center Project (WTCP) was conceived as a response to the 9/11 disaster. Anni Bergman in her introduction shared her own disbelief responses to the disaster as she recalled her early reactions to her World War II experiences. She noted that seeing the WTC disaster on TV confronted her with the terrible reality of the event. The magnitude of the attack was unimaginable. New Yorkers were experiencing not only disbelief but also a sense of helplessness. The smoke from the site covered the city. It was a frightening time; many families were impacted. Some of the men killed were husbands and expectant fathers. They left behind pregnant wives as widows when the towers went down. These women had sustained tragic losses during a critical time in their lives and the lives of their now fatherless children. These expectant mothers and their unborn children became the concern of the WTCP. The WTCP was designed as a pro bono effort to offer professional help that included primary prevention, early intervention with an optional research component for the future. In October 2001, Beatrice Beebe, Phyllis Cohen, Joseph Jaffe, Anni Bergman and the members of this panel came together to work on and establish the WTCP. All the members have a background in parent-infant psychotherapy and are actively engaged in the study and practice of this form of psychoanalytically informed treatment.

The unique component of the WTCP included the use of laboratory videotaped face-to-face interactions with infants and mothers at different time sequences, video play sessions with mothers and their children, and then feedback sessions with mothers using the videotapes in conjunction with the various therapists who were working with the family. The use of videotape feedback sessions acted as a powerful intervention for both therapists and the mothers as they viewed and shared their responses to tapes of the babies at play and the babies' interactions with the mothers and therapists.

[[RunningHead]]

Trauma, Envy and Revenge11

Lawrence Brown and Carole Tarantelli

Carole Tarantelli

37 Homer St, Newton Center, MA 02459, USA—larry.brown8@comcast.net

via delle Alpi 27, I-00198 Roma, Italy—c.tarantelli@libero.it

Carole Tarantelli  

Carole Tarantelli, in giving a brief overview of the presentations, highlighted the possible relationship of the concept of the death instinct to trauma and the varying opinions of its relevance held by the three presenters. The question of whether traumatic experiences unleashed the death instinct in the victim which was then projected on to the perpetrator was also to be addressed.

Sharon Roberts dealt with the theme of revenge in Euripides's Medea. She asserted that the play demonstrated the pain of betrayal and the pleasure of revenge. What factors might lead a mother to kill her children? In answering this question, it is important to consider the notion of the ‘alien,’ which in Euripides's drama is the placing into the protagonist of disavowed aspects of the human condition. Maternal filicide stirs disbelief in the audience that any mother could commit such a crime, which is even more horrifying than Oedipus's patricide and incestuous relationship with his mother. It is clear that the dangers of incest and patricide are less frightening than the thought of maternal filicide. While psychoanalysis has given great weight to the Oedipus myth, Roberts questioned whether the Medean story should be accorded wider significance in our analytic thinking.

Roberts posited a Medea complex that is characterized by a mixture of betrayal and revenge as a reaction to trauma. Jason's actions betrayed Medea and caused her to seek revenge for the loss of his love and the threat that she and her sons would be exiled. Medea killed their children to exact a revenge on Jason, an act that Roberts sees as the ascendance of her death instinct released by the trauma she endured. Roberts related her idea to the work of Laub and his colleagues who assert that trauma can trigger the emergence of the death instinct. The contributions of Freud and Klein regarding our understanding of the death instinct were briefly reviewed and, in this regard, Medea's actions are viewed as a reflection of her death instinct that was saturated by powerful feelings of envy. Roberts highlighted the insatiable nature of revenge and discussed Euripides's inherent appreciation of the destructiveness, especially in its envious appearance, in the character of Medea.

Psychic trauma: Dynamics, symptoms, and treatment by Ira Brenner Northvale, NJ: Aronson. 2004. 343 p.

Michael Good

Michael Good

74 Craftsland Road, Chestnut Hill, MA 02467, USA—michaeligood@pol.net

Michael Good  

The current renaissance in interest in psychic trauma echoes controversies from the earliest years of psychoanalysis. Contemporary ideas about trauma had their origins in the study of hysteria, dissociation, and repression. Reflecting that fact, the DSM-III (APA, 1980) still divided hysteria into ‘dissociative type’ and ‘conversion type.’ Beginning with the DSM-III-R (APA, 1987), however, this pair of ‘types’ was split asunder into separate ‘disorders’ (conversion disorder and dissociative disorder) and presently is no longer linked under the rubric of ‘hysteria.’ Some would view such current terms as ‘PTSD/borderline’ and ‘dissociative identity disorder’ as derived from persisting questions about the nature of hysteria, trauma, fantasy, and reality. The so-called ‘mysterious leap from the mind to the body’ in conversion (Deutsch, 1959) has an analogue in dissociation with what I would call the ‘mysterious leap from the mind to the psyche.’

From a primarily clinical viewpoint, a number of these issues are embedded in Brenner's book.

responseSet.title: Aspects of a Compliant Container: Considering Narcissistic Personality Configurations1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aspects of a Compliant Container

Considering Narcissistic Personality Configurations1

Vera Lamanno-Adamo

Vera Lamanno-Adamo

Rua João Mendes Junior, 180/17, CEP 13024-030, Campinas, SP, Brazil—veraadamo@uol.com.br
(Final version accepted 15 October 2004)

Vera Lamanno-Adamo  

container-contained dynamics, compliance, perverse transference, narcissistic defences

Attempts to expand Bion's container-contained model and to use it as an instrument for investigating psychic disorders arising in the analytical context have led the author to conjecture about the model's dynamics. In this paper, she illustrates a hypothetical compliant container by clinical material, in order to describe a psychic structure that contains only what is pleasant, only that which does not cause conflict or pain. Through a dynamic of ideal accommodation, a compliant container makes any disagreements, contradictions, limits and differences disappear, recognizing only what is very familiar and commonplace. The results are misleading, pseudological thoughts and concepts founded on strategic plans to maintain a narcissistic defensive system.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Lost childhood and the language of exile Edited by Judit Szekacs-Weisz and Ivan Ward London: Imago East West: Freud Museum. 2004. 293 p.

André Haynal

André Haynal

5 bd des Philosophes, CH-1205 Genève, Switzerland—andre.haynal@freesurf.ch

André Haynal  

This book is composed around a metaphor. ‘Lost childhood’ can be considered as an experience we all have; losing continuity between childhood and what follows later or experiencing disruption through specific life events like orphanage or migration. This work was conceived by three Hungarian-born Jewish female psychoanalysts, Judit Szekacs-Weisz (in London), Kathleen Kelley-Lainé (from Toronto, now in Paris) and Judit Mészáros (in Budapest, after many years in Washington, DC). It contains contributions made at diverse ‘lost childhood'conferences in London, Paris and Budapest during recent years. These encounters led to the creation in London of a place offering analytical psychotherapy in over 20 languages. This space allows therapists to deal with experiences of people living in changing contexts and languages, diverse cultures, and to find creative solutions for difficulties emerging from these situations.

To work in this field implies being confronted not only with the dimension of loss per se, but also with those of language and history. It requires not only a multilingual, but also a multidimensional understanding of the underlying forces shaped by historical changes, political manipulations, economical currents and sociocultural transformations. Accordingly, this volume brings together a rich array of essays written by a great diversity of people working with and/or being themselves asylumseeking exiles and/or other migrants.

311

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editors: On: ‘Projective Transidentification’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: ‘Projective Transidentification’

Richard Alexander

Richard Alexander

436 N Roxbury Dr, 216, Beverly Hills, CA 90210, USA—RAlexan564@aol.com
11 November 2005

Richard Alexander  

Dear Sirs,

Grotstein (2005) attempts to justify the addition of a new theory and a new terminology (projective transidentification) to deal with a problem that Bion had drawn attention to regarding the established notion that Klein's theory of projective identification is based on ‘an omnipotent phantasy’ (Bion, 1974, p. 105). However, this formulation, Bion points out, must be incomplete, since, when carried out by the patient, the analyst is often adversely affected in a very real way, for example the analyst may ‘have feelings of persecution or anxiety’ (p. 106). In other words, something beyond just ‘an omnipotent phantasy’ must be in the works.

With regard to these remarks, this phenomenon was of interest to Bion earlier (1961, p. 149), where he describes experiencing a sense of ‘being manipulated’ by the group leader and its members as if he were ‘playing a part in somebody else's phantasy,’ and in certain other group situations where he ends up feeling that he has made a terrible blunder and is guilty of blasphemy for his not conforming to group needs and expectations that he exercise omnipotent curative powers.

Grotstein points to Bion's comment that ‘we might consider whether there is not some other theory which would explain what the patient is able to do to the analyst’ (1974, p. 106). But Grotstein quotes incompletely, which results in his apparently not recognizing that what is being said is somewhat tongue in cheek, with Bion going on to say that ‘the trouble with [analytic] theories is that they so soon make themselves out of date,’ hence Bion prefers models over theories because models can more easily be discarded and replaced.

267
