responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Pan-Dora's BoxFetishism, Hysteria, and the Gift of Death in Die Büchse der Pandora (1929) Director: G. W. Pabst

Andrew Webber

Andrew Webber

Cambridge, CB3 0DS, UK - ajw12@cam.ac.uk

Andrew Webber  

While it was chance as much as anything else that led G. W. Pabst to direct the first film to deal explicitly with psychoanalytic therapy, Geheimnisse einer Seele [GS, Secrets of a soul] (1926), there is also arguably a particular, albeit ambivalent, affinity between his topics and techniques and those of psychoanalysis. Rentschler (1990) suggests that the exploration of psychoanalytic treatment in GS is also an ambiguous vehicle for the analysis of filmic treatment. On the one hand, the director is seen here as an alter ego of the ordering analyst, working towards an apparently successful narrative resolution of the case. On the other, the analytic treatment produces what might be called a neurotic compromise with the fantasies that the film releases and that threaten to exceed its control. Psychoanalysis will always look for evidence of how film serves to represent the repressed, but this first psychoanalytic film shows evidence of a repressive tendency that inheres in its appropriation of psychoanalytic material and method. It opens a Pandora's box of psychical secrets and then struggles to find closure for it.

The resolution of therapy and film narrative in GS, Rentschler argues, is ‘overly emphatic, almost hysterically thorough’ (1990, p. 7). And this symptomatic excess of control, in its turn, can be linked to various more unruly aspects of the film's aesthetic management: mismatches and unresolved enigmas that subvert its styling. I would like to suggest that Pandora's box [BP] displays similar symptomatic features, that its highly finished and ordered surface is broken and knotted at certain points by the psychical disorder that it conceals. While Pabst famously introduced an oblique and fluid style of editing into filmmaking, one that tends to hide its cuts and smooth its knots, elements of disjuncture are also displayed here, as in the mismatches of point of view identified by Doane (1990, p. 67). The ‘Lulu’ (1996) plays by Wedekind upon which the film is based are fraught with psychical disorder, from insomnia and obsessive-compulsive behaviour to hysteria and pervasive paranoia, and aspects of this diagnostic repertoire are carried over into the film version in less explicit form.

Björneborgsvägen 5 O, FIN-00350 Helsinki, Finland—barbara.mattsson@pp.inet.fi

Barbara Mattsson  

In the Finnish Psychoanalytic Society, there are several members who subscribe to classical psychoanalysis with its well-known emphasis on instinct theory and infantile sexuality. This is the path that the author follows in her work on the female psyche, and this book develops some themes recognizable from her previous book (Reenkola, 2002). The author sets out to trace the psychic work involved in becoming a woman.

Neri Daurella

Deu i Mata 152, E-08029 Barcelona, Spain—neri_dau@hotmail.com

Neri Daurella  

This book is the longest in the output of one of Spain's most prolific and well-known psychoanalytical authors, and illustrates well his professional profile, which also includes psychology and psychiatry. Throughout his long professional life, Tizón has found a way to combine dedication to clinical psychoanalysis in private practice with the management of a psychosocial primarycare team in the public mental-health system, the first such in Barcelona. Some of his numerous research- and teaching-inspired articles and books are devoted to reflection on epistemology and psychoanalytic knowledge and to dealing with psychopathology from a clinical psychoanalytic perspective; others deal with the application of psychoanalysis to healthcare. The latter (particularly Tizón García, 1996, 1999) have become standard works of reference for many interested Spanish-speaking readers.

responseSet.title: Impossible training: A relational view of psychoanalytic education by Emanuel Berman Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2004. 279 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Impossible training: A relational view of psychoanalytic education by Emanuel Berman Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2004. 279 p.

Janet Hadda

Janet Hadda

850 Masselin Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90036, USA—hadda@ucla.edu

Janet Hadda  

This book is intelligent, kind-spirited, and forward-thinking, if imperfectly integrated. The subtitle suggests that the author will use relational concepts to comment on psychoanalytic training. And Berman, Professor of Psychology at the University of Haifa and Training Analyst at the Israel Psychoanalytic Institute, does just that. But he also wants to show that the idea of a two-person psychology has been an active, if antiestablishment, presence in psychoanalysis since the early days of Freud. This ambition leads him to provide a long discussion of two early dyads that, in the end, splits the book into two separable entities.

Readers looking for a restrictive definition of the term ‘relational’ will not find it here, nor is such fine distinction Berman's intent. For him, the word is inclusive. Whether relational psychoanalysis, intersubjective approach, or two-person psychology are spoken of, the main point is the same: in every psychoanalytic dyad, there are always two mutually influencing subjectivities, each with fantasies, ideas, previous and simultaneous external relationships, and shifting affective states. Every analysis proceeds within this complex matrix.

Berman goes to greater lengths to show that the concept of the relationship between analyst and analysand has been an important consideration for theorists since the beginning of analytic history. His comments about the struggles between Freud and Ferenczi, on the one hand, and Klein and Winnicott, on the other, are fascinating to read. They are also painful, as the anguish of both Ferenczi and Winnicott becomes apparent when their pleas for the importance of external reality and interpersonal dynamics as essential aspects of psychological development fall on deaf ears and skeptical minds.

The essence of Berman's lengthy exposition can be summarized in his own words:

These days, we all seem to be constantly negotiating our way between the inner world of fantasy (Freud) and the impact of family and environment (Ferenczi) as shaping personality; between drives and defenses (Freud) and self-object relations (Ferenczi) as central inceptions; between interpretation (Freud) and affective experience (Ferenczi) as our main therapeutic tools; and between viewing ourselves primarily as impartial observers (Freud) and viewing ourselves primarily as involved in a mutual intersubjective process (Ferenczi). (pp. 62-3)

responseSet.title: Sex in the Mourning: Oedipal Love and Loss in The Door in the Floor (2004)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex in the Mourning

Oedipal Love and Loss in The Door in the Floor (2004)

Tod Williams and Gavin Ivey

Gavin Ivey

Private Bag 3, Wits 2050, Johannesburg, South Africa—iveyg@umthombo.wits.ac.za

Gavin Ivey  

Psychoanalysis can never become the dominant or received perspective from which to view ourselves, precisely because the truths it presents us with are so perturbing. But it does insinuate itself into our cultural reality in various ways, exposing us to ourselves and reminding us how we continue to live and dream in the shadow of Freud. Tod Williams's screen adaptation (2004) of the novel A widow for one year (John Irving, 1998) is a good illustration of Freud's enduring importance, and how film can simultaneously entertain and confront us with difficult aspects of our psychic reality. In this regard the Oedipus complex, cornerstone of Freud's personality theory, is perhaps the most unpalatable. This is hardly surprising given the unconscious desires and fears it captures. As Freud himself noted, ‘None of the findings of psycho-analytic research has provoked such embittered denials, such fierce opposition—or such amusing contortions—on the part of the critics as this indication of the childhood impulses towards incest which persist in the unconscious’ (1900, p. 263 [fn, 1914]). It is thus always interesting to see contemporary cinematic portrayals of oedipal themes. Despite its comic moments, Williams's film is a dark contemplation of oedipal sexuality and loss, and the unconscious strategies employed to avoid these facts of life in the psychological crucible of family relationships.

Ted Cole (Jeff Bridges), a successful writer and illustrator of children's books, is living in the aftermath of the tragic death of his two teenage sons in a motor car accident. The beauty and serenity of his Long Island seaside home merely accentuates the pervasive melancholic atmosphere. The opening images of the film show us Ted's naive but evocative ink drawings illustrating his children's story, The door in the floor. Our eyes are drawn to one particular image: a crouching human figure reaches out to open a door in a floor. The simple story set ‘in a cabin in the woods on an island in a lake’ is about a little boy ambivalent about being born. His mother, too, is uncertain, her concern based on a fear of what lies beneath a door in the cabin floor. Some children had once disappeared down the hole and the mother, upon entering it to look for them, had heard a terrible sound that turned her hair white. She saw ‘some things so horrible you can't imagine them’. The little boy ‘didn't know whether he wanted to be born into a world in which there was a door in the floor’. He took the chance, however, ‘and was born and was happy, and his mommy was happy again too’. Every day she warned him repeatedly not to open the door. But, ‘of course, he was only a little boy. If you were that little boy wouldn't you want to open the door in the floor?

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Self creation: Psychoanalytic therapy and the art of the possible by Frank Summers Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press, 2005. 288 p.

Ruth Imber

Ruth Imber

390 West End Ave, New York, New York, 10024, USA—rimberphd@aol.com

Ruth Imber  

Although analysts from different theoretical positions agree on its efficacy, there is still little consensus on why psychoanalysis is so often helpful, even transformative. Most of us now accept that ‘something more’ (PCSG, 1998), beyond interpretation and insight, is required for patients to change. Still, there is as yet no agreement as to what else is required. In this clear and well-written book, Frank Summers offers his ideas about what may be needed beyond intellectual insight to bring about change. Summers, a training and supervising analyst at the Chicago Institute for Psychoanalysis, has obviously thought hard about why so many patients remain stuck even when they understand fully the derivation of their problems. Several sections of his book derive from previously published material. With the exception of an occasional resulting redundancy, the volume is coherent and quite readable.

At the risk of overly simplifying his ideas on promoting change, Summers suggests that analysts may be excessively focused on the patient's past and the interpretation of the present in light of the patient's history. While he believes that valid interpretations are essential, Summers thinks analysts must also look to the future and help patients recognize latent goals and desires within themselves.

1157

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Spur und Umschrift. Die konstitutive Bedeutung von Erinnerung in der Psychoanalyse [Trace and transcription. The constitutive meaning of remembrance in psychoanalysis] by Ilka Quindeau Munich: Fink. 2004. 238 p.

Johann-Peter Haas

Johann-Peter Haas

395 Roermonderstrasse, D-52072 Aachen, Germany—Haas.ac@t-online.de

Johann-Peter Haas  

I

The author directs her thought to a subject that has been somewhat neglected in the psychoanalytic literature in recent decades: remembrance and memory. Working from a concept of remembrance that is consistently regarded as constructivist and intersubjective rather than historical, i.e. event based and concretistic, Quindeau seeks to establish its central function and significance both for the constitution of psychic material and for the human psychic structure. Drawing together sociological, psychoanalytic and humanities-based approaches, Quindeau attains deeper insights into individual and social remembrance processes. These ultimately lead to a theory of the constitution and socialization of remembrance that is based partly on a reconstruction of Freud's theory of remembrance and partly on the re-establishment of the seduction theory in a form modified by the author.

The author pursues three main lines of enquiry into the psychoanalytic theory of remembrance and memory.

The first trail leads the reader into a deep study of Freud's early writings, which contain isolated references to his theoretical and clinical views on remembering and forgetting. In his seduction theory, these are amplified into the most significant topos of the psychoanalytic model of remembrance. This demonstrates how far Freud's concept of remembrance had already moved between 1893 and 1897 from the prevailing contemporaneous image and theories of the memory as a storehouse: aetiological significance attaches not to the historical event as such but to its subsequent psychic working over in remembrance! On this basic premise, Freud constructed not only his model of the dual phase origination of trauma but also the extremely complex concept of ‘deferred action’ (Nachträglichkeit).

On the basis of the latter, the scientist pursues a second main trail of the psychoanalytic theory of remembrance, in which memories are conceived as permanent transcriptions of past experiences. The spatial metaphor of script appears with Freud in two different models of the memory that Quindeau seeks to link: the transcription model and the ‘mystic writing-pad’ model. She establishes that in both models bodily-physiological perceptions are recorded and encoded several times, at which phase-specific attributions of meaning take place retroactively as a constructive rather than a merely reproductive process of remembrance.

Transforming lives edited by Joseph Schachter New York, NY: Aronson. 2005. 192 p.

Brian Robertson

Brian Robertson

4999 Ste Catherine St W, Montréal, PQ, Canada H3Z 1T3—brian.robertson@mcgill.ca

Brian Robertson  

This book was conceived and written by the editor and the contributing psychoanalysts with two main aims in mind. First, to allow potential analysands to understand what they should expect if they decide to undergo psychoanalysis, and, second, to counteract the negative public image of psychoanalytic treatment.

The book opens with an introduction by Joseph Schachter in which he briefly discusses the present status of psychoanalysis and attempts to give a short answer to the question ‘what is psychoanalysis?’ He also comments, ‘I have seen people's lives transformed by psychoanalysis.’ Clearly, Schachter shares the frustrations of all experienced psychoanalysts faced by two incompatible facts. We have all seen the lives of our patients transformed by their psychoanalyses. At the same time, we have endured the minor and major attacks on the value of our work that is part of the current culture, at least in North America.

[[RunningHead]]

Resistance as a Response to Trauma in the Clinical Moment

The Approaches of a London Kleinian and American Ego-Psychologist1

Maria Escosteguy Carneiro , João Escosteguy Carneiro and Felisa De Fisch

Felisa De Fisch

Rua Eurico Cruz, #11, apto 602, 22461-200, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil—carneirojoao@alternex.com.br

Ayacucho 1639, 12 ‘A’, 1112 Buenos Aires, Argentina—ffisch@fibertel.com.ar

Felisa De Fisch  

Before presenting a Thursday session with Mrs B, her five times weekly patient of six years, Dr Sally Weintrobe related her feelings and thoughts regarding Mrs B and briefly reported what happened on the Wednesday session before the one she was about to report.

Mrs B is a bright woman in her early thirties. She can be very controlling and there is a tricky quality in her clinical material, as if she were a stage manager ready to tell the analyst what she wants her to do. There is also a remote, cold quality in her, resulting in a lack of contact with others. She can have clever insights, but doesn't elaborate them. She also has an implacable hatred of any form of authority. When interpretations are in any way painful, she feels ‘misunderstood’ and these sorts of feelings generally bring crisis into the analysis.

Recently, though, the analyst acknowledged that there may have been a shift: there is, according to the analyst, a sense that someone in her is trying to find a voice. The analyst feels that her patient is actually thinking more about herself. In her material, there are communications about how confused she is feeling. She seems like someone who has actual difficulties; this is a new aspect in the patient's mental functioning. Mrs B suffered early separations in childhood, and has a brother five years her senior. The analyst has the impression of Mrs B's mother as being rather anxious and narcissistic. She would leave Mrs B twice a year for six to eight weeks and, on one occasion, when Mrs B was 4, her mother went abroad with her husband for several months. Mrs B and her brother would then stay with the maternal grandparents.

Before the Wednesday session, Mrs B talked about her husband being fed up with her: ‘He said I think the world revolves around me—but there are others with feelings, too’.

On Wednesday, material came up about her going to the dentist after the analytical hour, and she spoke of being annoyed with a friend, Larry, who was coming to stay with Marie (his French wife) and their toddler, Henri, a 22 month-old boy.

responseSet.title: Time and History in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.authorMast: Sandra Pine and  Harold P. Blum
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Time and History in Psychoanalysis11

Sandra Pine and Harold Blum

Harold Blum

30 W 60th St, NY 10023, USA—sppine@att.net

23 The Hemlocks, Roslyn Estates, NY 11576, USA—ljw@cs.com

Harold Blum  

When addressing a topic so all embracing of human experience as time and history, the concept of relativity becomes most useful. The notion of things being fixed either in time, in memory, in an order, in a particular direction necessarily gets dispensed with. Human variables are ever changing. Our views of the past, of history, whether collective or individual can change in accordance with our subjectivity. The physical world itself is not a fixed entity, although we would like to believe it is.

Harold Blum in his introduction states, ‘Time is a creation of the human mind.’ It is created out of experiences of pleasure and pain, gratification and frustration. It may go fast or it may pass slowly. Past, present and future are concepts evolving from these experiences. We are victims of our senses though we adapt to clock time, the rotations of the earth, and the evolution of the present into the future. Psychoanalysis imposes a timed frame and termination is the first limit. Memory and fantasy manipulate our subjective sense of time. The notion that time is unidirectional is generally accepted. The unconscious on the other hand, is timeless. The unconscious past is not past as it persists into the present until it has been analyzed.

The first paper, given by Alberto C. Cabral, was entitled ‘From a past that condemns, to a history that allows’. Addressing Freud's statement that ‘Only nachträglich have become traumas’ (1895, p. 356) Cabral notes there is a distinction between differing translations of the Freudian concept of nachträglich. The Strachey definition differs from the Etcheverry in that it postulates a pre-existent cause that becomes known only after a later ‘moment’ in which the ‘deferred effect’ becomes explicable by association to the causal moment. This suggests a linear forward-moving conception of time. The second moment conjures up the first moment which is the original traumatic cause. The ‘before … holds the explanation of the after’.

The second translation of nachträglich, referred to as après-coup, postulates that a rewriting of the past occurs when new understanding leads to an altered comprehension of history. Thus, the future can constitute the past as a cause. In treatment, in reverse, these new signifiers become dislodged, allowing for a freeing from entrapment and repetition. Rather than a causal connection and linearity, there is realization, not reconstruction (Lacan).

responseSet.title: The Song of the Siren: Some Thoughts on Idealization and Creativity in Martinů's Julietta
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Song of the Siren

Some Thoughts on Idealization and Creativity in Martinů's Julietta

Debbie Hindle and Susie Godsil

Susie Godsil

20 Royal Terrace, Glasgow, G3 7NY, UK—kpross@tiscali.co.uk

1a Oakwood Lane, Leeds, LS8 2PZ, UK—susie@godsils.freeserve.co.uk
(Final version accepted 16 September 2005)

Susie Godsil  

Martinů, Bohuslav; Julietta; Segal, Hanna; idealization, creativity, aesthetic experience, psychic reality, mid-life crisis, psychotic processes

The authors draw on ideas posited by Segal in her seminal paper ‘A psychoanalytic approach to aesthetics’ to explore idealization and the creative endeavour as it is imaginatively portrayed in the Poutney/Lazaridis production of Martinů's Julietta, performed by Opera North (2003). The opera is set in a dreamlike world in which confusion proliferates, and Julietta is portrayed as a modern-day siren, luring Mischa, the main character, on to the rocks of psychic withdrawal. It depicts an inner drama where music, narrative and spectacle work together to evoke the main character's struggle which touches on universal themes—the ongoing nature of infantile longings, the pain of loneliness and separation and the fear of madness. Through careful reading of the opera and consideration of Martinů's life, the authors reflect on the factors that may have shaped Martinů's relationship to this work, including his feelings towards his homeland, his mid-life crisis and the social and political turmoil of Europe at the time. The authors conclude by reflecting on the aesthetic experience of the opera and the way in which the music transforms the drama and seeks to extend our understanding of psychic truth.

[[RunningHead]]

Gender as soft assembly by Adrienne Harris Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press (Relational Perspectives Book Series, Vol. 25). 2005. 320 p.

Gerald Fogel

Gerald Fogel

2250 NW Flanders, Suite 300, Portland, OR 97210, USA—geraldfogel@comcast.net

Gerald Fogel  

This remarkable book is an original and creatively organized treasure trove of information for those seeking a scholarly and approachable synthesis of the important subjects of gender, developmental psychology, and relational theory and their significance for contemporary psychoanalysis. The author's depth of background and ability to synthesize the many complexities and controversies surrounding these subjects into a clear, unified psychoanalytic voice contributes substantially to the book's unique quality. Harris's primary target audience is clinical psychoanalysts, and lively and impressive clinical vignettes appear throughout the book. But her clinical sensibility and even-handed approach is a tangible presence, whatever her subject, so that practicing psychoanalysts will find all of the book experience-near.

Harris clarifies early on that hers is an interdisciplinary work, immersed in an important larger context. She wants her psychoanalytic readers to absorb a crucially important climate of opinion—new ways of thinking about dynamic processes that include the human mind, but also play out in all of human life and the natural world. Throughout her work, she particularly stresses the power and constraints of binary thinking and categorical reduction when it comes to human meaning and theory making. One useful and important theoretical approach that provides interesting perspectives on binaries is nonlinear dynamic systems theory, also known as chaos or complexity theory, and the author's rich use of this theory is also original. Complexity theory is of particular use in Harris's overall approach to gender issues, as well as to other important aspects of contemporary psychoanalysis, such as relational and identity issues, and growth and development. Complexity theory creates opportunities for integration among these areas.

1164

[[RunningHead]]

Beyond Transference, Countertransference, the Silences and the Opinion11

Paulo Marchon

Paulo Marchon

Rua João Carvalho 800 cj 102 (Aldeota), Fortaleza, Ceará 60125-070, Brazil—paulomarchon@fortalnet.com.br
(Final version accepted 3 August 2004)

Paulo Marchon  

transference, countertransference, silence, opinion, neutrality, third subject, interpretative action, truth, self-disclosure, supervision

The author examines several works of an intersubjectivist trend, as well as writings by Hanly, Cavell and Bion, defending many of the named psychoanalysts'viewpoints. These viewpoints are expressed in the search and the struggle for truth, recognizing, like Popper, that truth exists but that we cannot know with certainty whether and when we touch upon it, only that this endless effort merits a lifetime's work because it is the attempt at an encounter with ourselves—the true encounter. The author explains the criticisms by Green of Jacobs, and defends the maintenance of ‘a certain possible neutrality’ (Eizirik). He poses some questions with regard to Ogden's ‘third subject’, considering it, among other aspects, from the supervisory point of view, which may demonstrate the existence of ‘a certain possible objectivity’ of the emotional conflict. He develops some criticisms concerning silence as an interpretative action by Ogden, and summarizes two case histories. Both were unconsciously attempting to manipulate the analyst intensely—one of them to get the analyst to intervene in his love life, and the other to interrupt acting out.

responseSet.title: Death in the Afternoon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Death in the Afternoon

Susanne Chassay

Susanne Chassay

663 Harvard Ave, Menlo Park, CA 94025, USA—schassay@earthlink.net
(Final version accepted 1 August 2005)

Susanne Chassay  

suicide, violence, terrorism, death drive, absence, infertility, evil, destructiveness, creativity, theater

One afternoon, a patient who had been in three-times-weekly psychoanalytic psychotherapy for over five years with the author left the office after her session, drove down to the train tracks half a mile away, and sat down facing an oncoming train. Her suicide occurred a little over a year after 19 hijackers took over four passenger airplanes and flew two of them into the World Trade Center, and during the period of the massive buildup for a pre-emptive war with Iraq. This paper explores the very personal impact of these interlocking events of personal, political, and state-sponsored terrorism on the author. Interweaving the patient's and the author's personal struggle with the patient's overwhelming destructiveness—and how it ultimately failed—with the inability to stop the war despite the unprecedented mobilization of voices for peace across the world, it is a journey through the shattering impact of violence into the tentative discovery of a sustaining vision of hope. It explores the theme of terrorism, both personal and political, as a theater of violence designed to create maximum impact, and how fantasies of redemption, fueled and blinded by righteous certainty, can transmute into acts of breathtaking violence, and find justification in their own mad logic. Through the elaboration of the patient's story and its impact on the author, she offers a very personal attempt to understand and reckon with violence and destructiveness as manifested in these different forms, to grasp how openings of hope and new creative possibility are often followed by a violent regressive backlash, and to find a way to survive them without losing heart for the work.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Flaming Red: A Session with Agnese

Roberto Basile

Roberto Basile

Via Lecco 1/A, I-20124 Milano, Italy—robertobasile@yahoo.com

Roberto Basile  

Agnese is a young woman around the age of 35; she is pretty, self-confident and trendy. She has a degree in law, works for a big multinational corporation and has spent long periods abroad to study and work.

I remember that at our first meeting she arrived wearing a red anorak, a red scarf and a red crash helmet, and she was carrying a red handbag. She also had a bag from a fashion shop which had the label ‘MAD’ in big letters. At the door, soon after introducing herself, she said, ‘Today it's a beautiful day, but everything is so frozen outside!’ as if she wanted to underline the freezing emotional atmosphere in which she felt she was living. This whole profusion of red colour did not convey to me a sense of seduction but rather the feeling of the mighty eruption of a volcano.

When presenting her problems, she initially concentrated on physical symptoms. In particular, she was suffering from a sense of chest oppression and difficult breathing. Just a few minutes later, she moved on to speaking of her feeling that everything lacked authenticity. She felt emotionally detached and she derived a little experience of truth only from painting, an activity she had taken up recently. She said it was not her first therapy. She had been under treatment for some time before, but then she had suddenly interrupted it. She had been with an ‘analyst’ who was intelligent, but brusque (‘he felt like my father’, she confessed). The analyst did not speak much. He was extremely irritable and got angry when he saw that she did not show proof of changing quickly enough, for example, in breaking up with her boyfriend who, according to the therapist, was unsuitable for her. During a particularly stormy session, the therapist—irritated by her existential inactivity—had literally told her to ‘fuck off’. At that point, she had left him forever. She said she had always been surrounded by ‘intelligent’ people, but in the end these intelligent people were not giving much to her.

She related an experience of sexual harassment (mainly fondling, with no real sexual intercourse), of which she had been a victim as a child, at the hands of a distant relative who was a paedophile. She said that she had always thought that she had come out of that experience with no traces of trauma and, in fact, she had hardly ever spoken of this to anybody.

responseSet.title: ‘Serious Things to be Faced’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

‘Serious Things to be Faced’11

Claudia Frank

Claudia Frank

Schottstr. 108, D-70192 Stuttgart, Germany—Cl.Frank@t-online.de

Claudia Frank  

To be invited to participate in the section ‘The Analyst at Work’ as a commentator presents one with a fascinating and engaging task. As such, it was not without a certain trepidation that I looked forward to the clinical report of Roberto Basile. Would I be able to meet the challenge in an alive and meaningful way? After having actually read through the case presentation with its colourful description of a 35 year-old patient, its detailed accounts of the internal and external interactions between patient and analyst, accompanied by the analyst's reflections, I was struck by a discrepancy: if in the initial meeting the patient had, so it seemed to me, authentically cast on to the wall a dramatic ‘fresco’ in which eruptions and clashes hung in the air, so, in the session presented from the tenth month of the analysis, a ‘feeling of peace’ seemed to prevail and serious dangers were apparently not being felt. What has happened?

Attempting to place myself in the situation and imagine how I would have under-stood the patient's communications is the exciting opportunity that the IJP offers to all its participants: to see how well we are able to communicate our work—what we share and how we differ—within the confines of this specialized format in which one has only the written report which has been noted by various contributors to this column, as well as the Editors. I have the impression that I share important theo-retical points with Basile. For example, the importance of the initial meeting/first session, as well as the conviction that an historical reconstruction as such is not helpful if working through in the here and now cannot take place. At the same time, my view of the material clearly differs. Is this due, perhaps, to the different value I place on the patient's phantasies as well as to a different understanding of the countertransference?

It seemed to me that the key to my task lay in the patient's reminder that ‘there are still serious things to be faced’, which Basile took to be the ‘working programme’ for the hour. The task remains fraught and is typical of the work with such patients. In every hour, one needs the courage ‘to face the serious matters’ and runs the risk of misstepping. Keeping in mind the inevitability of the situation, I will present my reading of Basile's text, which I think of as a possible amplification of the dynamics he describes. In this still early analysis, there hasn't yet been room for an ‘extremely irritable’ object (cf. the characterization of the patient's previous therapist) with its unreasonable and exaggerated impulses, judgements, actions, etc., though it naturally finds ways to assert itself.

responseSet.title: All Holes are the Same: Emerging from the Confusion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

All Holes are the Same: Emerging from the Confusion

Valéria Clark Nunes

Valéria Clark Nunes

Av. Visconde de Pirajá, 547 - 1110 - Ipanema, 22410-003 Rio de Janeiro RJ, Brazil—valeriaclark@superig.com.br
(Final version accepted 21 October 2005)

Valéria Clark Nunes  

character formation, pathological organizations, masturbation, body orifices, projective identification, pseudomaturity, countertransference

The author describes a type of mental development in which the primitive libidinal value of faeces and urine is kept unaltered all through adulthood. In this instance, individuals harbour fantasies of projective identification with the internal parents, denying their real dependence, which leads to a pseudomaturity. These fantasies are usually accompanied by anal and genital masturbation and have intense aggressive content. Furthermore, there is a significant confusion among all body orifices, leading to an inability to distinguish the self from the object, the inner world from outside reality. Individuals with this type of development can only maintain limited object relations and have highly erotized excretory activities. Manipulation of faeces may occur. This structure works as a defence against breakdown, but may also provide pleasure as it gives the illusion of omnipotent control of the object, as typical in pathological organizations. The author presents clinical material and discusses the difficulties of the analysis, in which the patient tries to seduce the analyst into colluding with his mental functioning, primarily as a means of communication as well as a projection of his despair and his disbelief in the analyst's ability to help him.

responseSet.title: The healer's bent: Solitude and dialogue in the clinical encounter by James T. McLaughlin Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2005. 256 p. Relational Perspectives series, Vol. 30.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The healer's bent: Solitude and dialogue in the clinical encounter by James T. McLaughlin Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2005. 256 p. Relational Perspectives series, Vol. 30.

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

240 Halah Circle, Atlanta, GA 30328, USA—ralphroughton@aol.com

Ralph Roughton  

I have always thought of James T. McLaughlin as a gentle radical. It was not that he preached an ideology or sought to establish a dissident school. His way was simply to tend his garden or his woodworking shop, those mind-freeing activities that gave him the quietude for contemplating his daily experiences with patients and for exploring more fully what he came to call ‘the interplay of dyadic transferences.’

This habit of self-analytic reflection, with attention to his mistakes and his own role in clinical impasses, allowed McLaughlin to become a more engaged analyst. From that perspective, then, he began to write about these experiences with uncommon candor and to question the mid-century orthodoxy of the objective, all-knowing analyst he had been trained to be. Earlier than most, he embraced the notion that the analytic encounter involves two people in all of their affectively laden transferences.

My first encounter with his work was in McLaughlin (1973), which made the case for the non-reporting training analyst, a position that was being adopted by some institutes, but not all. Today, it seems so self-evidently destructive to the analytic process for a training analyst to have any influence on institute decisions about his analysand that we tend to forget that it was ever otherwise. And yet the J Am Psychoanal Assoc editors felt obliged to balance McLaughlin's argument with another article that defended the reporting policy (Calef and Weinshiel, 1973).

McLaughlin (1973) antedates by nearly a decade the earliest paper in this collection; but it shows how his concern with the analyst's influence on the delicate fabric of the analytic encounter led him to challenge established views. Always a bit ahead of the curve in changing clinical opinion, McLaughlin's prescient principles have now been tested by time and experience.

This book collects six of McLaughlin's more influential papers, published between 1981 and 1996, which are contextualized and explicated by an equal number of chapters of previously unpublished material, some newly written for this book. This retrospective look at McLaughlin's 50 year psychoanalytic career shows how much his work foreshadowed the evolution of psychoanalysis toward a more flexible inclusion of the two-person, relational, and intersubjective perspectives. The value of his contributions to these developments is attested by the publication of this book in the Relational Perspectives book series. However, it would indeed be a mistake to label Jim McLaughlin as affiliated with any perspective other than his own. His is a unique voice that should not be subsumed in a category, even when his work is fully compatible with that category.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Michelangelos Moses und Freuds “Wagstück”. Eine Collage [Michelangelo's Moses and Freud's ‘piece of audacity’. A collage] by Ilse Grubrich-Simitis Frankfurt-am-Main: Fischer. 2004. 132 p.

Michael Günter

Michael Günter

Osianderstr. 14, D-72076 Tübingen, Germany—michael.guenter@med.uni-tuebingen.de

Michael Günter  

The starting-point for this short book was an interdisciplinary conference on Michelangelo's Moses, held in October 2001 during the restoration work, since completed, on the tomb for Pope Julius II in the church of San Pietro in Vincoli. Ilse Grubrich-Simitis has developed her conference lecture into what she terms a ‘collage’ of psychoanalytic and art-historical lines of argument. Her chosen approach—closely linking the text with the extensive and extremely well-reproduced illustrations—is highly instructive and can be qualified as an overall success, although, in contrast to the author's line of argument, this reviewer found the placement of the captions at the end of the book somewhat cumbersome and distracting. In spite of its brevity, the author has been highly successful in concisely summarising and incorporating into her argument both the relevant art-historical literature—here we might have wished for a little more detail from time to time—and the psychoanalytic literature relating to Freud's (1914) essay. In this sense, the book is perhaps, in fact, less a collage than a thorough piece of interdisciplinary research. The author's psychoanalytic reflections are underpinned by a sound knowledge of the state of art-historical research.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.607

responseSet.title: From obstacle to ally: The evolution of psychoanalytic practice by Judith Hughes New York, NY: Brunner-Routledge. 2004. 247 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

From obstacle to ally: The evolution of psychoanalytic practice by Judith Hughes New York, NY: Brunner-Routledge. 2004. 247 p.

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

11849 W Olympic Blvd, Suite 202, Los Angeles, CA 90046, USA—jaguayophd@earthlink.net

Joseph Aguayo  

To chart the theoretical and clinical evolution of some of the leading psychoanalytic pioneers of the 20th century is no small undertaking. In her book, Judith Hughes has provided an introduction for those analysts and historians wishing to understand how Freud, Klein and Bion wrestled with understanding the problems of neurotic and psychotic disorders. She organizes her material more or less chronologically into chapters according to diagnostic categories like hysteria, obsessional neurosis, depression, paranoia and narcissism. The informing metaphor is ‘obstacles encountered, allies made.’ The new psychopathologies encountered in the 20th century were seen to be obstacles until they were transformed into new theories and treatments.

620

[[RunningHead]]

Child analysisThe presence of the therapist: Treating childhood trauma by Monica LanyadoHove: Brunner-Routledge. 2003. 160 p.

Infant-parent psychotherapy. A handbook by Stella Acquarone London: Karnac. 2004. 308 p.

Child analysis today edited by Luis Rodriguez de la Sierra London: Karnac (BPAS Psychoanalytic Ideas series). 2004. 115 p.

Angela Joyce

Angela Joyce

21 Maresfield Gardens, London, NW3 5SD, UK—angela.joyce1@btopenworld.com

Angela Joyce  

At first sight, to review these three books together is a daunting endeavour. The first is by a member of the UK Association of Child Psychotherapists (ACP) and focuses on treating childhood trauma; the second is also by an ACP member who has been one of the pioneers of direct work with infants and their parents in the UK; and the third is a small edited book of papers about child analysis. Although they all address psychoanalytic work with children, they are curiously different and as such reflect the complexity of the child psychoanalytic field in the UK today.

From an historical perspective, this field has several dimensions, not solely the vagaries of theoretical and personal politics that are so clearly described by Rodriguez de la Sierra. The great pioneers of this work: Melanie Klein, Anna Freud and Donald W. Winnicott, are well known to the psychoanalytic community worldwide, as are the traditions they wittingly or unwittingly spawned. These, until very recently, also provided the political structure through which the theoretical traditions were bequeathed to ongoing generations of psychoanalysts in the British Psychoanalytical Society (BPAS) and beyond. However, in UK child psychoanalytic work, institutional aspects have other meanings and influences, and indeed on the ongoing extension and creativity of the field. This is one of the most interesting aspects when reading these three books together: the view they give of where the creative edge is in psychoanalytic work with children today.

I use the phrase ‘psychoanalytic work with children’, not ‘the psychoanalysis of children’, to reflect the breadth of the perspective I believe needs to be taken in appraising the situation with regard to this group, at least in the UK. I do not know the situation very well in other countries. It might also be the case in relation to work with adults, but that is for another time perhaps.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hate and the ‘Jewish science’: Anti-Semitism, Nazism and psychoanalysis by Stephen Frosh Basingstoke: Palgrave Macmillan. 2005. 228 p.

Hans Reijzer

Hans Reijzer

Olympiaplein 7hs, NL-1077 CJ Amsterdam, Netherlands—reijzer@xs4all.nl

Hans Reijzer  

This is a book of more than one dimension. The author discusses the links of psychoanalysis to Jewishness with the help of three case studies, starting with Freud, against the background of his era. The author considers psychoanalysis to be one of the expressions of the Jewish mind, which, on the one hand, reflects Western civilization and, on the other, analyses the latter, revealing its irrational and intense underside. The suspicion that psychoanalysis is a Jewish affair has had far-reaching consequences. In the Nazi period, that label was murderously dangerous. The Nazi period is seen as the moment when the psychoanalytic movement was brutally faced with the requirement to defend or repudiate its Jewish identity. In this connection, Frosh refers to the collaborationist tendency in psychoanalysis at that time, and its own anti-Semitic dimension.

Frosh devotes three chapters to the Nazi era, the period during which psychoanalysis underwent a divestment of its Jewishness, with regard to content, persons, and radical ideology. He tells the reader precisely which individuals who participated in psychoanalytic work survived the period 1933-45, relating the history exhaustively and in considerable detail. He explains the differences and similarities in outlook between the two German psychoanalytic societies (Deutsche Psychoanalytische Gesellschaft and Deutsche Psychoanalytische Vereinigung)

1428

responseSet.title: Intersubjectivity, Recognition and the Problem of the Negative
responseSet.authorMast: Marion M. Oliner and  Werner Bohleber
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intersubjectivity, Recognition and the Problem of the Negative11

Marion Oliner and Werner Bohleber

Werner Bohleber

670 West End Av, #9F, New York, NY 10025, USA—marionoliner@earthlink.net

Kettenhofweg 62, Frankfurt am Main, Germany—Wbohleber@compuserve.com

Werner Bohleber  

In his encompassing introduction, Bohleber showed the changes in psychoanalytic thinking brought about by the intersubjective point of view. According to him, analysts began to conceive of analyst and patient as a subject-subject relationship structured more by a symmetry than a polar complementarity. This brought about a shift from the intrapsychic to the intersubjective, from subject-object to self and other, from complementarity to mutuality. Bohleber traced the influence of modern philosophy on this approach. He cited Jürgen Habermas and credits Axel Honneth with emphasizing the struggle for recognition which depends on the other. ‘Individuation does not take place in opposition to socialization, but in accordance with it, through mutual recognition.’ It does not, according to Bohleber, account for the destructive potential, nor the other's alien nature.

According to Bohleber, the controversies surrounding intersubjectivity focus on 1) the validity of empirical infant research; 2) the conflict aroused by alterity and the neglect of the presocial and the prereflexive self with its stance against socialization (Whitebook); 3) the innovative encounter with the other, as the other and as alien (Puget and Berenstein) and their distinction between an egocentered relationship to the object such as in transference that causes the object to be represented, and the encounter with actual other that constitutes a presentation. Bohleber summarized Hoffman's approach to both the other and the innovative aspects of psychoanalysis, which resides in the interplay between spontaneity and normal ritualized asymmetry of the analytic situation.

Hoffman presented ‘Sixteen principles of dialectical constructivism’. He stressed that his theory overlaps aspects of relational theory (points 1-8), but that he is critical of some theorists with supposedly relational leanings for their objectivism. At the heart of the eight principles overlapping with relational theory is the recognition of the importance of the analyst's personal contribution to the patient's experience. The analyst assumes responsibility for that contribution, recognizing that the meaning of his or her participation is not transparent even to him- or herself. An ethical issue is at stake here that transcends questions of technique and of effectiveness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis: A Long and Troubled Relationship

Jeffrey Golland

Jeffrey Golland

145 Fourth Avenue. New York, NY 10003, USA—jgolland@att.net
1 December 2005

Jeffrey Golland  

Dear Sirs,

Kenneth Eisold and I have disagreed before over the issue of psychotherapy vs. psychoanalysis. I see him as a ‘leveler’ and myself as a ‘sharpener’ (Gardner et al., 1959). Although this paper had nothing with which I would disagree, we retain different perspectives.

In this impressive work, Eisold (2005) views the issue from several angles not usually considered: historical, political, economic, scientific and international, to name the obvious ones. Unlike Fosshage (1997), who concluded that there is no difference in the process, a position I took pains to rebut (Golland, 1999)

269

responseSet.title: The Technique and Practice of Psychoanalysis, Vol 3: The training seminars of Ralph R. Greenson, MD Edited by Lee Jaffe Madison, CT: International University Press. 2004. 216 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Technique and Practice of Psychoanalysis, Vol 3: The training seminars of Ralph R. Greenson, MD Edited by Lee Jaffe Madison, CT: International University Press. 2004. 216 p.

Jerome Winer

Jerome Winer

122 S Michigan Ave, Chicago, IL 60603, USA—jwiner@psych.uic.edu

Jerome Winer  

Jacob Arlow admonished us that no science but psychoanalysis uses textbooks a century old. Can seminars on evaluation for psychoanalysis given nearly a half century ago be of value today when so much in the way we treat patients psychoanalytically has changed? The answer is yes and no. Were these transcribed classes Ralph Greenson taught in Los Angeles in 1959 presented without commentary, they would be of some but limited value. With Lee Jaffe's splendid annotations they are not only fun to read but also instructional. Who in contemporary psychoanalytic writing has the wit of Greenson? Alongside the more serious material exist gems such as how to do an assessment of suitability for military service in 3 minutes. How does one tactfully confront a patient with a drinking problem whose husband has called claiming she urinates in inappropriate places? If Greenson got a call from a new patient who said only, ‘This is Mrs. X,’ he would say, ‘This is Dr. Greenson.’ If there was only silence he would say, ‘Your move.’ Even abstruse concepts come through clearly in Greenson's inimitable, colloquial style.

The book exists because not only did Jaffe recognize the current relevance of the transcripts, but also Greenson's widow Hilde had archived the material along with the rest of Greenson's papers at the San Diego Institute. One wonders why the papers are in San Diego rather than Los Angeles. Is it because of Greenson's somewhat controversial reputation behind the scenes rather than his theoretical stance? Despite the inadequacies of recording techniques of the time, all of Greenson's words are verbatim. Rather than preparing an extensive index, Jaffe divided the seminars into sections similar to the format Greenson used in Volume I (1967), his book on technique, which remains unquestionably a classic. The headings are most engaging. For example ‘The initial interviews have to be therapeutic,’ ‘how do you establish an object relationship?,’ ‘You must be more than an observer,’ ‘You must permit your wish to help the patient,’ ‘What can trigger an acute psychotic break?’ ‘The special role of exhibitionism and voyeurism,’ ‘How do you appraise psychological mindedness?’ Much like a volume of poetry translated from another language into English, Greenson's and his students’ words are printed on one side and Jaffe's notes and comments are on the other. This method is far kinder to the reader than endnotes appearing at the conclusion of a chapter or the book. Importantly, Jaffe's notes offer more than clarification and background; they attempt to make Greenson's ideas relevant to contemporary practice of not only analysis but also short-term psychotherapy and psychopharmacology.

responseSet.title: Dor sem nome: pensar o sofrimento [Nameless pain: A reflection on suffering] by Manuela Fleming Porto: Afrontamento. 2003. 164 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dor sem nome: pensar o sofrimento [Nameless pain: A reflection on suffering] by Manuela Fleming Porto: Afrontamento. 2003. 164 p.

Paulo Favalli

Paulo Favalli

Rua Dr. Veridiano Farias, 154, Porto Alegre, Brazil—favalli@brturbo.com.br

Paulo Favalli  

Manuela Fleming, a member of the Portuguese Psychoanalytical Society, offers us a thorough and enticing examination of a subject, which, while a common enough presence in the daily practice of every psychoanalyst, has on its own been granted little attention in the specialized literature. The concept of mental pain itself is an elusive one. It can either mean the expression of an actual experience, or work as a metaphor for a particular emotional state, according to the various uses that different psychoanalysts give to it. The author proposes, in this work, to focus on pain as a specific mental phenomenon that warrants a relevant conceptual status within the theoretical body of psychoanalysis. To that purpose, she suggests a provisional definition of mental pain: ‘[It is] a limitrophe phenomenon, situated in the frontier between soma and psyche, a constellation of undefined feelings of longing, helplessness and distress, to which the subject is unable to ascribe words or representations’ (p. 142). Two aspects of this characterization, which she considers later in the book, are the contiguity of soma and psyche and the representability of emotional states.

Reflecting on the boundaries between physical and mental pain then leads to the far more comprehensive subject of the continuity versus discontinuity of psyche and soma. That pain is simultaneously a psychological and somatic phenomenon is generally accepted. It is essential, however, to make a distinction between the two levels and to identify the possible transformations of one into the other. The author cites Joyce McDougall (1980), who believes that distinction to be brought about by the ability, acquired by the human being in the course of his development, to represent his experiences in a symbolic way, that is, to fashion a linguistic code whereby affective and sensorial experiences can be named. César Botella, on the other hand, favours the idea of an oscillation, since the ego withdraws cathexis from the object and invests it in the injured body or in pain. According to him, ‘as the somatic noise increases, the psychic noise diminishes’ (2000, p. 151).

Time present and time past: Selected papers of Pearl King by Pearl King London: Karnac. 2005. 267 p.

John Keene

John Keene

134 Marshalswick La, St Albans, AL1 4XB, UK—john.keene@ntlworld.com

John Keene  

This author, at 87, is probably best known as the co-editor of The Freud-Klein Controversies 1941-45 (King and Steiner, 1991). A former president of the British Psychoanalytical Society she has contributed to significant developments in psychoanalytic education and organization in Britain and the IPA. In 1992, she was awarded the Sigourney Prize for Outstanding Contributions to Psychoanalysis. King's career as a writer came about following her own reflections on the psychoanalytic psychology of ageing, which is a major theme in this volume. However, for many who have worked with her there has been a striking omission—her contributions to clinical thinking have been hard to obtain in print.

responseSet.title: On: Sanity and Madness
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Sanity and Madness

Richard Alexander

Richard Alexander

436 N. Roxbury Dr., # 216, Beverly Hills, CA 90210, USA—RAlexan564@aol.com
20 September 2006

Richard Alexander  

Dear Sirs,

I was intrigued by Dr. Symington's view (2006) of the human mind and his utilization of a very original schema for the purpose of making distinctions between mental health and the kind of sicknesses we are apt to meet in our work. I was also pleased to note his idea regarding ‘the achievement of sanity’ being ‘a joint venture,’ a view that I wholly agree with and one consistent with recent infant research, e.g. that of Daniel Stern (1995, pp. 99-115). But when he speaks of this ‘emotional reality’ involving ‘something more than Bion's reverie’ (p. 1065) and then immediately, in the text of his paper, makes a reference to ideas that appear in Symington (2001) regarding the ‘contemplative act’, it appears to me that he may be being a bit misleading.

To begin with, and incidentally, Bion made references to ‘reverie’ in his first book, Learning from experience (1962, pp. 36, 37), not in Elements of psychoanalysis (1963), as Symington suggests. But the point I wish to make here is that there is a great deal of time/space between 1962 and 1978 (when Bion made his last contribution) for a continuous evolution of his mind to have taken place. Specifically, Bion further deepened and expanded his views which would take him far beyond ‘reverie’, a matter recognizable early by way of his commentary in Second thoughts where he makes the recommendation regarding the importance of the analyst being able to gain the capacity to ‘eschew memory and desire’ (1967b, p. 137), it being through this action that the analyst is able to achieve the very contemplative state of mind that Symington refers to, one applicable both to the analytic situation as well as to the mother and her baby. This, as Symington points out, involves ‘an act of emotional wonder’, the interference of which would obstruct the establishment of and the ‘joint venture’ (p. 1065).

Bion continued his investigation into the nature of this mental state and introduced the concept of ‘reverence and awe’ (1967a). This state of mind is further pursued in Attention and interpretation (1970)

responseSet.authorMast: Giorgio Zanocco,  Alessandra De Marchi and  Francesco Pozzi
responseSet.authorCitation: Zanocco, G., De Marchi, A. & Pozzi, F.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sensory Empathy and Enactment11

Giorgio Zanocco , Alessandra De Marchi and Francesco Pozzi

Francesco Pozzi

Via Archirola 33, I-41100 Modena, Italy—zanoccogiorgio@libero.it

Via Stradella 311, I-41100 Modena, Italy—alsdmr@tin.it

Via Stradella 311, I-41100 Modena, Italy—franzpoz@tin.it
(Final version accepted 28 December 2004)

Francesco Pozzi  

sensory empathy, kinesthetic empathy, empathic intuition, imitation, enactment

The authors propose the concept of sensory empathy which emerges through contact between analyst and patient as they get in touch with an area concerning the primary bond. This area is not so much based on thoughts and fantasies as it is on physical sensations. Sensory empathy has to do with that instrument described by Freud as pertaining to the unconscious of any human, which enables one person to interpret unconscious communications of another person. The authors link this concept to that of enactment precisely because the latter concerns unconscious, early elements that find in the act a first meaningful expression. It involves both analyst and patient. In other words, the authors wish to emphasize the importance of the analytical process maintaining contact with that immense field of human interaction that can be defined as primary sensory area and which becomes intertwined with the evolution of affects. Clinical examples are provided to clarify these hypotheses.

responseSet.title: Analytic Impasse and the Third: Clinical implications of intersubjectivity theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analytic Impasse and the Third

Clinical implications of intersubjectivity theory

Lewis Aron

Lewis Aron

78 Country Club Drive, Port Washington, NY 11050, USA—lewaron@psychoanalysis.net
(Final version accepted 11 August 2005)

Lewis Aron  

impasse, third, Benjamin, intersubjectivity, self-disclosure, complementarity, relational, mutuality

The author examines the notion of the third within contemporary intersubjectivity theory. He utilizes a variety of metaphors (the triangle, the seesaw, strange attractors, and the compass) in an effort to explain this often misunderstood concept in a clear and readily usable manner. An argument is made to the effect that intersubjectivity theory has direct implications for clinical practice, and that the notion of the third is particularly useful in understanding what happens in and in resolving clinical impasses and stalemates. Specifically, the author suggests that certain forms of self-disclosure are best understood as attempts to create a third point of reference, thus opening up psychic space for self-reflection and mentalization. He provides a clinical case as well as a number of briefer vignettes to illustrate the theoretical concepts and to suggest specific modifications of the psychoanalyst's stance that give the patient greater access to the inner workings of the analyst's mind. This introduces a third that facilitates the gradual transformation from relations of complementarity to relations of mutuality.

[[RunningHead]]

Sanity and Madness

Neville Symington

Neville Symington

PO Box 6087, Pymble, NSW 2073, Australia—nevillesymington@hotmail.com
(Final version accepted 24 November 2005)

Neville Symington  

sanity, madness

The author sets out a schema with some underlying principles aimed at illuminating the nature of madness, how it sabotages our freedom and the deepest reasons why patients want to be sane. He claims that this is not possible without revising Freud's pleasure principle and determinism and replacing it with desire for freedom as the dominant motivating principle.

responseSet.title: Further Reflections on the Impact of Clinical Writing on Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Further Reflections on the Impact of Clinical Writing on Patients

Allannah Furlong

Allannah Furlong

2100 Marlowe Avenue, #533, Montréal, Canada, QC - H4A 3L5—allannahfurlong@cs.com
(Final version accepted 15 September 2005)

Allannah Furlong  

confidentiality, clinical reporting, ethics

Though it is unlikely that instituting universal guidelines will ever be possible for patient approval of the analyst's use of clinical material outside of the treatment setting, the author offers some supplementary reflections to those already available in the literature. Broadly applied informed consent guidelines would increase the distortion that already exists in our clinical literature due to self-imposed restraints by writers. Moreover, the powerful irrational forces mobilized by consent in the dyad are not easily ‘held’ by traditional applicable legal categories. Metapsychological formulations of the intrapsychic and intersubjective impact of patient participation in the writing process on individual analytic dyads are needed. Notions of privacy protection, validation, dyadic co-construction, or writing-as-containment by a third as rationales for informed consent fail to encompass the transindividual and external sources of human identity and the ineradicable lack of unity in the unconscious. Nevertheless, theoretical affinity and preferred technique may be mediating factors in positive outcomes of the consent process. Some paradigms not only accommodate more comfortably but also actively seek the intersubjective repercussions of informed consent. As an alternative or complementary viewpoint, the author offers the hypothesis that the clinical ramifications of either disguise or consent are not exclusively, nor even necessarily, concerned with what patients read about themselves, but what they assess or intuit—directly or indirectly through the material presented—of their analyst's unconscious strivings. To truly triangulate the clinical reporting project, it is wisest to consult the third ear of a colleague to assess the potential impact on patients on what might be being unconsciously transmitted by the analyst in the writing and the consent process.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical Implications of Bion's Thought

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

Via Cardano 77, I-27100 Pavia, Italy—hmdfe@tin.it
(Final version accepted 2 February 2006)

Antonino Ferro  

waking dream thought, Bion, projective identification, reverie, α-function, narrative derivatives, bipersonal field, interpretations, transformations, pictograms

The author focuses on some clinical implications of Bion's thought. He takes into particular consideration the great technical importance of Bion's concept of ‘waking dream thought’. He also proposes some developments of Bion's thought. Psychoanalytic exercises like those suggested by Bion are presented along with clinical material in order to render the author's theoretical-technical ideas as clear as possible. Furthermore, he deals with the fertile connection between Bion's theories and the field concept in psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: Seeing and Being Seen: Narcissistic Pride and Narcissistic Humiliation
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Seeing and Being Seen

Narcissistic Pride and Narcissistic Humiliation

John Steiner

John Steiner

28 Park Drive, London, NW11 7SP, UK—john@jsteiner.co.uk
(Final version accepted 24 November 2005)

John Steiner  

narcissistic pride, narcissistic humiliation, seeing, being seen, humiliation, shame, narcissism

Seeing and being seen are important aspects of narcissism, where self-consciousness is always a feature, and one which becomes acute when a patient loses the protection of a narcissistic relationship and is obliged to tolerate a degree of separateness. Having felt hidden and protected, he now feels conspicuous and exposed to a gaze which makes him vulnerable to humiliation. This often has a devastating and unbearable quality to it, particularly when it is felt to arise in retaliation to the patient's own use of gaze to establish a superiority which allowed the patient to look down on others. The need to avoid or cut short such humiliation may be so acute that the patient cannot deal with guilt and other emotions connected with loss which might otherwise be bearable. The author argues that development is impeded unless the patient is able to gain support to make the humiliation better understood and hence better tolerated. He describes some sessions from an analysis to illustrate how, in some analytic situations, much of the patient's concern and many of his defensive manoeuvres aim to reduce or to reverse experiences of humiliation. An understanding of the mechanisms involved seemed to enable some development to proceed.

responseSet.title: Mentalization, Insightfulness, and Therapeutic Action: The importance of mental organization
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mentalization, Insightfulness, and Therapeutic Action

The importance of mental organization

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

4180 La Jolla Village Drive, Suite 550B, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA—mumford2@cox.net
(Final version accepted 20 September 2005)

Alan Sugarman  

mentalization, insightfulness, action, enactment, procedural, self regulation, affect regulation, transference, countertransference, mental organization

Continuing debates over the relative importance of the role of interpretation leading to insight versus the relationship with the analyst as contributing to structural change are based on traditional definitions of insight as gaining knowledge of unconscious content. This definition inevitably privileges verbal interpretation as self-knowledge becomes equated with understanding the contents of the mind. It is suggested that a way out of this debate is to redefine insight as a process, one that is called insightfulness. This term builds on concepts such as mentalization, or theory of mind, and suggests that patients present with difficulties being able to fully mentalize. Awareness of repudiated content will usually accompany the attainment of insightfulness. But the point of insightfulness is to regain access to inhibited or repudiated mentalization, not to specific content, per se. Emphasizing the process of insightfulness integrates the importance of the relationship with the analyst with the facilitation of insightfulness. A variety of interventions help patients gain the capacity to reflect upon and become aware of the intricate workings of their minds, of which verbal interpretation is only one. For example, often it seems less important to focus on a particular conflict than to show interest in our patients' minds. Furthermore, analysands develop insightfulness by becoming interested in and observing our minds in action. Because the mind originates in bodily experience, mental functioning will always fluctuate between action modes of experiencing and expressing and verbal, symbolic modes. The analyst's role becomes making the patient aware of regressions to action modes, understanding the reasons for doing so, and subordinating this tendency to the verbal, symbolic mode. All mental functions work better and facilitate greater self-regulation when they work in abstract, symbolic ways. Psychopathology can be understood as failing to develop or losing the symbolic level of organization, either in circumscribed areas or more ubiquitously. And mutative action occurs through helping our patients attain or regain the symbolic level in regard to all mental functions. Such work is best accomplished in the transference. The concept of transference of defense is expanded to all mental structure, so that transference is seen as the interpersonalization of mental structure. That is, patients transfer their mental structure, including their various levels of mentalizing, into the analytic interaction. The analyst observes all levels of the patient's mental functioning and intervenes to raise them to a symbolic one. At times, this will require action interpretations, allowing oneself to be pulled into an enactment with the patient that is then reprocessed at a verbal, symbolic level. Such actions are not corrective emotional experiences but are interpretations and confrontations of the patient's transferred mental organization at a level affectively and cognitively consistent with the level of communication. Nonetheless, the goal becomes raising the communication to a symbolic level as being able to reflect symbolically on all aspects of one's mind with a minimum of restriction is the greatest guarantee of mental health.

responseSet.title: Implicit Memory and Early Unrepressed Unconscious: Their Role in the Therapeutic Process (How the Neurosciences Can Contribute to Psychoanalysis)1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Implicit Memory and Early Unrepressed Unconscious

Their Role in the Therapeutic Process (How the Neurosciences Can Contribute to Psychoanalysis)1

Mauro Mancia

Mauro Mancia

Via S. Marta 13, I-20123 Milano, Italy—mauro.mancia@unimi.it
(Final version accepted 26 April 2005)

Mauro Mancia  

implicit memory, explicit memory, unrepressed unconscious, neuroscience and psychoanalysis, dynamic unconscious, transference, countertransference, dreams, construction, reconstruction

The author discusses memory from the point of view of the neurosciences and molecular biology, proposing an integration with the psychoanalytic theory of the unconscious. The discovery of the implicit memory has extended the concept of the unconscious and supports the hypothesis that this is where the emotional and affective—sometimes traumatic—presymbolic and preverbal experiences of the primary mother-infant relations are stored. They could form the ground structure of an early unrepressed unconscious nucleus of the self. Identifying the unconscious with the memory leads to a theory about its morpho-functional organization. The unrepressed unconscious can be brought to the surface in analysis through the ‘musical dimension'of the transference, characterized by the voice (its intonation and rhythm) and the prosody of the language. Dreams can symbolically transform presymbolic and preverbal experiences, so that they can be put into words and thought about even without their recollection. Dreams can also create images to fill the gap of the absence of representation which characterize the unrepressed unconscious. The description of a segment of analysis of a patient suffering from death anxiety provides a clinical illustration of the theories discussed. The interpretation of her voice and of the prosody of her language, besides the work on dreams, reproduced the emotional essence of the analysand's traumatic childhood experiences. This reconstruction enabled her to speak and think about them even without the actual recollection.

responseSet.title: Trauma and Depression
responseSet.authorMast: Bruce H. Sklarew and  Harold P. Blum
responseSet.authorCitation: Sklarew, B. H. & Blum, H. P.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Trauma and Depression11

Bruce Sklarew and Harold Blum

Harold Blum

5480 Wisconsin Av, 211, Chevy Chase, MD 20815, USA—brucesklarew@att.net

23 The Hemlocks, Roslyn Estates, NY 11576, USA—haroldpblum@cs.com

Harold Blum  

In his introduction, the moderator said that past trauma may predispose to depression, suicidal behavior, and substance abuse. Dysregulation, fragile self-esteem, somatization, and internalized aggression frequently occur after trauma. Mania, delinquency and antisocial behavior may mask depression. Traumatic loss of nurturing, protecting objects may lead to depression with helplessness and extreme pessimism.

Object loss, especially where the relationship has been ambivalent, often results in depression, especially in parental loss in childhood. Having suffered a tremendous blow to narcissism and self-esteem, the child is unable to effectively mourn. Depression in childhood may appear in derivative form in being overly serious, clinging, being a ‘cry baby’ or in somatic disturbance like eating disorders and insomnia. In children, such loss may arrest development at the time and level of the experience. Adult depression following trauma is primarily characterized by sadness and grief, helplessness and hopelessness depending on the severity of the loss.

Sudden loss of a significant other or part of the self may be more traumatic since there has been no preparation, no time for anticipatory defense and mourning.

There is no trauma without loss, and no loss that is not potentially traumatic. Traumatic self or object loss impairs the sense of trust, safety, self-confidence, and self-reliance. The primary affect of trauma is anxiety, the primary affect of object loss is depression, but, clinically, the affects of loss and trauma often coalesce. There is usually a fantasy reunion with, as well as resentment toward, the lost object for no longer being available. Severe bodily damage and multiple object loss as in a train wreck or war may lead to a shattered self, and traumatic ego damage. Depression follows from aggregate losses of self, object, and the former object world and supportive social structure.

Caroline Garland said that, when there has been a clear external traumatic event, the unconscious hope is for the restoration of the pre-trauma self. Treatment involves mourning for that self and for the world view once held by it, as well as for the actual losses or the loss of limbs and capacities. When the trauma is of an early developmental kind, accumulating more gradually, it will have become embedded in the personality and its defenses, and can lead to unconscious re-enactments of situations that have not yet been able to be put into words.

responseSet.title: The Analyst's Excitement in the Analysis of Perversion
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Analyst's Excitement in the Analysis of Perversion

Stephen Purcell

Stephen Purcell

2116 Sutter St, San Francisco, CA 94115, USA—purcellsd@comcast.net
(Final version accepted 21 June 2005)

Stephen Purcell  

perversion, countertransference, perverse countertransference, transference perversion, narcissism, perverse transference, perverse resistance, excitement

The author believes that unconscious sexual excitement in the transference and countertransference is an especially problematic aspect of the analysis of perverse character pathology and that perverse sexual gratification deserves a more prominent position in the clinical theory of analyzing perversion than that which has been assigned tacitly through analysts’ routine focus on the defensive and destructive dynamics of perversion. He presents clinical material from the analysis of a perverse patient that illustrates the role of excitement in the transference perversion established in this analysis; and he asserts that gratifying perverse enactments occurring in the transference perversion can appear not only as conscious or unconscious excitement in the transference but also, at times most clearly, as the analyst's excitement. The author suggests that using a clinical theory that supports the analyst in understanding his excited responses as perverse countertransferences—i.e. evoked excitement complementary to the sexual component of a perverse transference—will assist him in locating and thinking about gratifying, perverse excitement in the transference where it is most usefully analyzed. Finally, he discusses some of the reasons why analysts might deny, suppress or otherwise avoid perverse countertransferences and in so doing contribute to sustaining perverse resistances.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Profession of Ferryman

Considerations on the Analyst's Internal Attitude in Consultation and in Referral1

Stefano Bolognini

Stefano Bolognini

Via dell'Abbadia 6, I-40122 Bologna, Italy—fef8279@iperbole.bologna.it
(Final version accepted 8 March 2005)

Stefano Bolognini  

consultation, referral, curiosity, concavity, convexity, interpsychic, aspirated vacuum, institutional transference, responsibility, oedipal mourning

Psychoanalytic consultation is an interview with a patient who will not be taken in treatment by the consultant analyst, but who will be sent to another, perhaps specifically chosen, colleague after one or more assessment sessions. This practice is becoming increasingly important since, besides traditional private relationships between consultant and patient, it is now common in many specialized centres affiliated to official psychoanalytical institutions. The author explores some fundamental aspects of the analyst's internal attitude in consultation: motivation to know; a ‘concave’ attitude to listening; responsibility in proposing suitable technical choices and referral to a further specialist; empathy and partial identification; the balance between authentic experience in the consultation and risks of seduction; internal links within the community of colleagues; the technique for sending patients on. He presents a clinical example, specifically aimed at reproducing the internal attitude of the analyst, together with the necessary work that allows a difficult patient to initiate psychoanalytic treatment.
