responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis with children and adolescents: Applications, settings and controversies by Alex Holder London: Karnac. 2005. 180 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis with children and adolescents: Applications, settings and controversies by Alex Holder London: Karnac. 2005. 180 p.

Deborah Steiner

Deborah Steiner

28 Park Drive, London, NW11 7SP, UK—deb@jsteiner.co.uk

Deborah Steiner  

This book is timely, reminding the psychoanalytic community that child analysis, meaning intensive treatment four or five times a week, is in the doldrums. This

1416

is particularly disappointing when, as reported in this book, the development of concepts and ideas in psychoanalysis was hugely enriched by the child work of both Anna Freud and Melanie Klein in the 1920s. At the end of the book, Holder concludes that there are glimmers of optimism for the future in an otherwise gloomy worldwide picture. One is the research work being done by Peter Fonagy and colleagues at the Anna Freud Centre, London, and the other is the establishment of a Child and Adolescent Psychoanalysis Committee by the IPA. The Child Analysis Training Committee (CATC) at the British Psychoanalytical Society is also trying to breathe some life into an area of work that is in danger of becoming moribund. One step that has been taken by CATC is to allow associate members to take a child or adolescent into treatment as part of the membership course. The response to this has so far been disappointing. Another is to create a forum, comprising colleagues from institutions like the Tavistock Clinic, the Anna Freud Centre, the Cassel Hospital, etc., where issues particularly relating to childhood disturbances, such as attention deficit hyperactivity disorder and problems specifically relating to adolescence, can be discussed. This has stimulated considerable interest but, nevertheless, it is still the case that there is not much enthusiasm for child analysis at the present time.

Holder's book begins with a clear and comprehensive foreword by Donald Campbell, himself an experienced child and adult analyst. The title suggests that the book is something of a history of child psychoanalysis and, in his preface, Holder speaks of his long-held dream of writing such a history. However, he goes on to say that the focus of the volume is not primarily a history but that he has several main aims, which are set out chapter by chapter:

responseSet.documentRef: Baudesson, D. (2006) Aux confins de l'identité [The frontiers of identity] by Michel de M'Uzan Paris: Gallimard (Connaissance de l'inconscient series). 2005. 165 p.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  87:1421-1424
responseSet.documentRefHTML: Baudesson, D. (2006) Aux confins de l'identité [The frontiers of identity] by Michel de M'Uzan Paris: Gallimard (Connaissance de l'inconscient series). 2005. 165 p.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 87:1421-1424

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2006 87 5 1421-1424 Aux confins de l'identité [The frontiers of identity] by Michel de M'Uzan Paris: Gallimard (Connaissance de l'inconscient series). 2005. 165 p. Dominique Baudesson1 29 rue Censier, F-75005 Paris, France—dbaudesson@noos.fr
responseSet.title: Aux confins de l'identité [The frontiers of identity] by Michel de M'Uzan Paris: Gallimard (Connaissance de l'inconscient series). 2005. 165 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aux confins de l'identité [The frontiers of identity] by Michel de M'Uzan Paris: Gallimard (Connaissance de l'inconscient series). 2005. 165 p.

Dominique Baudesson

Dominique Baudesson

29 rue Censier, F-75005 Paris, France—dbaudesson@noos.fr

Dominique Baudesson  

Michel de M'Uzan addressed the problem of identity as early as the 1960s, as shown by two previous books. De M'Uzan, (1977, 1994) placed an emphasis on the paradoxically but naturally uncertain nature of ego boundaries and the obscurities in demarcation lines between ego and non-ego, as demonstrated for example by the depersonalization phenomena that arise in the course of treatments. In this new collection, the author goes much further; he pursues this theme in greater depth and considers the modalities that govern the emergence and development of a subject's identity. His investigations arise from his experiences of treating not only psychoneuroses but also pathologies such as psychosis, addiction and psycho somatic complaints, and of supporting people suffering from terminal illnesses.

The problematization of demarcations in identity leads de M'Uzan to reconsider the classical opposition between self-preservation and sexuality. According to the author, self-preservation is, in fact, the expression of ‘a general programme for creation, development and preservation’ that is essentially genetic, which further implies that it is finite.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton.1421

responseSet.title: Response to Dr Riccardo Lombardi
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr Riccardo Lombardi

John Steiner

John Steiner

28 Park Drive, London, NW11 7SP, UK—john@jsteiner.co.uk
4 November 2006

John Steiner  

Dear Sirs,

I was pleased to read the letter from Dr Lombardi because it adds a dimension to the discussion of the material in my paper which complements the interpretations I made.

Certainly it seems evident and important that shame and humiliation are often associated with our perceptions of the body, and excretory and sexual functions are particularly vulnerable to feelings of being observed.

I look forward to acquainting myself with some of the research in this area which Dr Lombardi quotes and agree that it may open up a more primitive level of functioning which it would be important to understand.

1721

responseSet.title: The twin in the transference by Vivienne Lewin London: Whurr. 2004 234 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The twin in the transference by Vivienne Lewin London: Whurr. 2004234 p.

Jane Milton

Jane Milton

6 Narcissus Road, London, NW6 1TH, UK—jmilton@waitrose.com

Jane Milton  

Vivienne Lewin, the author of this book, is a London-based psychoanalytic psychotherapist. As a non-twin, she describes herself as an ‘outsider’, but ‘with an insider's perspective’ through her experiences as a transference twin in her psychoanalytic work. She has had a number of twins in therapy with her. She has also worked in a discussion group with mothers of twins and has clearly carried out a thorough survey of the (particularly analytic) literature on twins.

1728

The subject of twins, as Lewin reminds us, both fascinates and disturbs us. There is the uncanniness of the double and also what the author calls our ‘universal urge towards twinning’. By ‘twinning’, it emerges that the author means two different things. One is the common childhood fantasy of having a perfectly attuned companion; in exploring the origins of this, the author refers to Klein's (1963) idea of a ubiquitous internal loneliness, and an unsatisfied longing for a perfect understanding without words, that the primary relationship can never quite provide, or provide for long enough.

The other idea subsumed in Lewin's use of the word ‘twinning’ is an early infantile process which she describes as ‘the twinning process between infant and breast’. The author refers to the work of both Bion and Meltzer as suggesting that the breast is the infant's first imaginary twin, omnipotently created. She implies that this is a universal phenomenon.

Lewin asserts that these two processes (the formation of the imaginary companion and the formation of the ‘breast twin’) are very closely linked, and she uses the word ‘phantasy’ to describe both, although she acknowledges that the infantile one is unconscious while the later childhood phenomenon is not. The author did not entirely convince me that the two were similar enough to be subsumed under this same word ‘twinning’, which emerges as so central in the book. Surely they are rather different and variable phenomena, belonging to different stages of development?

responseSet.title: Endings and beginnings: On terminating psychotherapy and psychoanalysis by Herbert J. Schlesinger Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2005. 256 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Endings and beginnings: On terminating psychotherapy and psychoanalysis by Herbert J. Schlesinger Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. 2005. 256 p.

Peter Blos

Peter Blos

111 S. 4th Ave, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA—pjblos@umich.edu

Peter Blos  

‘Ending,’ Dr. Schlesinger asserts, ‘is what psychotherapy is all about’ (pp. xi, 23). In this book he explores in detail the end phase as it occurs in brief and long-term treatments including psychoanalysis, and the challenge posed to both the patient and the therapist. He reserves the familiar term ‘termination’ for particular aspects of the concluding psychological work.

By linking endings with beginnings in the title of his book, Dr. Schlesinger also makes the important point that they are not events separated in time, but rather inextricably intertwined phenomena. Thus, what transpires when a patient begins a psychotherapy is informed by endings in previous human relationships. The author notes further that the form and function of treatments' ending, as well as the associated difficulties, will often be discernible in the opening phase, or even in the evaluative interviews that precede it. For the therapist to be aware of these and other unconscious communications early on can be very useful to the treatment once it has begun and in facilitating its later progression toward a finite end.

The author is fond of using ordinary English words and elects to use those that are ‘more-or-less synonyms to particularize endings’ (p. 3). By this he means words such as ‘stop, finish, quit, flee, interrupt, discontinue, elope, drop out’ (p. 1). In this book they, and additional terms, are used to convey singular meaning.

1747

responseSet.title: Father Regression: Clinical Narratives and Theoretical Reflections1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Father Regression

Clinical Narratives and Theoretical Reflections1

Ruth Stein

Ruth Stein

355 E 72nd St, Suite 3AB, New York, NY 10021, USA—Ruthstein111@aol.com
(Final version accepted 17 March 2006)

Ruth Stein  

archaic, father, perversion, regression, religion, sacrifice, separation, son, submission

The author deals with love-hate enthrallment and submission to a primitive paternal object. This is a father-son relationship that extends through increasing degrees of ‘primitiveness’ or extremeness, and is illustrated through three different constellations that constitute a continuum. One pole of the continuum encompasses certain male patients who show a loving, de-individuated connection to a father experienced as trustworthy, soft, and in need of protection. Further along the continuum is the case of a transsexual patient whose analysis revealed an intense ‘God-transference’, a bondage to an idealized, feared, and ostensibly protective father-God introject. A great part of this patient's analysis consisted in a fierce struggle to liberate himself from this figure. The other end of the continuum is occupied by religious terrorists, who exemplify the most radical thralldom to a persecutory, godly object, a regressive submission that banishes woman and enthrones a cruel superego, and that ends in destruction and self-destruction. Psychoanalytic thinking has traditionally dealt with the oedipal father and recently with the nurturing father, but there is a gap in thinking about the phallic, archaic father, and his relations with his son(s). The author aims at filling this gap, at the same time as she also raises the very question of ‘What is a father?’ linking it with literary and religious themes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalytical Psychotherapy for/with Couples

Theoretical Basis and Clinical Utility1

Zulema De Forster and Miguel Spivacow

Miguel Spivacow

Rodríguez Peña 1641, 8°, 1021 Buenos Aires, Argentina—zuleforster@fibertel.com.ar

Ortiz de Acampo 2561, 9° ‘A’, 1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina—miguelspi@elsitio.net

Miguel Spivacow  

The objective of the panel was to discuss the characteristics of psychoanalytical psychotherapy for couples and with couples, its theoretical foundation and clinical utility.

One of the first topics to be covered related to the question of the need for therapy for couples or with couples, and to discuss what this type of treatment adds to the contribution of the classical Freudian model. What was proposed in response to this question was that the role of ‘the intersubjective’ will differ according to the type of psychic suffering, and that this dimension has a central importance as regards much of the suffering in a relationship as a couple and must have a place in the design of therapy. This then justifies the idea of a treatment specifically for couples.

This begs the question: what is the intersubjective dimension? It was proposed that all psychic functioning constitutes two faces of the same coin: one face is intrasubjective, in that the fundamental determinants come from the inner world, while the other face is intersubjective, in that the psychic determinants include the other (person) and the intersubjective context in which the subject functions. This intersubjective context and the ‘other’ are fundamental factors in much of the suffering which takes place in the love life and the relationship of the couple. The cases which are suited to treatment as a couple are therefore those where certain factors have a particular importance. These include: the partner; bidirectionality; unconscious interconnections; the interweaving of the phantasies of both partners (interphantasmic weft). In other words, we are dealing with problems in which the psychic determinant of the suffering must be found in an aspect of the functioning of the psyche which is not part of the Freudian psychic apparatus but which lies in the link between the members of the couple (the ‘intersubjective’).

Dr Moraes asked which concepts make up the basic, minimum toolbox of a therapist who works in couples therapy.

responseSet.title: On Nachträglichkeit: The Modernity of an Old Concept
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Nachträglichkeit: The Modernity of an Old Concept11

Friedrich-Wilhelm Eickhoff

Friedrich-Wilhelm Eickhoff

Engelfriedshalde 20, D-72076 Tübingen, Germany—fweickhoff@t-online.de
(Final version accepted 13 September 2005)

Friedrich-Wilhelm Eickhoff  

Nachträglichkeit, après-coup, deferred action, afterwardness, construction, reconstruction, fear of breakdown, implicit and explicit memory

Nachträglichkeit provides the memory, not the event, with traumatic significance and signifies a circular complementarity of both directions of time. Conceived by Freud as early as 1895 in the Project for a scientific psychology, the concept remains in his work without official status but through its character of biphasic development and latency indispensable for understanding temporal connections and psychic causality. As an implicit principle it is linked with the postponement and biphasic onset of sexual life … and retains its sometimes hidden importance until the late Moses study. Temporarily virtually forgotten, it was recalled to memory by Lacan in 1953. Translations into French as après-coup and into English as ‘deferred action’ emphasized the two vectors (retroactivity and after-effect) separately which are united in the substantive form coined by Freud. Unnoticed, it played a part in many aspects of clinical practice, especially in Winnicott's Fear of breakdown and the subsequent (nachträglich) working through of unconscious infantile and transgenerational conflicts. The author uses a clinical illustration to elucidate the belated understanding of the striving for non-existence in Winnicott's sense. Wolfgang Loch extended Freud's concept of Nachträglichkeit in a constructivist way, advocating an art of interpretation as an innovative enterprise through which connections are not only unmasked but also created, constituted by subsequent (nachträglich) reinterpretation of a subjective past. Very briefly the author refers to the interdisciplinary reception of the concept of Nachträglichkeit, especially in cultural studies.

The German Specimen Case, Amalia X: Empirical Studies

Horst Kächele , Cornelia Albani , Anna Buchheim , Michael Hölzer , Roderich Hohage , Erhard Mergenthaler , Juan Jiménez , Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber , Lisbeth Neudert-Dreyer , Dan Pokorny and Helmut Thomä

Ulm, Germany

horst.kaechele@uni-ulm.de

Universitäts- und Poliklinik für Psychotherapie und Psychosomatik, Universität Leipzig, Karl-Tauchnitzstr. 25, D-04107 Leipzig, Germany

Departamento de Psiquiatría y Salud Mental, Universidad de Chile, Av. Salvador 486, Santiago, Chile—jjimenez@med.uchile.cl

Sigmund Freud Institut, Myliusstrasse 20, D-60323 Frankfurt-am-Main, Germany—m.leuzinger-bohleber@Frankfurt.netsurf.de

Psychoanalytische Arbeitsgemeinschaft Ulm, Am Hochsträß 8, D-89081 Ulm, Germany—neudert.dreyer@t-online.de

Funkenburgstr. 14, D-04105 Leipzig, Germany—ThomaeLeipzig@t-online.de
(Final version accepted 16 September 2005)

Helmut Thomä  

single case, process research, evaluation

The authors provide a perspective on how psychoanalytic process research can be implemented. This is based on a process research model described elsewhere and summarizes the kinds of studies that can be situated on the four levels of the model. The authors summarize multiple empirical studies that were performed in a completely tape-recorded psychoanalytic therapy and have been published. These studies demonstrate the many modalities empirical process research has available to objectively study psychoanalytic process phenomena and their implication for outcome.

responseSet.title: Reading Loewald: Oedipus Reconceived
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Loewald: Oedipus Reconceived

Thomas Ogden

Thomas Ogden

306 Laurel St, San Francisco, CA 94118, USA
(Final version accepted 9 August 2005)

Thomas Ogden  

Oedipus complex, Loewald, succession of generations, emancipation, individuation, atonement, superego formation, incestuous object relations, close reading

Loewald's ‘Waning of the Oedipus complex’ is a watershed paper in the history of psychoanalytic thought. By means of a close reading of Loewald's paper, the author frames, discusses and clinically illustrates his understanding of Loewald's reconceptualization of the Oedipus complex. The principal elements of Loewald's reformulation include: 1) the idea that the tension between the pressures of parental influence and the child's innate need to establish his own capacities for originality lies at the core of the Oedipus complex; 2) the notion that oedipal parricide is driven, most fundamentally, by the child's ‘urge for emancipation.’ Parricide involves a revolt against, and an appropriation of, parental authority; 3) the idea that the child atones for the act of parricide by internalizing a transformed version of the child's experience of the oedipal parents. This results in an alteration of the very structure of the child's self (i.e. in the formation of the superego as the agency of autonomy and responsibility); 4) the notion that, in the child's appropriation of parental authority, he in reality ‘kill[s] something vital in them … [thus] contributing to their dying’ and to the succession of generations; and 5) the idea that the incestuous component of the Oedipus complex involves, in health, the creation of a transitional incestuous object relationship which, over the course of one's life, mediates the interplay between undifferentiated and differentiated aspects of self and relatedness to others. The author concludes with a comparison of Freud's and Loewald's conceptions of the Oedipus complex.

responseSet.title: Julie's Museum: The Evolution of Thinking, Dreaming and Historicization in the Treatment of Traumatized Patients
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Julie's Museum

The Evolution of Thinking, Dreaming and Historicization in the Treatment of Traumatized Patients

Lawrence Brown

Lawrence Brown

37 Homer St, Newton Center, MA 02459, USA—larry.brown8@comcast.net
(Final version accepted 25 April 2006)

Lawrence Brown  

trauma, Bion, α-function, β-screen, psychoanalytic technique, traumatic dreams, historicization

The author contends that, following Freud, trauma may be viewed as a disruption of the ego's ‘protective shield’ and that a central factor of this shield is an internalized relationship to a thinking-containing mother. Severe trauma destroy this inner connection, resulting in the reversal of α-function and the establishment of a rigid traumatic organization (β-screen) that brings coherence to the shattered psyche. However, this is an ‘organized chaos’ in which concrete forms of thinking predominate. The patient's ability to think, dream and imagine is significantly curtailed and she is consequently locked in a traumatic world from which she is unable to evolve. He offers a detailed case history to illustrate these points and the vital role the analyst's imaginative capacities play in the analysis of such individuals. Finally, he addresses the development of the capacity to represent the trauma, starting with primitive, often somatically encoded experiences, and evolving toward the capacity for historicization.

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Westchester Division, 21 Bloomingdale Road, White Plains, NY 10605, USA—okernber@med.cornell.edu
(Final version accepted 7 April 2006)

Otto Kernberg  

psychoanalytic education, training analysis, supervision, candidate selection, certification, analytic identity, authoritarianism, graduation requirements, Eitingon system, institute structure, paranoiagenesis

The author presents a critical overview of the literature on psychoanalytic education, focusing on criticism regarding structural aspects of our educational institutions. He then presents arguments for the need of radical changes in the organizational structure of institutes, and focuses on the problems of the training analysis system. He proposes concrete solutions for these problems, in the form of changes both in the assignment of responsibilities for the personal analysis of candidates and in the selection and function of supervisors.

responseSet.title: Letters to the Editors: On: Whose Bion?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Letters to the Editors

On: Whose Bion?

James Grotstein

James Grotstein

522 Dalehurst Ave, Los Angeles, CA 90024-2516, USA—jgrotstein@earthlink.net
17 February 2006

James Grotstein  

Dear Sirs,

I wish to compliment the Editors of the IJP for sponsoring a discussion on Bion in ‘Psychoanalytic Controversies’ in which a most important interchange took place (O'Shaughnessy, 2005; Ferro, 2005; Tabak de Bianchedi, 2005). Having been analyzed by Bion and having just completed a book about his work (Grotstein, in press), I naturally lean toward the views so admirably and scholarly expressed by Elizabeth de Bianchedi and Antonino Ferro, and subscribe to them without qualifications. Yet I am also sympathetic with much of Edna O'Shaughnessy's views. Bion was dense and enigmatic in his writing and his utterances and thus frustrating for many to comprehend. I have come to believe that his enigmatic style of presentation may possibly have been, in part, an offshoot of one of his greatest gifts to psychoanalysis, his radical conception of dreaming (by day and by night) along with his immersion in poetics. Perhaps what we read in and hear from Bion is the product of his poetic way of dreaming his ideas and his responses to us.

O'Shaughnessy is troubled by his having become less disciplined after the 1960s. I was in analysis with him in the 1960s and 70s and never found a more disciplined analyst or man in my life than he. I found him, as a matter of fact, to be a very disciplined Kleinian in his interventions with me. Moreover, I believe that it is important to note that the discipline that it takes to follow Bion's technique, i.e. the abandonment (suspension) of memory, desire, understanding, and preconception, is more strenuous and demanding than that required by other standard techniques, including Kleinian.

I think what seems to bother O'Shaughnessy are what appear to be contradictions in his writing. Were they contradictions or were they bringing opposites (as in the unconscious) together in a new way? She approves of K but not of O. H, L, and K constitute a troika of drives which Bion reconfigured as a troika of emotions, actually, emotional linkages between objects. O was his way of dealing with a new psychoanalytic paradigm. With Kleinians as well as Freudians, drives have constituted first cause, i.e. positivism. Bion subordinates them to the concept, resonant with relativity theory and the uncertainty principle, that first cause lies beyond the drives and implicates O, the absolute truth about an ultimate reality which is infinite, uncertain (chaotic), and always unknowable but which inexorably intersects our finite emotional frontiers. Since Kleinian and Freudian theories and techniques are positivistic and since the drives are held to be first cause, there may be a tendency for their techniques to become formulaic. Bion propounded that the analyst's interpretation should be spontaneous and always a surprise to both the analyst and the analysand. Put another way, truth and unknowable reality, more than the drives, constitute the significant content of the repressed (Grotstein, 2004).

responseSet.title: After Pluralism: Towards a New, Integrated Psychoanalytic Paradigm1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

After Pluralism

Towards a New, Integrated Psychoanalytic Paradigm1

Juan Jiménez

Juan Jiménez

Av. Salvador 486, Santiago, Chile—jjimenez@med.uchile.cl
(Final version accepted 23 June 2005)

Juan Jiménez  

pluralism, neurosciences, empirical research, crisis of psychoanalysis, epistemology, relational psychoanalysis, theory of drives

After a restatement of the isolationism of psychoanalysis from allied disciplines, and an examination of some of the reasons for the diversity of schools of thought and the fragmentation of psychoanalytic knowledge, the author suggests the need to adopt principles of correspondence or external coherence along with those of hermeneutic coherence to validate psychoanalytic hypotheses. Recent developments in neurocognitive science have come to the aid of psychoanalysis in this period of crisis, resulting in the proposition of integrating both areas to form a new paradigm for the construction of the theory of the mind. This emerging paradigm tries to integrate clinical knowledge with neurocognitive science, findings from studies on the process and outcome of psychotherapy, research into the early mother-infant relationship, and developmental psychopathology. The author examines theoretical-technical models based on the concept of drives and of relationships in the light of interdisciplinary findings. He concludes that the relational model has a broad empirical base, except when the concept of drives is discredited. Interdisciplinary findings have led to the positing of the replacement of the Freudian model of drives with a model of motivational systems centred on affective processes. He draws certain conclusions which have a bearing on the technique of psychoanalytic treatment. These arise from the adoption of the new integrated paradigm.

responseSet.title: What is Conceptual Research in Psychoanalysis?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

What is Conceptual Research in Psychoanalysis?11

Marianne Leuzinger-Bohleber and Tamara Fischmann

Tamara Fischmann

Am Ebelfeld 1A, D-60488 Frankfurt-am-Main, Germany—SFI-M.Leuzinger-Bohleber@t-online.de

Myliusstr. 20, D-60323 Frankfurt—SFI-T.Fischmann@t-online.de
(Final version accepted 11 June 2005)

Tamara Fischmann   In co-operation with the RESEARCH SUBCOMMITTEE FOR CONCEPTUAL RESEARCH OF THE INTERNATIONAL PSYCHOANALYTICAL ASSOCIATION3
conceptual research, clinical research, extraclinical research, empirical research, interdisciplinary research, philosophy of science, epistemology, research quality criteria

The development of psychoanalysis as a science and clinical practice has always relied heavily on various forms of conceptual research. Thus, conceptual research has clarified, formulated and reformulated psychoanalytic concepts permitting to better shape the findings emerging in the clinical setting. By enhancing clarity and explicitness in concept usage it has facilitated the integration of existing psychoanalytic thinking as well as the development of new ways of looking at clinical and extraclinical data. Moreover, it has offered conceptual bridges to neighbouring disciplines particularly interested in psychoanalysis, e.g. philosophy, sociology, aesthetics, history of art and literature, and more recently cognitive science/neuroscience. In the present phase of psychoanalytic pluralism, of worldwide scientific communication among psychoanalysts irrespective of language differences and furthermore of an intensifying dialogue with other disciplines, the relevance of conceptual research is steadily increasing. Yet, it still often seems insufficiently clear how conceptual research can be differentiated from clinical and empirical research in psychoanalysis. Therefore, the Subcommittee for Conceptual Research of the IPA presents some of its considerations on the similarities and the differences between various forms of clinical and extraclinical research, their specific aims, quality criteria and thus their specific chances as well as their specific limitations in this paper. Examples taken from six issues of the International Journal of Psychoanalysis in 2002-3 serve as illustrations for seven different subtypes of conceptual research.

responseSet.title: Art and Emotion in Psychoanalysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Art and Emotion in Psychoanalysis

Elliot Jurist

Elliot Jurist

138th St/Convent Av, NAC Building, Room 8/109, New York, NY 10031, USA—ejurist@gc.cuny.edu
(Final version accepted 8 May 2006)

Elliot Jurist  

art and emotion, aesthetics, affects, affect theory, affect regulation, mentalized affectivity

Freud's view that art satisfies psychic needs has been taken to mean that art has its source in the unconscious and that it unifies pleasure and reality. The author argues that there is a third point that Freud repeatedly emphasizes, which should not be overlooked, that art influences our emotions. The author examines what Freud means by this claim, in particular, his reading of Michelangelo's Moses. Freud's focus here on emotions as fundamental to subjective experience, as subject to regulation and as potentially healthy forms of communication serves to supplement and even challenge what he says in his theory of affect. The author concludes by making inferences about a contemporary psychoanalytic theory of affects: that it ought to be inclusive of science (more receptive to neurobiology and less bound to Freud) as well as art (preserving the focus on subjective experience, especially the processing of complex emotions), which is illustrated with the concept of mentalized affectivity.

responseSet.title: Constructing the Truth: From ‘Confusion of Tongues’ to ‘Constructions in Analysis’1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Constructing the Truth

From ‘Confusion of Tongues’ to ‘Constructions in Analysis’1

Jacques Press

Jacques Press

62, Quai Gustave Ador, CH-1207 Genève, Switzerland—mjpress@vtxnet.ch
(Final version accepted 22 March 2005)

Jacques Press  

S. Freud, S. Ferenczi, J. Assmann, Moses, trauma, self-analysis, construction, material and historical truth

The author hypothesizes that the papers Freud wrote in the period 1934-9 constitute a final turning point in his work resulting from an attempt to work through, by means of self-analysis, early traumatic elements reactivated by the conditions of his life in the 1930s. The author emphasizes that the ups and downs of Freud's relationship with Sándor Ferenczi and the mourning which followed his death in 1933 played an important role in this traumatic situation. He suggests that through these last works, Freud pursued a posthumous dialogue with Ferenczi. This working through led Freud, in Moses and monotheism, to an ultimate revision of his theory of trauma, a revision which the author examines in full, in the light of the works of the Egyptologist, Jan Assmann. A new analytical paradigm emerges: that of constructions in analysis developed in the article of the same name. The activity of construction appears as an alternative to the mutual analysis proposed by Ferenczi and is closely bound up with the notion of historical truth. In psychoanalysis, it would mean constructing a historical truth whose anchoring in the material truth of the past is essential, though it should not be confused with it.

responseSet.title: Vann E. Spruiell (1926-2005)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vann E. Spruiell (1926-2005)

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel

245 E 87th St, New York, NY 10128, USA—shelleyorgel@aol.com

Shelley Orgel  

Vann E. Spruiell, IJP's Editor for North America from 1988 to 1993, died on 18 August at the age of 78. He had been suffering from Alzheimer's disease for several years. His death came just 10 days before Hurricane Katrina destroyed much of the city he loved, and scattered the psychoanalytic community for which he had been an inspiring intellectual and organizational leader for half a century.

He was born in Leeds, Alabama, a small town in which his father was the pharmacist. He received a B.S. degree from the University of Alabama in 1948, and an M.D. degree from Harvard Medical School in 1952. Psychiatric residency was at New York Hospital, and psychoanalytic training at the New Orleans Psychoanalytic Institute from which he graduated in 1965. For the American Psychoanalytic Association, he was a member of most major committees of the Board on Professional Standards, and was its Secretary from 1979 to 1982. He was President of the New Orleans Psychoanalytic Institute from 1985 to 1989. Although he was always willing to accept positions of responsibility in psychoanalytic organizations, I don't think he felt truly at home in this aspect of professional work. Perhaps a primary need to be truthful rather than diplomatic hampered his effectiveness in navigating in the seas of the political maneuverings and arguments endemic to psychoanalytic organizations.

He was a superbNorth America editor for IJP, following the path and mentorship of his predecessor Edward M. Weinshel. He wrote masterly critiques of submitted papers which helped writers to build their work toward their highest possible level of creative achievement. He assembled a group of associate editors with whom he worked intimately and tirelessly. For me it was an inspiring privilege to belong to this team. The uninhibited exchanges of critiques and ideas that passed among us, which he encouraged and coordinated, stimulated all of us to think more clearly, constructively and imaginatively.

For several years in the 1990s, he worked with Stanley Goodman to develop a database containing more than 30,000 references to papers and reviews in the 12 major English-language psychoanalytic journals. He called this project JOURLIT and BookREV. Subsequently, he founded PACT, a non-profit corporation which would include the full text of the major journals on CD-ROM. This pioneering work, done mainly for the love of it, became a basis for the development of the now indispensable online and offline PEP services.

responseSet.title: The Origins of Bion's Work
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Origins of Bion's Work11

Paulo Sandler

Paulo Sandler

Rua Joinville 157, SP 04008-010 São Paulo, Brazil—sandler@uol.com.br
(Final version accepted 7 September 2004)

Paulo Sandler  

science, epistemology, history of ideas, Enlightenment, Romantic Movement

This study attempts to identify the scientific, philosophical and psychoanalytic origins of Bion's work, and includes an organization of these in a comprehensive and synthetic way with the help of a synoptic table. Investigation has revealed Bion's scientific orientation, fed by classical and modern authors—notably, Locke, Hume, Kant, Sylvester and Cayley, Poincaré, Heisenberg, and the German Romantics. Bion was able to rescue certain transcendent aspects of human, and also of Freudian, knowledge that had largely fallen into neglected obscurity. He made an original use of new verbal expressions related to the immaterial facts of psychic reality, the unconscious and the id. The method involves a search for counterparts in reality with two kinds of evidential source: some works and their authors have appeared ipsis litteris in Bion's work. In those situations where Bion does not cite the sources, the study has been able to establish connections with the lengthy marginal notes which Bion left in the texts of the books from his library.

responseSet.title: Children in Genocide: Extreme Traumatization and the ‘Affect Propeller’1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Children in Genocide

Extreme Traumatization and the ‘Affect Propeller’1

Suzanne Kaplan

Suzanne Kaplan

Tomtebogatan 17, S-113 39 Stockholm, Sweden—suzanne.kaplan@tele2.se
(Final version accepted 5 May 2005)

Suzanne Kaplan  

extreme traumatization, child survivors, affect regulation, memory, space creating, revenge

The author bases this paper on extensive research concerning children in genocide with a starting point in the Holocaust and in the genocide in Rwanda 1994. She demonstrates indicators for psychological phenomena concerning the child survivors’ affect regulating that appeared in life histories presented in videotaped in-depth interviews. The psychological phenomena concern experiences of persecution and ways of coming to terms with recurring memory images and affects. The interviews that have been analysed in detail form a basis for an emerging conceptual model about trauma- and generational-linking processes within each individual—the ‘affect propeller’. An overall conclusion from this study is that past traumatic experiences are recovered not as memories in the usual sense of the word, but as affects invading the present. Accordingly, affects seem to tell the story of the past traumatic experiences.

responseSet.title: Freud's free clinics: Psychoanalysis and social justice 1918-1938 by Elizabeth Ann Danto New York, NY: Columbia University Press. 2005. 348 p.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Freud's free clinics: Psychoanalysis and social justice 1918-1938 by Elizabeth Ann Danto New York, NY: Columbia University Press. 2005. 348 p.

Neil Altman

Neil Altman

127 W 79th St, #3, New York, NY 10024, USA—neilaltman@hotmail.com

Neil Altman  

This book tells two compelling psychoanalytic stories. The first concerns the free clinics set up by the European psychoanalytic societies in the early days of the movement. In Danto's narrative, Freud led the way when he wrote:

1408

It is possible to foresee that the conscience of society will awake and remind it that the poor man should have just as much right to assistance for his mind as he now has to the life-saving help offered by surgery; … institutions and out-patient clinics will be appointed, so that men … women … children … may be made capable … of efficient work. Such treatments will be free.

(1919, p. 167)

Between 1920 and 1938, analysts responded to Freud's call by instituting centers offering free treatment in 10 cities, including Vienna, Berlin, Budapest, and London, in seven countries. Danto puts this development in historical context. At the end of World War I, the Hapsburg Empire had fallen and Vienna was part of the democratic Weimar Republic. The government of the city of Vienna, ‘Red Vienna’ as it was called, was strongly committed to social services, including free health care, for all its citizens. There was a strong feminist movement, with women winning the right to vote. Freud, along with Max Eitingon, Sandor Ferenczi, Wilhelm Reich and Ernst Simmel among others, brought the generous spirit of the time into the psychoanalytic movement, starting free clinics including the Poliklinik in Berlin and the Ambulatorium in Vienna, where analysts, from the most junior to the most senior, took on at least one free case and/or dedicated a part of their income from private practice to the maintenance of the clinic. Even the most senior analysts saw one-fifth of their patients free, according to Danto. Sigmund Freud saw some of his own patients without charge.

Meanwhile, Anna Freud, Dorothy Burlingham, Erik Erikson, Peter Blos, August Aichhorn and others were bringing psychoanalytic principles into experiments in education, especially early childhood education, for the poor. Danto views this liberal spirit as part of a larger concern with public space in Vienna and Berlin at the time; she includes details about the architecture of the clinic buildings developed by Freud's son Ernst, the modernist interiors and the furniture. It was a time when there was a resistance to elitism, to relegation of the masses to second-class products and services.

responseSet.title: The Impulse to Infidelity and Oedipal Splitting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impulse to Infidelity and Oedipal Splitting

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs

Garden City, NY 11530, USA—josephs@adelphi.edu
(Final version accepted 28 June 2005)

Lawrence Josephs  

infidelity, oedipal splitting, primal scene, seduction and betrayal, oedipal conflict, defensive organization, identification with desired but unfaithful parent

Freud suggested that the child perceives parental intercourse as an act of infidelity by the desired but unfaithful parent. Parental sexual infidelity is felt to be a major narcissistic injury that gives rise to fantasies of revenge. A defensive organization arises to manage this trauma and its attendant revenge fantasies. That organization involves splitting of the desired parent into faithful and unfaithful parts, displacement of hostility on to the rival parent, and identification with the desired but unfaithful parent resulting in the impulse to infidelity. Romantic fantasies of escape and rescue from evil rivals provide guilt free ways of satisfying fantasies of oedipal revenge. In those fantasies the evil rival is turned into an injured third party who gets his or her just deserts as the romantic couple gets to live happily ever after. This defensive organization may embroil patients in complicated love triangles as adults for which they may seek treatment. Analyzing the repudiated narcissistic wound of parental infidelity and the disguised revenge fantasies that defend against that wound may provoke narcissistic rage towards the analyst as a moralistic, possessive, controlling, envious, and spoiling oedipal parent.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinical Concept of Analytic Process

A Conceptual Investigation1

Manuel Pires Dos Santos , Maria Tiellet Nunes and Lúcia Freitas Ceitlin

Lúcia Freitas Ceitlin

Rua João Abott, 319/405, 90460-210 Porto Alegre/ RS, Brazil—manuelps@sppa.org.br

Rua Vicente Fontoura, 2539/203, 90640-003, Porto Alegre/ RS, Brazil—tiellet@pucrs.br

Rua Mostardeiro 333/310, 90430-001, Porto Alegre/ RS, Brazil—lceitlin@zaz.com.br
(Final version accepted 17 October 2004)

Lúcia Freitas Ceitlin  

analytic process, analytic relationship, research, concept, theory

The authors discuss the term ‘analytic process’, confirming the variability of the meanings ascribed to it. Although all the psychoanalysts of this and previous times acknowledge the existence of something called analytic process, as well as its importance, it has not been possible up to now to establish a consensual definition of it. The definitions are not only numberless, they also contradict one another. The possible advantages to analytic theory and practice proceeding from a uniform concept are incontrovertible. A review of the subject in the psychoanalytic literature has been performed. A conceptual study concerning the term, carried out among members of a psychoanalytic society affiliated to the IPA is presented, no consensual conceptualization for the term analytic process having been found. The subjects referred to the term as deriving from a variety of elements, some in common, but there was no agreement regarding the elements themselves. There was consensus regarding the role of the analytical relationship in the process, considered fundamental, as well as that of the extent to which the individual life experiences in that relationship define the unique character acquired by the process within it.

responseSet.title: Reply to Dr Levenson
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Dr Levenson

John Steiner

John Steiner

John Steiner  

Dr Levenson's reaction to my paper on enactment gives me a chance to clarify my thinking, to correct some misunderstandings, and to register areas of agreement and disagreement between us. I begin with the premise that the psychoanalytic method, together with the analytic attitude which makes it possible, represents a unique opportunity to engage with another person, to explore the way their mind works, and to provide a setting in which the patient's mental capacities can grow and develop. This opportunity carries with it a responsibility to facilitate the special kind of attention which is peculiar to psychoanalysis, that is, one in which priority is given to understanding. Of course, this attitude has to be consistent with our duty to the patient and giving a primary place to understanding is only justified because of our belief that it is precisely this understanding which is of therapeutic value to the patient.

Freud's development of the psychoanalytic method, in particular the discovery of transference and resistance, helped us to recognize that the analyst has to become involved with his patient at a human level. His emotions have to be available and he has to be willing to observe and face uncomfortable truths about his patient, and also about himself. At the same time, my view is that the analyst has to keep his feelings under control; he must be involved and yet has also to stay separate; he has to fully engage in the relationship and also be able to observe and evaluate it.

Developments in our knowledge of projection, introjection and identification have further enhanced our understanding of the transference and have allowed us to view the patient-analyst relationship as truly interpersonal. Our interest and knowledge of the patient's mind, his internal world, the methods he uses to relate to people, his beliefs, feelings and thoughts is made possible by the fact that these are all externalized in the transference and affect the emotional reactions of the analyst. I see this extension into the interpersonal to be central to the work of Klein who saw the transference as resulting from the projection of internal objects and their relationships on to the analyst, and on to the setting. Bion (1962) added the concept of containment which involves refraining from action in order to give priority to understanding, which in turn allows the patient to reintroject projections which have been modified as a result of the analytic work. Heimann (1950) saw that the relationship between the patient's projective identification and the analyst's countertransference gives us an additional potential source of information about the patient, and Sandler (1976, 1977) and Joseph (1989, 2003) extended this approach by suggesting that internal object relations become actualized in the transference and again can provide useful information about the patient. When I wrote my paper, I thought this was generally understood and I did not anticipate that I would be seen to advocate the suppression of countertransference.

responseSet.title: Response to John Steiner
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to John Steiner

Edgar Levenson

Edgar Levenson

330 West 58th St #204, NYC NY 10019, USA—ealevensonmd@aol.com

Edgar Levenson  

I am very grateful to Dr. Gabbard for affording me this opportunity to respond to Dr. Steiner's stimulating and provocative paper on enactment. I come from an interpersonal perspective—heavily influenced by Sullivan, Fromm and Ferenczi—that has virtually since its inception been very invested in this issue.1 There is a hoary psychoanalytic joke that goes, ‘What is acting out?’ And the answer is, ‘Whatever makes the analyst nervous.’ It suggests how elusive and subjective these categories can be.

So, I must begin by indicating some puzzlement. Steiner seems to be conflating acting out, frame violations, countertransference and enactments, when he says that, ‘It is difficult to reconcile the idea that enactments can give helpful information about the patient and his relationships with the recognition that, in the process, boundaries may be violated and to a greater or lesser extent both the patient and the analytic work can be damaged.’ He continues, ‘Enactments by definition cross the boundary from thought to action and, unless they are recognized and regulated, can enter that grey area between normal technique, technical error, and unethical boundary violation’ (my italics).

I quote this in detail so that there can be no misunderstanding or misprision. This seems to put all failures of an analyst's neutrality on a continuum, a slippery slope whereon countertransference may open the floodgates to a major violation of the integrity of the therapy. Can Steiner mean this? In his opening sentence he references both Sandler and Joseph as supporting the idea that enactments are ‘recognized … [as] harmful to the … analytic process’—not sometimes harmful, one notes. In the very next sentence, he points out the very same two authors utilize enactments therapeutically. Is this not a contradiction?

I certainly agree about the importance of a clearly defined frame, a set of a priori rules that are designed to protect the analyst even more than the patient. Violations of the frame are, by my definition, acting out whether by the patient or by the analyst. In contrast, countertransference denotes the range of feelings the therapist is experiencing in the course of the therapy. We may differ significantly as to whether we would list all the therapist's reactions under the rubric of countertransference, or only those we consider called out by the patient. In other words, we might argue whether the therapist's reactions are a version of projective identification, or represent an authentic participation based on his/her own personality. We may also differ on tactical issues, but I will elaborate that later.

responseSet.title: On: Seeing and Being Seen
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Seeing and Being Seen

Riccardo Lombardi

Riccardo Lombardi

Via dei Fienaroli 36, I-00153 Roma, Italy—dr.riccardolombardi@libero.it
23 October 2006

Riccardo Lombardi  

Dear Sirs,

Having read John Steiner's article (2006), I should like to comment briefly on the clinical material, which, I feel, lends itself to being viewed from a different perspective: one which connects the theme of seeing, being seen and shame to the importance of the body in psychic development (Lombardi, 2002; 2003).

When Mr. A saw the loo door ajar and the toilet seat up, and later reported that he had peed, wondering the while whether the two were simultaneously thinking of each other, I would hypothesize that the patient was thus reflecting on having a body that pees, as something he had in common with the analyst. Reading this passage as a communication of the patient's discovery that he had a body, I would view it as a springboard for the entire subsequent evolution.

When the patient subsequently set about looking around, the analyst seemed to offer visibility to elements that characterized the patient's internal functioning. The detail that the papers were ‘more…than he had realized’ apparently referred to his perception of an inordinate clutter of thoughts in his own mind, keeping it, until then, in a state of saturation such that it prevented him from realizing that he had a body of his own. What left the patient ‘in a mess’ appears to have been his unawareness of his own corporeal existence, as a consequence of the lack of maternal reverie (Bion, 1962).

The motive power of clinical evolution here is the patient's discovery of his body, as well as his perception of the disharmony in his body-mind relationship (Ferrari, 2004), which had led him to obscure his own physical existence, or to deny it by means of shame (Lombardi, 2006). The intrusiveness to which Dr. Steiner refers at various points could be connected with the patient's tendency to present forms of physical excitation (unmodulated by thought) which are linked to his internal body-mind disharmony, rather than to a primarily relational event.

I am attempting here to bring out the importance of a more primitive level of functioning—body-mind integration in the presence of an analytic reverie—than the level connected with the recognition of otherness, to which Dr. Steiner's hypotheses refer. My concern to differentiate between these two levels is also linked to the risk of impasse which I maintain that his sort of interpretive approach may involve, when dealing with a patient who is more seriously ill and less determined to carry on with his evolution than Mr. A showed himself to be.

responseSet.title: When a Patient Commits Suicide: An Empirical Study of Psychoanalytic Clinicians
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When a Patient Commits Suicide

An Empirical Study of Psychoanalytic Clinicians

Jane Tillman

Jane Tillman

Austen Riggs Center, 25 Main St, Stockbridge, MA 01262, USA—Jane.Tillman@austenriggs.net
(Final version accepted 11 May 2005)

Jane Tillman  

suicide, research interview, professional identity

Twelve psychoanalysts/psychoanalytic psychotherapists who had a patient commit suicide while in treatment, or shortly after leaving treatment, were interviewed to gain a deeper understanding of the effects of patient suicide on the clinician. A phenomenological research interview was used to gather data, and a psychoanalytic lens was used to interpret the data, generating a descriptive account of the experience of having a patient commit suicide. Thematic analysis of the transcripts yielded eight common themes associated with the clinician's subjective experience of having a patient suicide. The eight themes may be broadly grouped into three general structures: I. Traumatic loss and grief; II. Interpersonal relationships; and III. Professional identity concerns.

responseSet.title: The Myths of Free Association and the Potentials of the Analytic Relationship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Myths of Free Association and the Potentials of the Analytic Relationship

Irwin Hoffman

Irwin Hoffman

55 E. Washington Street, Suite 1217, Chicago, IL 60602, USA—izhoffman@aol.com
(Final version accepted 7 June 2005)

Irwin Hoffman  

agency, alliance, co-construction, dialectical constructivism, dialectics, free association, free will, interpersonal influence, mutuality, recognition, resistance, responsibility

The author challenges the traditional and still prevalent view of ‘free association’, arguing that it entails three forms of denial (also formulated in terms of corresponding myths): 1) denial of the patient's free agency; 2) denial of the patient's and the analyst's interpersonal influence; and 3) denial of the patient's share of responsibility for coconstructing the analytic relationship. That responsibility includes some degree of consideration of the analyst's needs. Sometimes, the patient's good judgment to that end may be reflected in what is automatically and mistakenly reduced to a form of ‘resistance’. Attention to the patient's responsibility must be balanced against the effort to provide a uniquely safe environment for the patient's revealing of shame and anxiety-ridden feelings and attitudes. But the therapeutic action of psychoanalysis, ideally, includes the cultivation, through lived experience, of the dialectical interplay of self-expression, on the one hand, and caring relational engagement, on the other. Recognition of the patient's free agency does not preclude exploration of constraining structures laid down in the past. On the contrary, it deepens such exploration. At the same time, it opens the door to the possibility of explicit recognition, via challenge, criticism, or affirmation, of the patient's contributions to the analytic work.

responseSet.title: Silence: Cultural Function and Psychological Transformation in Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy1
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Silence

Cultural Function and Psychological Transformation in Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy1

Elsa Ronningstam

Elsa Ronningstam

115 Mill Street, Belmont, MA 02478, USA—ronningstam@email.com
(Final version accepted 31 January 2006)

Elsa Ronningstam  

silence, psychoanalysis, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, cross-cultural, transference, countertransference, narcissistic, change

Cultural experience of silence and individual vicissitudes between talking and being silent influence the way individuals form an alliance and pursue the analytic process. This is of relevance both for the patient and for the psychoanalyst/therapist. The author describes a patient, whose silent phase occurred in the fifth and sixth year of intensive psychoanalytic psychotherapy. She suggests that a) the silence functioned as a protection of a space for the core self and promoted inner transformation and psychological development; b) the silence involved a transference-countertransference matrix with projective identifications of the patient's internalized mother- and father-related objects that caused a tenuous balance between maintaining and erasing the relationship between the patient and the author; c) the silence phase was highly influenced by the author's own cultural background and what she brought into the relationship of tolerance of being silent in the presence of another, and understanding of the many complex functions of silence. During the silent phase the patient moved from simply describing and naming her affects and inner experiences or expressing them as somatic processes, to being able to internally access and verbally convey her own affects and experiences in the therapeutic alliance. This process involved both affect desomatization, affect differentiation, and affect verbalization.

responseSet.title: A Young Woman's Distress
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Young Woman's Distress11

Jean-Claude Rolland

Jean-Claude Rolland

45 rue de la République, F-69002 Lyon, France—jean.claude.rolland@wanadoo.fr

Jean-Claude Rolland  

No other initial encounter with a patient has made such an impression on me as this one did. At the time of the appointment that we had made by telephone, a young woman arrived, carrying a heavy, bulky rucksack, an old-fashioned one, the kind they used to make before the war. She kept what looked for all the world like a burden on her lap, wrapping her arms around it as though it were the most precious thing she possessed. F was not dressed in rags, but her sexless clothes gave the impression that she was a complete drop-out—to such an extent, indeed, that I wondered (although just for a moment or two) if she were a drug addict who might be much better off in hospital than in a psychoanalyst's consulting room. She sat down on the chair facing me and fell silent. I could not say that her silence surprised me because it simply mirrored her despair; her overall appearance and the unchanging nature of the expression on her face evoked the image of a pathetic kind of unveiling, thanks to which I had neither hesitation nor difficulty in understanding the enigmatic message she was communicating. Then tears began to dampen her expressionless face, which was corpse-like in its thinness. The end of the interview was approaching, and all we had shared was this silent presence. I said to her that I would like to meet her again, and asked if she wanted to make a new appointment then and there. She nodded. We agreed to meet the following week, same day, same time. I make that point because I wonder if, somewhere inside myself and without my being able to be fully aware of it, I had already, in my countertransference, decided to embark on an analysis with her.

In the following session, she began talking almost at once. She told me of her severe and life-threatening anorexia nervosa, which was the reason for her being hospitalized at that time in a psychiatric institution, and of her recurrent suicide attempts. She spoke of her twin sister and of the extremely powerful and exclusive relationship that existed between them; she added that she was studying biology in a grande école2 where she was working on her doctoral thesis. She mentioned also a recent attempt at psychotherapy that had not been successful. She added that she had no hope of recovery and that she was simply giving in to the requests made by her (female) psychiatrist, who was actively encouraging her to have psychotherapy. At that point, F again began to cry. I gradually came to realize that she could see, just as much as I could, that there was a contradiction inside her between her firstclass intellectual aptitudes that remained intact and the decline in her spiritual and physical wellbeing, which seemed almost to be malignant in its progression.

responseSet.title: On the Psychodynamics of Collecting
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Psychodynamics of Collecting11

Peter Subkowski

Peter Subkowski

Empter Weg 5, D-49152 Bad Essen, Germany—dr.subkowski.paracelsus@t-online.de
(Final version accepted 22 February 2005)

Peter Subkowski  

collecting, ego-syntonic, ego-dystonic, gender differences, sublimation, perversion, addiction, compulsion, messy syndrome, defence mechanisms

The urge to collect is a ubiquitous phenomenon which has anthropological, sociobiological and individual psychodynamic roots, but occurs far more frequently among men than women. The author examines the reasons for this gender difference and defines systematic collecting to distinguish it from addictive, obsessive and messy collecting, and from related phenomena such as perversion. The mode of collecting and choice of object are important indicators as to the unconscious psychodynamics of a collector and offer opportunity to describe his structural level. Collecting ranges across a broad spectrum, from an ego-syntonic integrated mode, i.e. sublimation, to a neurotic defence against pre-oedipal or oedipal traumas and conflicts. Alongside this drive-theoretical approach, object and Kleinian theory are also applied to the understanding of collecting. Collecting represents a specific form of object relating and way of handling primary loss trauma, which is different from addiction, compulsion, or perversion. Under certain circumstances collecting can also result in a successful Gestalt or way of life. The paper concludes with a case study showing how collecting develops from a pre-oedipal to a more integrated oedipal mode during the course of the analysis, which is reflected in changes in the transference.

responseSet.title: The Role of Pre-oedipal and Oedipal Factors in Psychic Life
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Role of Pre-oedipal and Oedipal Factors in Psychic Life11

Luis Kancyper

Luis Kancyper

Güemes 2963, 10°, C1425BKA Buenos Aires, Argentina—kancyper@uolsinectis.com.ar
(Final version accepted 22 April 2005)

Luis Kancyper  

Oedipus complex, fraternal complex, paternal complex, father-son symbiosis, analytic field, narcissism, identifications, generational confrontation, neurosis, guilt feeling, mourning

The Oedipus complex, a basic concept in Freudian theory, is an essential factor in the constitution of the human subject. It plays a key role in the structuring of the personality and in the orientation of desire. It is the oedipal triangular structure that precedes the pre-oedipal situation (in a logical, not chronological, order), and not vice versa. The oedipal structure exists before the infant's biological birth. It is present in the parents’ desires and identifications, which inexorably fall upon each subject. That is why the author believes that it is necessary to leave behind a solipsistic reading of the nuclear complex of neuroses—a reading that is based solely on Oedipus's drive nucleus—and take a joint and comprehensive view of Laius's and Jocasta's histories and traumatic experiences, which were invested in their son. Among these three vertices, a dynamic set of forces emerges whereby a basic, original unconscious field phantasy is created that bears a unique narrative and an invisible and hermetic web made of passions and beliefs, scandals and secrets. This phantasy gives shape to an unrepeatable oedipal structure in each subject, a structure that articulates with the effects of the narcissistic and fraternal dynamic and may determine the subject's fate. This paper develops the following issues: 1) Oedipus, victimizer or victim?; 2) the generational confrontation as dynamic field; and 3) neuroses with a preponderance of dualistic relationships.

responseSet.title: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder and Trauma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder and Trauma11

Alan Sugarman

Alan Sugarman

4180 La Jolla Village Drive, Suite 550B, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA—Mumford2@cox.net

Alan Sugarman  

Alan Sugarman introduced the panel as being concerned with the fact that attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is being understood by too many professionals and parents around the world as a neurologically or biochemically based disorder for which stimulant medication is the treatment of choice. The three panelists are part of a cadre of child clinicians challenging this disturbing trend and the assumptions that underlie it. All three have found that early trauma contributes to the symptoms for which children are diagnosed with ADHD. The prominence of early trauma in these children should not be surprising; after all, ADHD is just a set of behavioral, emotional, and cognitive symptoms that indicate an underlying disturbance in the synthetic, organizing, and integrative functions of the ego (Gilmore, 2000). Sugarman emphasized that such children have serious problems with self-regulation because affect regulation, narcissistic regulation, and stability of self- and object-representations are problematic. Consequently, their minds have difficulty balancing and maintaining a homeostatic equilibrium between the many mental processes and contents necessary for adequate self-regulation. Some ADHD children are born with constitutional limitations in these regulatory functions. But even these children develop unconscious fantasies to account for their functional difficulties, fantasies that must be analyzed. Other ADHD children have had these regulatory functions disrupted by early trauma, leading to internal conflict and inhibition of the functions in question.

Sugarman pointed out that the panelists suggest that the psychoanalytically informed treatment of these children be two pronged; they need both sensitive individual treatment to overcome the regulatory problems, and they need intervention in the traumatizing sociocultural milieu to prevent the trauma from repeating itself and undermining treatment gains. He concluded by suggesting that the individual treatment of these children should help them to remobilize and reintegrate their regulatory functions by helping them to consciously reflect on their minds’ workings and to see where, how, and why it works less well in certain areas. This approach shifts our analytic focus from the traditional emphasis on the dynamic, conflictual, mental content thought to be expressed through any particular impulsive action to exploring what makes it difficult to use verbal, symbolic channels to contend with these issues (Sugarman, 2003).

[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis As a Work in Progress11

Cláudio Eizirik

Cláudio Eizirik

Rua Marquês do Pombal 783/307, 90540-001 Porto Alegre RS, Brazil—ceizirik.ez@terra.com.br
(Final version accepted 6 January 2006)

Cláudio Eizirik  

In your service, an arm inured to battle;

In your praise, a mind given to the Muses.

Camões [White, 1997, p. 228]

With these words, which so aptly convey my deepest feelings for psychoanalysis, I accept the presidency of the International Psychoanalytical Association handed over to me by the honourable and worthy Prof. Daniel Widlöcher.

To be sharing this moment with my family and so many friends and colleagues reminds me, in a very personal way, of an expression employed by a character out of Erico Veríssimo's creative mind. Every time Liroca, a native of the Campanha Gaúcha (the Brazilian Pampas, situated at the extreme southwest of Rio Grande), stood face to face with new, surprising or somewhat unbelievable circumstances, he cried out: It is such a wide old world without gates!

This is a significant moment for our discipline and the IPA. We are gathered here in Brazil for the first time ever (the third time in Latin America), and the evidence that the ongoing Congress has been one of the most successful of recent years, in terms of scientific level, attendance numbers and organization, is cause for rejoicing.

Psychoanalysis is going through a crisis, we are told. Nonetheless, we witness a growing interest in our theories, a lively blossoming of new developments and their increasing application in the fields of mental health and human sciences. Statistics tell us that the number of analyses in progress has decreased, as has that of candidates at the institutes, and yet, in our congresses as well as in our publications, we find accounts of technically and methodologically thorough analysis and discussion of clinical material. The news about a so-called crisis in psychoanalysis contrasts strongly with our direct experience of dynamic reality, the construction of which is a task for all of us, each in his or her own practice.

The controversy between Robert Wallerstein (2005a, 2005b) and André Green (2005), two of our greatest contemporary thinkers, is a recent example of the high level reached by internal debate. In order to grasp its full signifi cance, one must bear in mind that each of these authors represents a school, an approach to psycho-analysis, in a culturally and historically distinctive part of the world. Wallerstein argues for the existence of a common ground in clinical psychoanalytical theory, and believes that psychoanalytical pluralism, that is, the existence of a variety of theorizations or metapsychologies, may not be a lasting trend.

responseSet.title: The Impact of Words on Children with ADHD and DAMP: Consequences for Psychoanalytic Technique
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Impact of Words on Children with ADHD and DAMP

Consequences for Psychoanalytic Technique

Björn Salomonsson

Björn Salomonsson

SE-171 76 Stockholm, Sweden—bjorn.salomonsson@ki.se
(Final version accepted 16 September 2005)

Björn Salomonsson  

attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder, ADHD, disorder of attention, motility control and perception, DAMP, psychoanalytic treatment of infants and mothers

Children with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and disorder of attention, motility control, and perception (DAMP) are often sensitive to the analyst's interventions. This is not always due to the literal import of the intervention. The children sometimes react as if the words were dangerous concrete objects, which they must physically fend off. The author traces this phenomenon to the child's unstable internal situation. A bad, un-containing internal object is easily awakened and threatens to expel the analyst's words independently of their content. This results in violent clinical situations. Infant research and psychoanalytic work with infants and mothers evince how a complex semiotic process develops between mother and baby. The prerequisite for this process to get started and maintained is a secure external object, which gradually is internalized. Findings from developmental research and clinical infant work are used to illuminate analytic work with children with ADHD and DAMP. Vignettes demonstrate how important it is for the analyst to phrase interpretations after having gauged the state of the analysand's internal object as well as his/her own countertransference. If this is overlooked, the psychoanalytic dialogue easily capsizes. The author provides some technical recommendations on the psychoanalysis of these children. As part of the theoretical discussion he raises the general question of how the representations, which the baby forms in interaction with the mother, and the analysand forms in interaction with the analyst, should be classified. Rather than dividing them into bipartite thing- or word-presentations (Freud), the author suggests C. S. Peirce's tripartite semiotic classification in that the baby forms representations of icons, indices, and symbols.

responseSet.title: Reflections on the Setting in the Treatment of Adolescents
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reflections on the Setting in the Treatment of Adolescents11

Sara De Filc

Sara De Filc

Las Heras 3855 Piso 6, 1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina—dfilc@fibertel.com.ar
(Final version accepted 17 January 2005)

Sara De Filc  

adolescent, setting, frame, communication, internalization, transference, countertransference, projective identification, transgression, change

The author focuses on the significance of the setting for the development of the psychoanalytic process, especially in the case of adolescents who request analytic treatment. Her main goals are to specify: a) how the setting is configured with this type of patients; and b) to what extent it contributes to the creation of an inner space that may internalize a figure with reverie—a good object that will metabolize the bad and thus enable identification. The setting, which is considered the necessary context for analytic work, is defined as bearing two facets: that of the analyst, which must be constant and stable, and that of the adolescent, which will progressively change provided that the analyst maintains a firm context that contributes to make the adolescent feel contained and accepted. It is such a feeling that will enable the unfolding of the analytic process. The author emphasizes the importance of the presence of the analyst (his or her voice, the manner of his or her speech, and so on), and the need for the analyst to comply with the rules he or she has established together with the patient. She presents a clinical case to illustrate this conceptualization.
